body:
🍿 IMDb.com | Full-Stack/Front-End Engineer | Seattle or Remote (US) | Full-time |  $100-$150k base (level-based) + generous $AMZN equity | [Link](https://www.amazon.jobs/en/jobs/1261725/front-end-engineer-imdb?no_int_redir=1)

> IMDb is embarking on a bold re-imagining of the consumer experience built on GraphQL, NextJS and React. We are building an entirely new distributed architecture to support iOS, Android and Web clients. We are seeking an experienced frontend engineer for the web engineering team who will work with technology, product and design leaders to deliver performant and delightful experiences that help entertainment fans explore the world of movies and shows and decide what to watch next.

> IMDb is a lot like working for a smaller company with all of the big company benefits. We know each other, we like each other, we share a passion for movies and TV. We believe in hands on career development, setting clear goals and expectations, and being transparent with opportunities to grow your career.

Hiring front-end-oriented folks to help my team migrate IMDb.com to Next.js. We're rebuilding it all, one page at a time, on Next and GraphQL, redesigning and reimagining along the way. Interested and want to chat? Drop me an email at matfrey@imdb.com and we can set up a referral.

The above link is for [front-end devs](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1264EHpy8AKf_tar7wsFDq_hlHP9WrdTdF7A9qUbPAVg/edit?usp=sharing), but we're looking for [backend](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1y09qDXdosWNrI8cYoOYE9V5FP6s5g3LHC-qkE99jMZs/edit) / fullstack and [machine learning engineers](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1QYJKwhTrlb5hMb_crKffPpQtiyr_plAr7aVvYhAMhqg/edit) as well (with different base pay for those positions depending on experience). [Note: The [current product](https://burnerpage.com/) does not yet advertise this purpose - it was just a manual prototype we launched that hit [#1 on ProductHunt](https://www.producthunt.com/posts/burner-page), but the stealth AI based product is the real focus that we're hiring for].

body:
Mawla | React Engineer | EU (remote possible) | Contract

Mawla are looking to grow our pool of frontend freelancers. We are always busy and having a network of strong freelancers means we know who to turn to when the next project comes up.
We have a boilerplate engine we use for all our next generation websites built on Tailwind, Typescript, Nextjs, Sanity.
A lot of the sanity and next work is done already by using the boilerplates so we're mostly looking for people with strong react, tailwind, typescript exp.

Rates, communication quality, timezones and availability are important to us so please DM me following this format:
-  Rates in $
-  English fluency - Yes/no
-  Timezone - we can't work with India and similar timezones right now.
-  Availability - full, part time, X hrs per week

We've three potential projects right now:

-  One react web app wizard for creating quotes
-  One SaaS company marketing website
-  One agency marketing website

We've met some of our favourite people in this slack so I'm enthusiastic we'll meet more great people.
PS our site is under construction newer one here - https://mawla-marketing.vercel.app/mawla-engine

projects@mawla.ie

body:
Genie AI | React Developer | London OR fully remote (UK) | Full-time | [https://apply.workable.com/genie-ai/j/8166DCED3C/](url)

Genie AI is a deep learning based software company with a mission to open source the law.

We are disrupting the legal industry by changing the business model ground up.

What Github did with open source code, Instagram and TikTok with entertainment, Airbnb with hospitality, and Uber with travel, Genie is doing with Legal contracts.

We are building a community-based AI law platform.



Requirements
About the role
We're looking for an experienced Senior React developer who has a passion for building an elegant & modern interface to solve complex real-world problems.

You are a product-focused, design thinking and curious developer who wants to turn user needs into simple yet clever solutions
You are excited to develop a community-based platform web application in React
You are interested in creating a rich, beautiful and elegant user interface to perfection
You enjoy and find it fun to build re-usable components
You are keen to protect your code with solid tests ranging from unit to automation
You care to collaborate and participate in solution design and performing experiments with the team
You are not afraid to take risks and learn advanced topics
You are eager for friendly, coherent spoken and written communication to exchange complex ideas
You build coding standards and peer review code to ensure code is neat and clean
What we need
Experience in building SaaS web-based products using;

Solid knowledge of React or NextJS framework
Typescript and Javascript
Proficient in HTML, CSS
What we would like
GraphQL
Knowledge of Design Patterns in Web development
Experience with test frameworks for unit, integration, and automation testing

What would be nice

Working with real-time collaboration applications & browser based editors
Experience with server-side rendering, web workers & service workers
Built apps with Micro-services architecture
Solid experience in responsive UI development

About our stack
Micro-services architecture, React, Next.js, Angular 12, Playwright, NodeJS, RabbitMQ, GraphQL, Redis, MongoDB, Python, AWS, Docker, Kubernetes, TensorFlow, PyTorch, ProseMirror. You're not expected to know all of this, but you will get the opportunity to learn based on your interest!


Benefits

What do Genies get?
Substantial equipment budget for you to set up a home working environment, including a top of the range laptop, monitor, wireless keyboard, mouse, and office chair
Generous stock options - we want everyone to share in our success!
Unlimited budget for business, law, or technology books
Unlimited holiday
Fully flexible working
Cycle to work scheme
Regular meditation and wellbeing sessions
Employee benefits platform where you can buy plants, food deliveries, yoga, wellbeing sessions, and more on us!
Optional pre-tax charity giving
Regular team lunches and dinners after lockdown (and plenty of social activity in the meantime!)
The opportunity to join a bright, fast-growing startup at a grassroots level and genuinely make a difference

author: simontong

body:
I don't believe it is an actual bug, but more of a feature request, since you are describing some functionality that just does not exist as of today. (Unless we document it otherwise. Can you confirm?).

From the source code, it looks like we only look for changes in the `pages` directory in dev mode: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/a5bfc1eecc34e176f9f27955fde7b16e17de3c5e/packages/next/server/dev/next-dev-server.ts#L254

If you actually mean adding files when running in production (eg `next start`), that's expected behavior. See the docs:

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving

>  Only assets that are in the `public` directory at [build time](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/cli#build) will be served by Next.js. Files added at runtime won't be available. We recommend using a third-party service like [AWS S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) for persistent file storage.
author: balazsorban44

body:
The problem is that I place a file in `./public/uploads/myimage.jpg` while the production server is running and when I access it via `https://website.com/_next/image?url=/uploads/myimage.jpg` it says "The requested resource isn't a valid image.". 

If I restart the server (in production) then and refresh the same URL then the image appears as expected.

Basically the way it is right now I would need to restart the server every time someone uploads an image in order to display said image using the <Image/> nextjs component

body:
Tailwind doesn't involve your site after build time. It compiles the optimized css and it ends there.

SSR is slowing down your site. It makes requests to CMS/API to get the page content on every request.

1. You could opt for [Automatic Static Optimization](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization). If contents update after build, you could opt for [Incremental Static Generation](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching).
2. Your homepage props contains unused data: `termsOfUse` and `privacyPolicy`.

body:
it depends, the `schema` or any `custom folder` that is excluded from the `pages`/`app` directory won't be directly included in the client side bundle (exposed to the public). 
If the only import is in an `api route ` the schema content won't be exposed, however if a component in a page directory (example pages/home) imports the schema file then the content will be exposed.

body:
Hey folks, wanted to swing back here with an update. After digging through many different issues and discussions, I've [**made a new page in the documentation**](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy) ([PR](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/54601)) specifically for Content Security Policy and nonces. This docs page: 

- Explains how to generate a `nonce` with Middleware
- Shows how to consume the `nonce` in a route with `headers()`
- Shows a complete CSP without needing to use any `unsafe`
- Shows how to ignore the `nonce` Middleware from running on prefetches / static assets

Further, we've patched some bugs and made improvements to `nonce` handling in Next.js that will be available in the latest `canary` version (for those of you time traveling from the future, upgrade to Next.js 13.5). We also updated the `with-strict-csp` example in the `examples/` folder, which is backlinked from the [new documentation page](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy).

Really hope this helps out, thank you all 🙏 I'll be closing this discussion out. To continue the discussion, [please go here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/54907).

Using this very simple code:

```js
// pages/_app.jx
import Head from "next/head"

export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    <>
      <Head>
        <title key="title">TITLE</title>
        <meta key="description" name="description" content="DESCRIPTION" />
      </Head>
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </>
  )
}
```

And running `next build && next export` produces an HTML containing the correct elements in `head`:

```js
// out/index.html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>

<head>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width" />
  <meta charSet="utf-8" />
  <title>TITLE</title>
  <meta name="description" content="DESCRIPTION" />
// ...
```

body:
+1, also seeing this behaviour. The documentation [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import) is underwhelming.

My guess is that with `{ ssr: true }`, your getting a smaller chunk for loading in parallel, but it behaves as if it's in the main code path.

`{ ssr: false }` is the behaviour that I'd expect from `next/dynamic` where chunks are requested as they're inserted into the dom
author: zyqxd

body:
I think there's a fundamental misunderstanding of what's going on here.

First of all, dynamic (from next) and lazy (from React), under the hood are doing link prefetch and preload.

Here's a verbose break down of these two, from Webpack's perspective: https://webpack.js.org/guides/code-splitting/#prefetchingpreloading-modules.

What you are describing is the ability, to lazy load these scripts, when the viewport is nearby, like it's done with images for example. That's out of the responsibility boundary of dynamic imports. 

The idea of the lazy loading technique is to break apart a large bundle, into chunks that are loaded as needed by the document. The fact that the main bundle is broken into chunks, means less joint time is spent parsing and rendering the first page, and gives room for interactivity. When the chunks are loaded these are again parsed and rendering into the browser is done, at a later time.

Chunking is not as simple as breaking into dynamic modules, sometimes it can hurt more than it helps. Dynamic modules have overhead, and in total, more time is spend parsing a main bundle with a bunch of chunks, compared to a single monolith bundle, the advantage is that there's breathing room in between the main bundle and chunk loading (this is a fair assumption).

You can do this by implementing two event listeners for scroll and resize, and calculate whether or not a placeholder is on the viewport as the events fire, as soon as you come near the viewport, let react know to render the dynamic/lazy component, only then the chunk would be required. The https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Intersection_Observer_API, also helps out on this regard.
author: icyJoseph
icyJoseph

body:
Because Next.js is doing source code transformation during build-time you really have to be careful about how you write and structure the code.

My guess is that when a dynamic component appears directly (unconditionally) in the JSX the bundle is loaded immediately irrespective of the runtime logic.

I've made an example of getting the code structure right so it lazy-loads properly: https://github.com/Fractal-Web/next-ssr-hydration

Hi there @cdaringe! I spoke with the team about this a bit and unfortunately its not something we are able to prioritize internally, though as always we would, of course, welcome contributions if anyone is interested.

This would be very difficult to accomplish and have many edge cases, and we are currently heavily invested in a couple of projects that we think will solve the same root problem in a more efficient manner -- namely, some improvements to routing within next.js (we are planning to put out an RFC with more detail here in the next few weeks), and react server component support.

Sorry I didn't have a more positive answer for you here and I hope that over the next few months we can bring you some new features that will greatly alleviate this pain for you all 😀
author: jescalan

body:
Hi @jescalan,

Thanks for writing.

Yes, we'd be interested in working on it. If someone could help enumerate the cases in play and confirm that such patches would be welcomed, we could budget time to work on this. We haven't seen any technical discussion on this matter yet, other than "use getServerSideProps" in an email, which does not consider or address our usage of getInitialProps discussed above. We think a config option could allow opt-in participation in next's pre-existing DCE & tree shaking, along with what is already done for the other data fetching methods.

I also recall that internally, next.js only applies optimizations to source in the `pages/*` dir, but our pages actually import page components from something akin to `@our-scope/my-page`, then re-export the components from library space. We do this because as we host multiple applications that _re-use_ page components. Thus, some consideration/support on getting tree-shaking to work more flexibly than just on source files matching `pages/*` would be helpful as well. This topic applies even if we were using `getServerSideProps` vs `getInitialProps`.

Thx for the consideration
author: cdaringe

body: I've also wanted this feature! It'd be great if I could make a request to e.g. `/slug/_revalidate` or `/_next/revalidate/slug` when the data changes (e.g. in a CMS or database), then not have the site need to run the `revalidate` every x seconds. The API could look like `return { props: {}, revalidate: 'manual' }` or something. This would save at least tens of thousands of useless Vercel serverless function invocations per month across my projects, which right now are wasting electricity in order to be update-able quickly when data does change.

author: ahmedaboutlaeb

body:
@Darkmax512 What you and @lachlanjc probably want to look into is something like the SWR library (also worked on by the vercel/nextjs team). It has a lot more fine tuned options with data fetching and revalidation. It used the stale-while-revalidate method to cache data and reduces the amount of unnecessary requests that your app might be making when the data has not changed. You can also mutate the data in the cache without revalidating if you know what the updated data is going to look like already, which even further reduces the amount of calls to your api

SWR is also great because you can still statically generate your whole app and use it.

body:
I can confirm that this works, even if you have more complex logic (server components, for example). 

It might be useful to point out that I moved the transpiling part to NextJS, so it can be useful for local development:

next.config.js
```js
  transpilePackages: ['the_library']
```
Also, if you want to make this work with Docker, you would do it via volumes, such as:

docker-compose.yml
```yml
    volumes:
      - ../the_library:/var/www/main_project/the_library
```

package.json

```json
    "the_library": "file:the_library"
```

body:
This sounds like a use case for [Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching#incremental-static-regeneration)

Essentially, you wouldn't build the static pages at build-time, but rather run-time. The first visiting the page would get an initial loading screen, but all subsequent users will receive the static page instantly. The pages will keep regenerating depending on the `revalidate` value in `getStaticProps`

My guess would be you were previously inlining svgs from a location not covered under `options.dir`.

For example, if you have a component library that includes svgs that you wish to inline, (i.e. a path you were adding to `config.resolve.modules` and/or `config.resolve.alias`) then you should also add that to the `include` parameter of the rule.

The path after the `error` log should indicate whether this is the case.

author: cupofjoakim

body:
In my case, the issue was caused by Next.js lazily compiling routes during development. The first time an API route was requested, it triggered a regeneration of the central _buildManifest.js. In a large application, this file can become so large that webpack cannot apply the update incrementally on the client. As a result, Fast Refresh falls back to performing a full page reload.

Switching to Turbopack resolved the issue for me, as it can handle these updates properly.

body:
I can update on our end too. Our issue was also the lazy compilation of the routes during development. For us a turbo migration is not in the cards (yet) due to some odd webpack dependencies. 

We worked around the issue by adding a call to the api endpoints on server startup to trigger the compilation early. Not great, but we do it only in dev and it gets us there.

> _Note: This proposal is not suggesting changing the output to ESM, but rather using that feature as a way to determine which browsers we consider “modern”._

Does this mean that some `*.js` files in `.next` will remain using CommonJS? Asking because of this issue: 

- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/24334

I have a project with a bunch of ESM scripts, for which I added `"type": "module"` in `package.json`. This broke Next.js commands, so I had to add another `package.json` into `.next` dir [as a workaround](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/32220#discussioncomment-1766378).

If Next outputs are for modern browsers, it’d be really cool if it was ESM everywhere! Altenratively, if files in `.next` have explicit `.cjs` and `.mjs` extensions, an app should work regardless of `"type": "module"` in `package.json`.

author: kylemh

body:
1. I'd advocate for 2 bundles if the legacy build is enabled, Meteor-style, the use case being support for older browsers while not affecting modern browser with a less optimized build.

What is a modern browser is something that will evolve with time, so this pattern is probably here to stay. For many companies and products supporting legacy browsers will always be important, yet they don't want to reduce the quality of the main build.

The build time is really not a problem here imo, if you want a legacy browsers you have good business reason for it and you'd accept a small overhead when releasing your app in prod.
You can opt-out of the legacy build during dev, test or local build anyway.

2. About browserlist, one thing I don't like is that you cannot really build it based on your app dependencies. You'd probably want this option as well though, so you can control the build better. However, what happens if I want to include non-modern browser to the list? You still end up with the need to produce 2 bundles.

author: Brooooooklyn

body:
I'd like to kindly remind folks as we work towards a solution here that while the majority of individuals are using modern browsers, the market for Internet Explorer 11 (and below) is still large even though it is reaching end of life soon. For example, the company I work for gets about 20% of annual revenue through IE 11. This is primarily through B2B integrations with older SAP instances. Many IT departments are very slow to upgrade, which puts our end customers in a position where they do not have a choice of what browser to use, so asking the end customer to upgrade to a modern browser is sometimes not possible. We have more users on IE 11 than on modern Firefox or Safari.

We recently made the decision to use Next.js, partially due to its built in support for IE 11, so I ask that we keep this large customer base in mind.

---

That being said, is there an option to have 2 builds, modern and legacy, and then do user agent sniffing on the server to serve the appropriate bundle? It would be nice to relegate the ES5 transpiling and polyfilling to the legacy browser bundle only. The tradeoff of a longer build time is fine if I'm giving my customers a better experience by serving a leaner bundle to modern browsers.

I'm curious if Next.js could also handle modern and legacy CSS stylesheet bundles? AFAIK Differential Serving using `module`/`nomodule` is specific to `<script>` tags. Can we build a modern and legacy CSS bundle also and serve it to the appropriate browser?
author: ryanoglesby08

body:
I like the idea of adding opt-in support and configuration for v12 using built-in browserlist support in package.json. It seems to require the least amount of effort. It also feels better in terms of feature alignment since if I follow correctly there's currently no way to accomplish this with SWC. As far as I can tell, if someone would like to [do this in a project today](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/12826), it would only be possible if they fall back to using Babel which has other obvious performance drawbacks and is (relatively) more painful to configure. 

Adding support in this way cleanly tees up the wider rollout for this feature to be as simple as a configuration flip. I don't know when v13 is supposed to be released, but this way if enough users express a desire to push this change back (for example until after IEs EoL) their concerns can be addressed appropriately without preventing other users from experiencing the benefits of a lighter bundle with the fastest compiler today.
author: hu0p

body:
I’m sympathetic to the continued need to support IE but I don’t think that it should be so influential as to bend the implementation for the vast majority of end-users of Next.is apps.
That said I myself would prefer a solution incorporating browserslist in order to give the support to the “standard” (for want of a better word) tooling to this end. I think a decent default is fine, but I’m not sure that a toggle is necessarily of great value if browserslist is supported: and I do really think it should be. Being able to switch between two defaults with a flag could be handy, but I don’t think it’s enough to then not support browserslist.

body:
I've implemented the JS part of this RFC and the experimental flags to try it out are landed in 12.2:
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12-2

> We've added experimental support for shipping only modern JavaScript by modifying browsersList. You can opt-into this behavior by setting browsersListForSwc: true and legacyBrowsers: false in the experimental option of next.config.js.

- `browsersListForSwc: true` enables browserslist support for JS
- `legacyBrowsers: false` enables the future default that targets the browsers mentioned in the RFC. This flag depends on `browsersListForSwc` so in order to try it out you'll want to use this config:

```
experimental: {
   browsersListForSwc: true,
   legacyBrowsers: false
}
```
author: timneutkens

body: FWIW I just tried out setting `browsersListForSwc` in https://github.com/replayio/devtools/pull/7291 , along with a custom `browserslist` value to match what we previously were doing with `@babel/preset-env`.  I confirmed that with SWC instead of Babel and default SWC behavior in Next 12.1, our bundle sizes went _up_ by about 40K, and with SWC + Next 12.2 + the custom `browserslist` behavior those same bundle sizes went _down_ by about 8K. So, this appears to be working as desired!

body: This one helped me but not sure if this is your case: If you're using `useRouter` anywhere on that page watch out for the following: 👉 In dynamic routes the pathname value in `useRouter` returns the file name instead of the pathname. I did some checking with `query.slug` in such cases and it resolved my issue: ```js const { pathname , query } = useRouter() // pathname is /artist/release/[releaseId] in your case // my solution: // relativePath will be artist/release/6480b71f-e8d4-4e9c-b672-c47690b69662 const relativePath = typeof query.slug === 'string' ? pathname.replace('[slug]', query.slug) : pathname ``` The issue was in my navbar where I had internalization control with a dropdown that displays either english or turkish version of the website. Based on the choice the locale value changes from `en` to `tur` and the pathname follows `/tur/[pathname]`... The issue, of course, was that I ended up with the following anchor tag ```js tur ``` which should have been ```js tur ```

author: iiroj

body:
Thanks for chiming in! I wrote a lambda handler 6 months ago that gives the user the option to:

* Run renderer function manually (no server, no `node_modules`)
* Run renderer function by starting a Node server (server, no `node_modules`)
* Run a NextJS server (server, `node_modules`)
* Run a Node server with a NextJS handler (server, `node_modules`)

It's currently only using NextJS as target framework but it's built in a modular fashion so it's easy to add other frameworks as well.

Obviously, the 2 last render strategies take more time (like 1-2 orders of magnitude) and requires a lot more space due to `node_modules` folder. The last two strategies still work with NextJS 12, but as I wrote above, it's a step in the wrong direction. It's not that it's infeasible to start a server and render the response from it but it's kind inefficient when it could OBVIOUSLY be done in a better way with NextJS 11.

I just started this thread to see if anyone from the NextJS team could inform us users about what they were thinking; either there is some huge misunderstanding and it can be done in a simpler way than outlined above, or it would be interesting to see what they had in mind. 

Also, it doesn't matter if we use `experimental.standalone` or not, we seem to still be forced to wind up a server and send an artificial request to it, `experimental.standalone` just takes care of some file organization for us.

It's just kind of absurd isn't it... given a cloud provider we now have:

1. HTTP request to Cloud provider
2. Lambda event from cloud provider to lambda
3. Start up server in Lambda
4. Convert lambda event BACK into HTTP request
5. Await HTTP response from temporary server
6. Convert HTTP response to lambda response event
7. Cloud provider turns lambda response in HTTP response

It's not an architectural dream if I may say so and with NextJS 11, step 3-6 at least did not have to involve starting a server and converting the lambda event into a full-fledged HTTP request (even though converting it into an `IncomingMessage` is perhaps not so much simpler after all).
author: fast-reflexes

body:
I'm currently not working on it, sticking with NextJS 11 ... I mean, if you're ok with storing a `node_modules` folder in your lambda and firing up a temporary Node server, NextJS 12 works fine for serverless but that's such a suboptimal pattern compared to how it can be done in NextJS 11 with just a render function and an `IncomingMessage` and `ServerResponse` (no `node_modules` no temporary server). I'm just curious what the development team had in mind for this functionality.

Anyways, I don't see where a page is actually rendered anywhere in the commit you pointed to, I guess that's the interesting part... whether that can be solved in a non-hacky way without all these extra stuff or not.

Will post in this thread if I find something interesting :)

author: jinqian1998

body:
The problem is not that we can't run NextJS 12 in a Lambda, the problem is that NextJS 12 makes it more cumbersome and less Lambda friendly.

I have a NextJS lambda handler that I wrote some time ago and in that project, I tried to explore all the ways you could generate a NextJS response from a Lambda event. The following were the methods I tried in that project:

* Generating a response right from the method handler (e.g. converting the incoming Lambda event to an `IncomingMessage` and call the handler with the resulting `IncomingMessage` and a `ServerResponse` object. In NextJS 11 this requires ONLY the handler method and a `chunks` folder with some extra files. Back in the days, I thought it was wrong that these `chunks` files were needed since NextJS docs claimed that ONLY the generated handler file / lambda would be needed. I filed a complaint about this [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/26297).
* Running a basic Node server which calls the handler method and returns the response. This requires converting the Lambda event to a `RequestOptions` object instead which in turn can be used to create a `ClientRequest` from the `http.request` method in Node. This method is quite good as well since only the same files that we needed in the last strategy are necessary. 
* Running a NextJS server (like we do when we have built a project and then run our project locally with `npm run start`). This is much less efficient since we now need `node_modules` with a `nextjs` installation and so on. This method also requires us to transform the Lambda event to a `RequestOptions` object.
* Finally, we may also run a standalone server with a NextJS request handler (like we do when we want to use a custom server in NextJS). This method is essentially no different than the above since we still need `node_modules` with a `nextjs` installation for the request handler.

So, what NextJS 12 essentially does is that it removes the possibility to use either of the first two methods and now we BOTH have to start a server AND have a `nextjs` installation in `node_modules`. Nonetheless, it is still doable;

* Convert Lambda event to `RequestOptions` object and body string.
* Start a NextJS server.
* Run a `http.request` with the `RequestOptions` from first step, a possible request body and a callback function against your local NextJS server (updating some values in the request as necessary for the request to work out).
* Handle the response in the callback function and then closing your local server.
* Convert the response to an output Lambda event.

Sounds straightforward, and even though it's doable, there are a lot of small things that can go wrong :)

Seems the previous posts are about using some outside layer to turn the Lambda event into a `ClientRequest`? If so you could do it yourself as well if you spend some time on it but it seems good if there is an existing solution for it.. nonetheless, I only reach 404 when I click the link :D

author: AriaFantom

body:
I find TypeScript to be overly verbose, and as [this article](https://medium.com/javascript-scene/the-typescript-tax-132ff4cb175b) puts it: taxing. Before developing in JavaScript I worked in Java, I since moving I've come to appreciate vanilla JavaScript for being **simple and expressive**. Adding TypeScript into the mix can make it harder to read (and therefore easier for bugs to be hiding). If your code is not legible, adding TypeScript is a band-aid solution. The majority of bugs in my experience are not type-related, but issues related to bad behavior (even if all the types are perfectly correct!). TypeScript does not fix these bugs.

I understand TypeScript may be necessary in **some** use cases, but in my opinion unless you have a specific need for it it's going to increase your development time for very limited rewards. With Next.js being a "batteries-included" framework one of it's specific strengths is how fast development can be. While I understand that means Next.js has opinions, I believe making TypeScript the default moves away from ease of use and towards type safety which may be overkill. 

In Robert C. Martin's book "Clean Code" he describes comments in this way: 

_"Nothing can be quite so helpful as a well-placed comment. Nothing can clutter up a module more than frivolous dogmatic comments. Nothing can be quite so damaging as an old crufty comment that propagates lies and misinformation._

_Comments are not like Schindler’s List. They are not “pure good.” Indeed, comments are, at best, a necessary evil. If our programming languages were expressive enough, or if we had the talent to subtly wield those languages to express our intent, we would not need comments very much—perhaps not at all._


_The proper use of comments is to compensate for our failure to express ourself in
code. Note that I used the word failure. I meant it. Comments are always failures. We must
have them because we cannot always figure out how to express ourselves without them,
but their use is not a cause for celebration."_

I believe that static typing can largely be viewed the same way. To me having to explicit describe each and every type is indicative that our code is simply **not self-explanatory enough**. I can see the benefits of publishing strict typings in a very popular library (or even internally inside of Next.js) for the sake of absolute safety beyond a shadow of a doubt or convenient suggestions the user's IDE, but when we are talking about a small team of developers trying to deploy their website quickly, or just anyone trying to get started with Next.js quickly, having the example code interspersed with (hopefully) redundant typings means more time wasted trying to comprehend it. Having examples preloaded with TypeScript means we're pushing this type system onto the developer.

Furthermore, the time spent reading through and maintaining type definitions could be better spent on more-effective safety measures like TDD, or simply more time spent developing the application. Though I know this isn't a 1-1 comparison, if I was writing a book I wouldn't want to waste my time _verb_ prefixing _determiner_ my _noun_ words.

Edit: Another option would be to separate the JS code and the TypeScript definition themselves
author: L1lith

body: Could not have put it better, I completely agree 🙏 especially concerning the library part. Obviously using Typescript in developing Next.js the framework has lot of benefits, but to put that tax on users of the framework is heavy-handed.
author: ghost

body:
> Reference: [#33245 (comment)](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33245#issuecomment-1012094076)
> 
> Personally, I'm _not_ in favor of converting examples to Typescript. Unless there are some extraordinary benefits to new Next.js projects (which I wager often start as prototytpes), speed of development is the most important metric and Typescript definitely slows this down.
> 
> In addition, Next.js was created with the philosophy of _incredmental adoptability_ – including Typescript goes against this philosophy. The framework's name is also `Next.js`, not `Next.ts`. I am in favor of the existing pattern of having Typescript examples alongisde the JavaScript ones (e.g. [blog-starter-typescript](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/blog-starter-typescript)).
> 
> What does everyone else think?

I fully agree with you. I was following a tutorial that used the example "with-tailwindcss" and created an app with normal, vanilla js. Then, for my surprise, the one I got was fully configured with typescript. I don't like typescript and rather do my things in js itself. So I'm losing a big time trying to figure it out how to get back to the previous behavior and create my app with vanilla js.

The examples shouldn't be so opinionated. It should be an user choice. If one want something in ts, then it should be a ts example for it.
It is kind of dictatorial move to force people something. Freedom is a matter of individual choice, not someone else deciding for you what they think is better for you.

Can someone help me how to create an app with the previous behavior? If I fork the previous way and point the create-next-app to the forked repo would it work? I'm completely new on this. Thanks everyone.

p.s.: This doesn't mean the maintainers shouldn't enforce ts usage. They should if they want. But it shouldn't change well known behaviors like that. 

p.s.2: By the way, for tailwindcss, there is an official way to install it together with Next.js in the link below, if somebody needs it: 
https://tailwindcss.com/docs/guides/nextjs
author: danielfoxp2

body:
I think adding examples in Typescript aligns 100% with the Next.js philosophy. 

Next.js calls itself 'The React Framework for Production', If it were to focus it's efforts on making developers feel comfortable with getting to boilerplate code than it should drop the production part of that tagline. 
But that's not the goal, the goal is to make your app (code) production ready. Typescript makes this easier, although admittedly with some initial hurdles to jump over. But these are hurdles you will have to deal with either before you ship or after. I prefer to do so before.
author: Dueen

body:
> I think adding examples in Typescript aligns 100% with the Next.js philosophy.
> 
> Next.js calls itself 'The React Framework for Production', If it were to focus it's efforts on making developers feel comfortable with getting to boilerplate code than it should drop the production part of that tagline. But that's not the goal, the goal is to make your app (code) production ready. Typescript makes this easier, although admittedly with some initial hurdles to jump over. But these are hurdles you will have to deal with either before you ship or after. I prefer to do so before.

Are you implying that vanilla js it is not good or ready for production? I can't agree with your statement. In my case, the ts made it harder for me to be ready for production. It is slowing me down a lot, right now. Vanilla js is also 100% aligned with Next.js philosophy. 

And it is all fine that you have your preferences and you should have a "with-tailwindcss-ts" to help you achieve your goals the way you like. But who prefer to use vanilla js, the well known "with-tailwindcss" should still work as expected. Typescript is not for everyone and it shouldn't be assumed as so.
author: danielfoxp2

body:
Of course vanilla JS is ready for production. Typescript compiles to vanilla JS at the end of the day. 
My argument is on the benefits vs the pitfalls of Typescript. If you're shipping production code, having that extra benefit of type safety at compile time is a net benefit to the extra coding time you have to do to make Typescript happy. So ultimately you can focus on the complicated bugs that Typescript can't catch, and less on the ones that Typescript could've gotten for you.
author: Dueen

author: JesperHoff

body:
@balazsorban44 As you can see in my logs, there's no difference between cases for my directory (`c:\wwwroot\nextjs-blog` actual path `C:\wwwroot\nextjs-blog` - if you don't mean drive letter must also be in the casing which is a weird thing and not in my control)

@hajdun I found a workaround for now. 
1. I created the app using `npx create-next-app` on Windows
2. Removed `node_modules` folder
3. Started `WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux)`
4. Ran `npm i` and then was able to start the project with `npm run dev`

Since WSL 1 uses the same network adapter as Windows, you can access the app via `localhost:3000`. Also you'd be able to edit files from your preferred IDE on Windows.

There is still an issue with Next.js not working on Windows with a blank project.

I'm on Windows 11 with Node.js 16.9.0.

I run this `npx create-next-app@latest --typescript`

````bash
cd test

```

```bash
warn  - Invalid casing detected for project dir, received c:\Work\test actual path C:\Work\test, see more info here https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/invalid-project-dir-casing
```

Then a wall of warnings like this:

```bash
warn  - ./node_modules/next/dist/lib/is-error.js

Use equal casing. Compare these module identifiers:

When you open the app in the browser you get the same sort of warnings in the browser console and a blank screen.

author: qhkm

body:
At the end, I was not required of passing the props from the page to the layout, but just to leverage that data to a ContextProvider within the layout itself (and doing the required modifications at the _app component for it to run the getLayout function inside the contexts I was in need of - that is, authentication providers, themes, etc.)

If you need to pass some static or serverSideProps from the page to the component, you could clone the page's layout (with reactjs functions) and pass the pageProps that your _app gets to it.

Remember that you can check the module exported (that is, your page component) name, and so you can modify the behavior of _app in specific cases.

body:
Yo, I was able to fix this issue. 
In my components and pages, I was importing `Fragment` from some unknown source, but somehow it was working fine locally.

I fixed it by doing the following in my components

Before
```javascript
import { Fragment } from "react/cjs/react.development"
```
After
```javascript
import { Fragment } from "react"
```

I believe these issues exist solely because of wrong imports and exports. Just make sure you are exporting and importing everything correctly in each of your pages & components.

author: Arotiana137-51

body:
Got the answer for this i was having this error too...
The function is trying to request from an API endpoint that hasn't been built yet. The API routes in this application are in the NextJS application which is getting built. To solve this issue take the logic from the getPageInfo, getExperience...etc and put it in the respective helper methods in the utils folder. Basically, make your request to sanity straight from the helper utils without the api routes. After I did this it deployed. 

So you will be fetching like this

const res = await sanityClient.fetch(query)

const experiences: Experience [ ] = result

return experiences 

The query is your groq query string.


If you want the github repo here is the link to my repo. :-  https://github.com/AnmolPandita-sudo/Portfolio
And the link to my build :-  https://anmolpandita.vercel.app/

author: MarkivV

body:
My problem was I set up Vercel the way it wouldn't using the `package-lock.json` to install the dependencies, so despite working in my machine, it won't build on Vercel. My project is a monorepo using [npm's workspaces](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v9/using-npm/workspaces?v=true), so there's a root-level `package.json` and a Next.js app's `package.json`:

```
.
├── www/     <- Next.js app
│   └── package.json    
├── package.json
└── package-lock.json
```

Before, I set `www` as the root directory on Vercel deployment, so it wouldn't touch the `package-lock.json`. I fixed it by setting the root directory back to my project's root. I also had to change the **Build & Development Settings** for it to work:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28887874/231233017-3b8faa68-f4c7-4d9f-8857-0804812ea580.png)

body: I think they're the same. The build process encountered `TypeError` and printed it, then Next.js caught the error and printed again I suppose. Your props could be undefined in some cases, therefore it couldn't read `email`. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/prerender-error
author: wonrax
ninsau

body:
Solved: At first i was fetching the data from a countries external api and passed **country** as props to the component, but the problem was that when i was trying to access let's say **country.name** it was undefined at the very first time so it caused the deployment fail while was working pretty good in development.. I had used the null safety before but it was useless and still was throwing error: `

{country?.name?.common}

` The solution was as simple as the following: `{country && (

{country.name.common}

)}`

body:
Pretty sure it's because next is accessing the window when it is not in the browser and therefore can't find it.

So if you just put all the references of window inside of a useEffect that should solve it.

author: akashish504

body:
Got the answer for this i was having this error too...
The function is trying to request from an API endpoint that hasn't been built yet. The API routes in this application are in the NextJS application which is getting built. To solve this issue take the logic from the getPageInfo, getExperience...etc and put it in the respective helper methods in the utils folder. Basically, make your request to sanity straight from the helper utils without the api routes. After I did this it deployed.

So you will be fetching like this

const res = await sanityClient.fetch(query)

const experiences: Experience [ ] = result

return experiences

The query is your groq query string.

If you want the github repo here is the link to my repo. :- https://github.com/AnmolPandita-sudo/Portfolio
And the link to my build :- https://anmolpandita.vercel.app/

body:
Got the answer for this i was having this error too...
The function is trying to request from an API endpoint that hasn't been built yet. The API routes in this application are in the NextJS application which is getting built. To solve this issue take the logic from the getPageInfo, getExperience...etc and put it in the respective helper methods in the utils folder. Basically, make your request to sanity straight from the helper utils without the api routes. After I did this it deployed.

So you will be fetching like this

const res = await sanityClient.fetch(query)

const experiences: Experience [ ] = result

return experiences

The query is your groq query string.

author: coltenkrauter

body:
Hello!

There is no particular documentation for `NextApiHandler`.
It is just a basic type that you can find here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/shared/lib/utils.ts#L301
author: thibautsabot

body:
I use custom `server.js`.

Passing `hostname` and `port` params helped me:

```
...
const isDev = process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'

const port = Number(process.env.PORT || 3030)
const app = next({ dev: isDev, hostname: 'localhost', port })
...
```
author: iksent

body: But there are no information [in the docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/middleware) about this...
author: iksent

body: @iksent you save my day.. I am struggled with this error `Error [TypeError]: Invalid URL: http://undefined:undefined/?__nextDefaultLocale=` while using custom server and middleware. And adding port and hostname solve the problems.

Since the `TreeView` is part of the page, navigating to a new one will reset its state.

Another way would be to use query params in the URL.

body:
This thread has been open 4 years with community interest but no maintainer verdict on whether a build-time warning approach is welcome. Rather than submit a cold PR and risk close-as-duplicate-discussion, leaving a concrete proposal here for feedback.

## Proposal

A build-time warn-once check in `getNextPublicEnvironmentVariables()` (`packages/next/src/lib/static-env.ts`) that fires `Log.warnOnce` when a `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` value matches one of a curated list of anchored high-confidence credential shapes. The prefix semantics (`NEXT_PUBLIC_` = intentionally public) are preserved — the warning never blocks build, never alters emitted code, and is opt-out-able via `NEXT_SUPPRESS_PUBLIC_ENV_SHAPE_WARNING=1`.

Design choices made to minimize false-positive churn (the #1 reason the framework team has historically paused on this class of change):

- **Anchored regex only**, no entropy heuristics. 16 patterns cover Tier-1 providers: Stripe `sk_(live|test)_`, AWS `AKIA`, GitHub `ghp_`/`gho_`/`github_pat_`/`ghs_`, OpenAI `sk-(proj|svcacct|admin)-` and legacy `T3BlbkFJ`, Anthropic `sk-ant-api03-`, Slack `xoxb-`/`xoxp-`, SendGrid, npm, PEM private-key headers, Supabase `service_role` JWT. Every regex is anchored (`^...$`) and requires a minimum tail length.

- **Public-by-design identifiers are explicitly excluded** from the warn list: Firebase `apiKey`, Stripe publishable key `pk_`, PostHog `phc_`, Supabase anon JWT, Google Maps `AIzaSy`, Algolia search key, Sentry DSN, Mapbox `pk.`. These all flow through `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` legitimately and would never trigger a false warning.

- **16-character minimum** avoids sentinels (`true`, `development`, `1`) ever matching.

- **`warnOnce`** means no noisy repeat during hot-reload.

- **No behavior change**: if a user intentionally ships a `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_TOKEN` that happens to look like a Stripe `sk_live_`, they get one warning and can either rename or set `NEXT_SUPPRESS_PUBLIC_ENV_SHAPE_WARNING=1`.

## What it looks like

```
warn  - Next.js detected a NEXT_PUBLIC_* env var whose value matches a known
        credential shape (Stripe secret key).

        NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_SECRET=sk_live_***

        NEXT_PUBLIC_ values are inlined into the browser bundle and shipped to
        every user. If this is intentional, set
        NEXT_SUPPRESS_PUBLIC_ENV_SHAPE_WARNING=1. Otherwise rename it to omit
        NEXT_PUBLIC_.
```

## Prior art

No framework in the ecosystem currently ships this class of warning — Vite, SvelteKit, CRA all inline their public-prefix env vars without value inspection. Next.js shipping it first would establish a reasonable default.

## Branch + patch

I have the three-file patch (`check-public-env-shape.ts` new, `static-env.ts` 2-line edit, 170-line unit test) ready. It applies cleanly against current `canary` HEAD and uses existing `Log.warnOnce` infrastructure.

Two questions before opening a PR:

1. Is the build-time warn-once approach acceptable in principle to the team? (I don't want to submit cold and duplicate this thread.)
2. If yes — preferred integration point: `getNextPublicEnvironmentVariables` directly, or factored into the `@next/eslint-plugin-next` as a new rule that the framework default-enables?

Happy to reshape either way. Will hold on PR until a maintainer weighs in.

(Context, for transparency: this proposal was sharpened after the April 2026 Vercel incident publicly highlighted how much friction exists between "`NEXT_PUBLIC_` was designed to be public" and "customers are misusing it as a shorthand for any env var." The framework warning is a zero-blast-radius hardening; no exploits are being withheld from a PR by keeping the proposal public here.)
author: Hybirdss

body:
The nature of a global opt-out feels too coarse to me. You add PAT for a burner account, disable the warning, and now you're back to square one when you accidentally add a new, sensitive secret later on. An opt-out per variable would make more sense but isn't that already what the `NEXT_PUBLIC` prefix does?

Keep in mind that it's not easy to include a sensitive secret in browser bundles. You actually have to prefix it with `NEXT_PUBLIC_`. E.g. `process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN` will **not** be filled in during build even if there is a `GITHUB_TOKEN` available. Only `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_GITHUB_TOKEN` would work if `NEXT_PUBLIC_GITHUB_TOKEN` is available during build time. See https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/guides/environment-variables#bundling-environment-variables-for-the-browser
author: eps1lon

body:
Thanks for the feedback — both points are well taken.

**On the global opt-out**: Agreed — dropped. I should also acknowledge the gap this creates: without any escape hatch, the only documented silence path is renaming the variable. If the team identifies a concrete use case that needs per-variable suppression, I think a `next.config.js` allowlist is the right shape (rather than another env var):

```js
// next.config.js
experimental: {
  suppressPublicEnvShapeWarnings: ['NEXT_PUBLIC_MY_KEY']
}
```

Happy to add that in a follow-up or in this PR if it's a blocker. For the current Tier-1 set specifically, I don't know of a case where `sk_live_`, `AKIA`, or `ghp_` is legitimately public — the patterns were chosen to exclude all public-by-design identifiers — but I could be missing something.

**On "isn't the prefix already the opt-in"**: That's a fair framing. The distinction I had in mind is that the prefix opts-in the variable *name*, while this checks the *value content* — but I recognize that might not be a distinction worth encoding in the framework. If the team's read is that this belongs in a linter or external scanner (gitleaks, trufflehog) instead, I think that's reasonable. Happy to close this PR and take it that direction.

Either way, appreciate you taking a look.
author: Hybirdss

body:
Follow-up: I've opened #93005 as a **draft** containing the 3-file reference implementation described above. The draft status is intentional — it's there as concrete code to look at during this thread's discussion, not a request for review.

Will flip from draft to ready-for-review only after a maintainer weighs in on the approach here. Happy to close the draft entirely if the team's direction differs.

body:
Thanks - I had assumed that `eslint-config-prettier` would raise prettier issues as eslint issues - but I can see from your link that's not what it does.

In case anyone finds this wanting to be able to get prettier integrated into eslint in this way, this is my `.eslintrc.json` file:

```
{
  "extends": ["next/core-web-vitals", "plugin:prettier/recommended"],
  "plugins": [
    "prettier"
  ],
  "rules": {
    "prettier/prettier": "error"
  }
}

```

package.json (without version numbers)

```
"eslint": "...",
"eslint-config-next": "...",
"eslint-config-prettier": "...",
"eslint-plugin-prettier": "...",
"prettier": "...",
```

body:
Because npm does not give us any way to mark "development" vs "production", so there's no differentiation between what should be needed in production vs development. 

If you want the Next.js app to run without node_modules and only the deps needed for the app you can use the new experimental flag `outputStandalone` that leverages the node-file-trace files to create a standalone Next.js app: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing#automatically-copying-traced-files-experimental.
author: timneutkens

body: Converted to discussions as the proposed behavior is not possible because of upstream ecosystem limitations (npm) and we have an experimental flag implemented for standalone outputs.

> `devDependencies` of a package you're installing are never installed

`npm install next` will not install the `next` package `devDependencies`. We are not able to say "install this in dev" "install this when `npm install --production`" because npm does not support a way to flag deps like that.

author: mostlylikeable

body:
Those swc helpers are needed. I just removed the @swc/core-<yourplatform-arch-etc> folder since that's the one that takes considerably more space than the rest of it.
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/36910d5b-380e-4198-a98d-514e4e01d81d)
I'm using the standalone output so I just added this to my next.config.js file (see [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/api-reference/config/next-config-js/output#caveats)):
```
  output: "standalone",
  outputFileTracingExcludes: {
    "./**/*": [
      "node_modules/@swc/core-*",
    ],
  },
```
and then the large swc binary will not end up in the standalone/node_modules folder anymore.

body:
For those confronting this issue that need a quick fix for the dev server and are following the [express custom server example](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/custom-server) or similar.

You can change your instantiation of app from:
```javascript
const app = next({ dev })
```

To something like
```javascript
const app = next({
    dev,
    hostname: "localhost",
    port: 3000 // or whichever port you're using
});
```

Hope this helps :)

This does not sound like a NextJS issue. NextJS does not use ssl (by default). Perhaps it is not able to connect from nonsecure to secure (is this happening in the browser or in the terminal).

If you are seeing this on the terminal, you might test to access the api with a simple GET via curl or wget, and maybe even with a simple nodejs script (just to test connectivity).

You could also try to connect to 127.0.0.1 directly, but that feels like shooting in the dark to me.
author: joggienl

body:
From the docs:

> Next.js allows you to create or update static pages after you’ve built your site. Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) enables you to use static-generation on a per-page basis, without needing to rebuild the entire site. With ISR, you can retain the benefits of static while scaling to millions of pages.

So technically if you would add `revalidate: 60` to the return of `getStaticProps`, that would be valid for that particular page (after 60 seconds). So, a single page will get invalidated after that time. It will only be rebuild _after_ you hit that page again. As long as you don't hit the url, nothing will be rebuild automatically.

I noticed the fetch in `getStaticPaths`. Perhaps you are fetching all blogs there, causing them to have more or like the same invalidation time, perhaps that is what you are noticing.

More practically you could generate only the most popular blogs (or the last, or whatever other preference) and generate those. Due to the `blocking` fallback, the rest will be generated once someone hits the url.

Also, what was your `revalidate` time? you could set it to `60 * 60 * 5` to have it invalid once per 5 hours.
author: joggienl

body:
Some links:

- https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-props#revalidate
- https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration

body: I had it set to `revalidate: 7200, // Revalidate every 2 hours` initially. As for the `fetch` call in the `getStaticPaths`, you have to do that when using something like `[slug].tsx` https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#getstaticpaths-return-values.
author: JamesSingleton

body: I don't want to set it for too long as once I update it, it gets posted to social media. So I wouldn't want someone going to it and looking for the update and not be able to find it because it hasn't been updated due to it being set to every 5 hours.
author: JamesSingleton

body:
You can set it as short or as long as you please indeed. Shorter means updates get in quicker. Perhaps for your use case something between 5 to 60 minutes would be “better”?

About the `getStaticPaths`, it is indeed mandatory when using a dynamic path for `getStaticProps`. The point I was trying to make there is that it is up to you which paths you return. You don’t have to return all of them. You can also return an empty array to make every first visit blocking.

You could use SSR (`getServerSideProps`) to always get the most up to date content. But in most cases for a blog that is overkill. In that case you could consider the cache control header (with `stale-while-revalidate`) but it would essentially be ISR that way in this situation.
author: joggienl

body: Yeah I have a Sports news website, redshirtsports.xyz which I guess you could classify as a blog for now. So if I had it set to lets say 1 minute, it would only revalidate once someone hits it again. In theory I would just publish my post, post to social media and as long as it is after a minute from publishing it _should_ be updated?
author: JamesSingleton

body:
The first one after the timer ends will get the stale content, the regeneration will be triggered and once it is done it will be served.

If you would use 60 as value, perhaps could also (instruct to) visit the page after updating to verify the new content and then post to social media? That would technically take you 60 seconds extra plus generation time (which is very fast in general) 😅.
author: joggienl

body: Technically it is more correct to say that if you hit an url you get the content, if the revalidation time has ended it is considered stale and regeneration will be triggered and if done will be send out as the new version. There are some awesome graphs out there on how it technically works.

Hello.

You should use the `priority` property on any image detected as the LCP element. You can get this in the "performance" tab in your chrome devtools.

As for the array, you could use the index or any uniq value to add priority to only specific images.
Something like: 
```js
yourImages.map((index) => <Image {....} priority={index === 42} />)
```

body:
We currently have the same problem.
Our solution is to remove the generated html pages after the build. This forces them to be rebuilt on first request to said page.
After that they remain static and are served as such.

```bash
❯ npm run build
...

❯ rm .next/server/app/*.html
...

❯ npm run start
```

This is and feels very hacky. But it gives us pre-generate pages at runtime.

In our deployment system, our environment variables are injected at runtime, so any page generated with getStaticProps at build time will not have the right environment variables. Ideally, in my case, there would be a way to request a build on the first request during runtime and then all subsequent requests could be cached.

body:
@JordanTheriault Did you find a solution for this problem?

I have the same issue and I'm pretty sure it's because the Docker image that is deployed contains a generated cache on build time. Every time a Cloud Run container is activated it serves the files from the Docker image and therefore ignores any file that any other container generated and cached. This kind of defeats the purpose of ISR as containers are scaled up and down all the time... I do use a CDN that fixes and the performance is still great, but it's a shame that a container setup like Cloud Run can't fully use the power and efficiency of Next ISR.
author: djohalo2

body: @djohalo2 how does the CDN ensure that no outdated (/ build-time) content is shown? I don't think you can solve this easily without having a shared cache (eg. Redis) for these generated files. Unfortunately only a filesystem is supported as a caching mechanism for Nextjs ISR, which means is not compatible for Cloud Run
author: gboston

body:
@gboston Something went wrong in that sentence. I meant that I configured a Cloud CDN to generally improve caching of statics and get some load of the Cloud Run instances. But you are correct it does not solve the problem of outdated content.

I did find https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/tutorials/network-filesystems-fuse that talks about mounting a Google Cloud Storage network filesystem with Cloud Run. This might be a possible solution. What do you think?
author: djohalo2

body:
I've also looked into this. But it just looks as it is not compatible with a Cloud Native approach if you want to make use of ISR. Build-time files and cache (no idea where the ISR files go at runtime?) should be separate so you could easily clear the cache if a new build is pushed.

I've experimented with a Compute Engine with a Docker container and a volume mounted for the .next directory, but you still need to push the build time files to this volume to ensure you have the latest build. So you need to 2 processes: one to push the static build files and one for your container.

@devolasvegas I recall trying that initially with no luck. Besides, all of the examples in the docs you referenced treat `source` as starting from the root of the domain (without specifying the actual domain name). And even if you could specify the domain name as part of the `source` value, it probably wouldn't be domain-agnostic.

Regardless, the middleware solution has been working well for me, and in fact may not even be necessary in my case since Vercel's servers seem to perform the redirect automatically (I currently host all my NextJS projects on Vercel).
author: caleb531
devolasvegas
caleb531

body:
Heads up for folks potentially implementing this solution, the middleware redirect laid out here doesn't pass the request's search parameters. If that's wanted, it should look more like this:
```
return NextResponse.redirect(
  `${host}/${req.nextUrl.pathname}?${req.nextUrl.searchParams}`,
  308
)
````

Also, note that I'm using the `308` HTTP status code instead of `301` to preserve the HTTP method--that's currently how most frameworks are redirecting, including [Next](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/redirects )

author: vHades0199

body:
I just put together the first steps on getting `zero install/PnP` working with the docker builds. The main trick was to handle the dependencies as your stack trace indicates. I did this by disabling the global cache which moves the dependency cache to the local area, and then copy that local cache with the `.pnp.cjs` file to the same relative location in the image.

Also when I use the latest version of Typescript (4.8.4 at the time of this post), I needed to use the canary version of yarn via:
`yarn set version canary` prior to doing the docker build. Maybe I could have downgraded Typescript to work with 3.x..

Also as an aside, my stuff uses yarn workspaces (monorepo) with the `packages` holding the each workspace. You should be able to figure things out for not a monorepo.

When this runs I get a ~ 180 MB runnable image.


`.yarnrc.docker.yml`
```
enableStrictSsl: false
nodeLinker: pnp
httpTimeout: 300000
enableInlineBuilds: true
enableGlobalCache: false
packageExtensions:
  eslint-config-next@*:
    dependencies:
      next: "*"
      react: "*"
      react-dom: "*"
yarnPath: .yarn/releases/yarn-4.0.0-rc.22.cjs
```

`Dockerfile`
```
FROM node:16-alpine  AS builder

WORKDIR /app

ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

# copy dependencies from the build image
COPY package.json .

COPY yarn.lock .
COPY .yarnrc.docker.yml .yarnrc.yml
COPY .yarn/releases ./.yarn/releases

# copy the app code over
COPY tsconfig.base.json .
COPY tsconfig.json .
COPY .eslint* .
COPY packages ./packages

ENV NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

# install dependencies
RUN yarn install --immutable

# build the web gateway application app
RUN yarn workspace @web-gateway-monorepo/web-gateway-application build


FROM node:16-alpine  AS runner

WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1
ENV NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.pnp.cjs ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.yarn/cache ./.yarn/cache
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/packages/web-gateway-application/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/packages/web-gateway-application/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/packages/web-gateway-application/public ./public

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000

CMD ["node", "-r", "./.pnp.cjs", "server.js"]
```

`next.config.js` - the output standalone is important
```
/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
const nextConfig = {
  reactStrictMode: true,
  swcMinify: true,
  output: 'standalone',
  poweredByHeader: false
}

module.exports = nextConfig
```

Here's some miscellaneous files so you can make sense of the workspaces and general layout.

root `package.json`
```
{
  "name": "web-gateway-monorepo",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "license": "MIT",
  "private": true,
  "workspaces": {
    "packages": [
      "packages/web-gateway-application"
    ]
  },
  "packageManager": "yarn@4.0.0-rc.22",
  "repository": {
    "url": "https://whatever.git"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "yarn run eslint --ext .js,.jsx,.ts,.tsx .",
    "web-dev": "yarn workspace @web-gateway-monorepo/web-gateway-application dev",
    "prepare": "husky install"
  },
  "lint-staged": {
    "*.{ts,js,jsx,tsx}": [
      "yarn prettier --write --ignore-unknown",
      "eslint"
    ]
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "eslint": "^8.24.0",
    "eslint-config-next": "^12.3.1",
    "eslint-config-prettier": "^8.5.0",
    "eslint-plugin-unused-imports": "^2.0.0",
    "husky": "^8.0.1",
    "next": "^12.3.1",
    "prettier": "^2.7.1",
    "react": "^18.2.0",
    "react-dom": "^18.2.0",
    "typescript": "^4.8.4"
  }
}
```

`packages/web-gateway-application/package.json` (where the next.js application lives)
```
{
  "name": "@web-gateway-monorepo/web-gateway-application",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start",
    "lint": "next lint"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "next": "^12.3.1",
    "react": "^18.2.0",
    "react-dom": "^18.2.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/node": "18.7.23",
    "@types/react": "18.0.21",
    "eslint": "^8.24.0",
    "eslint-config-next": "^12.3.1",
    "typescript": "4.8.4"
  }
}
```

author: lewandom

body:
I was ultimately forced to create a seperate `next` and `yarn` config files (standalone + node-modules for Docker; pnp for development). I don't like it, doesn't come with PnP advantages and makes building images longer, but I couldn't see a more suitable solution. 

Dockerfile:
```
FROM node:lts-alpine@sha256:47d97b93629d9461d64197773966cc49081cf4463b1b07de5a38b6bd5acfbe9d as builder

ENV NODE_ENV production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

USER node
WORKDIR /home/node

COPY --chown=node:node . .
RUN mv .docker.yarnrc.yml .yarnrc.yml
RUN mv .docker.next.config.js next.config.js

RUN yarn && yarn build

FROM node:lts-alpine@sha256:47d97b93629d9461d64197773966cc49081cf4463b1b07de5a38b6bd5acfbe9d as runner

EXPOSE 3000

ENV NODE_ENV production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

USER node
WORKDIR /home/node

COPY --from=builder --chown=node:node /home/node/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=node:node /home/node/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=node:node /home/node/.next/static ./.next/static

CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

.dockerignore
```
.next
next.config.js
.yarnrc.yml
```

Should take less space than the cache method though.
author: sszczep

body:
Here may be a better workaround cause I found that `.pnp.cjs` only support CommonJS modules:

**next.config.js:**
```js
/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
const nextConfig = {
    reactStrictMode: true,
    output: "standalone",
    // Other configuration
};

module.exports = nextConfig;
```

**.yarnrc.yml:**
```yml
# These configurations are generated by yarn with commands:
#
# yarn plugin import workspace-tools
# yarn set version latest
#
# You should replace 3.5.1 with the real version of your yarn,
# if you want to manually set these configurations. 
plugins:
    - path: .yarn/plugins/@yarnpkg/plugin-workspace-tools.cjs
      spec: "@yarnpkg/plugin-workspace-tools"

yarnPath: .yarn/releases/yarn-3.5.1.cjs
```

**Dockerfile:**
```dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine AS base

ENV NODE_ENV=production

RUN corepack enable
RUN corepack prepare yarn@stable --activate

FROM base AS deps

WORKDIR /app
COPY ./package.json .
COPY ./.yarn/plugins ./.yarn/plugins
COPY ./.yarn/releases ./.yarn/releases/
COPY ./.yarnrc.yml .
RUN yarn install

FROM base AS builder

WORKDIR /app

COPY . .
COPY --from=deps /app/.yarn ./.yarn/
COPY --from=deps /app/yarn.lock .

RUN yarn build

FROM base AS runner

WORKDIR /app

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static/
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public/

FROM runner AS production

WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=builder /app/.yarn/plugins .yarn/plugins/
COPY --from=builder /app/.yarnrc.yml .
COPY --from=builder /app/.yarn/releases .yarn/releases/

# Delete this folder cause it contains the `next` dependency which was output by Next.js builder.
# If we install dependencies later, yarn will try to rename cache file `***.tmp` to `***.zip`.
# But the name has been claimed already when it comes to `next` to be installed,
# which causes a failure of building image.
RUN rm -rf ./.yarn/cache

RUN yarn workspaces focus --all --production

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

# Replace 3.5.1 with the real version of your yarn.
CMD ["node", "/app/.yarn/releases/yarn-3.5.1.cjs", "--", "node", "/app/server.js"]
```

With this you will only install production dependencies while building docker image.

body: It sounds like your `revalidate` time is low, which would be causing those function calls to be invocated in the background. You might consider raising that revalidation time if you're concerned about function execution time – or taking a look at how long those functions are running. For example, if they're running for 5 seconds, you might consider optimizing / caching the code in that function.
author: leerob

body:
Yes. Revalidate was set to 1 second. That was the result of me misunderstanding how revalidate worked. I was under the impression that getStaticProps would only be invocated if a check of some kind determined that the page data was stale. (I admit I have no idea what that check would be now that I think of it.) But it seems that revalidate simply means that the page will execute getStaticProps at the interval specified by revalidate. Is this correct?

Anyway, I increased revalidate to 3600, and I noticed immediately that the pages were generally only executing functions after the first execution for an hour or so. But after the second validation they began revalidating at unexpected intervals. I've attached an image for reference. You can see in the image multiple static requests to the slug. But then you see lambdas at interval < our current revalidation value (3600).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23176631/150669340-ad372c4a-e665-4327-bc2f-2e21e00bd9f5.jpeg)

This causes me to wonder whether there is some special case in which a serverless function will execute outside of revalidation or build. Any insight here would be helpful!
author: jadamconnor

body: One other thing I noticed is that after increasing the revalidate value, build speed has increased significantly (~3-5x) and there are no more build failures--we were getting a lot of those. The majority of the build fails had to do with the API returning an empty JSON object for a given page. Might this have something to do with attempted revalidations during build?

body:
There are a few issues here.

1. You are on an older `canary` version of Next.js. You should investigate using the newer `canary`/`latest` versions before concluding as issues have been fixed since then.
2. Your module contains JSX, which should be transpiled as it is not an ESM feature. I assume you were trying to create a simple reproduction, but I do want to mention if the module is in the same project as Next.js, you don't need the `package.json` at all.
3. Your `package.json` contains a non-standard `module` field to export `Foo`. Please refer to the Node.js documentation to understand how to do this correctly. I just gave a few pointers in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/33599#issuecomment-1019995460 which are relevant.

body:
I think you have a bit of weird per page layout system, compared with the suggested in the docs anyway, but there's a typo here:

```js
 const Layout = layouts[selected as keyof typeof layouts] || ((children) => <>{children}</>);
```

The way you have it, it should be:

```js
 const Layout = layouts[selected as keyof typeof layouts] || (({ children }) => <>{children}</>);
```

Notice that the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/layouts#per-page-layouts) do:

```js
// Use the layout defined at the page level, if available
  const getLayout = Component.getLayout ?? ((page) => page) // no fragment
```

body:
I think I solved this one for myself. The issue seems to be that if you don't set limits on the rich text field then the complexity can easily exceed the API limits because the complexity is based on the number of linked assets allowed. See https://www.contentful.com/developers/docs/references/graphql/#/introduction/query-complexity-limits.

So, there seems to be 2 ways to fix this. The quick one (if you're just trying to get the sample up and running) is to add some limits into the GraphQL queries in the lib/api.js file in the starter. The better ways seems to be to update the settings for the content model of the Content field of the Post type to limit the number of the linked entities, blocks, etc. In the validation tab of the settings, I just checked all of the "Limit number of..." checkboxes and set the upper limit to 5. I'll adjust that as I create blog posts if I need to.

@ayasalam0
Consider the route `plans/[id]`
If `pageToRedirect = "/plans` then `id` is never supplied, and must be supplied via the query object:

`router.replace(pageToRedirect, { id: 123})`

Equivalently, you could do `router.replace(pageToRedirect + "/123")`

> In fact, we found the error here, it was an internal error. We were doing a redirect without spreading the whole URL, only the pathname, and because of this, the error was happening.
> 
> 
> 
> Before: 
> 
> ```
> 
> if (user?.id) {
> 
>       replace(pageToRedirect);
> 
>     }
> 
> ```
> 
> 
> 
> Now:
> 
> ```
> 
> if (user?.id) {
> 
> replace({ pathname: pageToRedirect, query });
> 
>     }
> 
> ```
> 
> 
> 
> This fixed the error for me. Thanks for all help!
