body: You need to use the Vary header to indicate to the cache (any CDN) that there are multiple variations of the page.
author: lukebussey

body: Sorry but I don't see how that would help. The data flow is like this Azure FrontDoor CDN -> Our Frontend. If the CDN already cached the page, how would A/B testing work?
author: zmarty

body:
Every CDN has a cache key. Usually it'll be the full URL including query string, but if you serve different content for the same full URL as in the case with a server side a/b test, then you'll need to tell the CDN which headers to also include in the cache key.

A good example of this is when your origin server performs content negotiation to determine if the client can accept gzipped responses. In this case there will be two objects stored in cache for the same URL. One uncompressed, and one compressed. In this case the CDN is using the Content-encoding header as part of it's cache key.

- https://developers.cloudflare.com/cache/about/cache-keys
- https://www.fastly.com/blog/ab-testing-edge#keeping-different-versions-cached-with-http-vary
author: lukebussey

body: How would one do an A/B test using the `Vary` header? I mean, you would need to have something that is sent from the browser on each request and can be unique for a group of users. As far as I can see, only a cookie can do this, and using `Vary: Cookie` does not really work. You can of course add some logic to your CDN to read from the cookie and use that to create a cache key.
author: stigkj

body:
Yeah but what would set the cookie in the first place? Anyway I don't think using Vary header would work.

Our ask is this: If we use middleware to switch between two static pages, then let us override the cache header in next.config.js instead of forcing it like it does for all static pages. So then we would make this route return no-cache, middleware will run on every request, and CDN will not cache this route. Then A/B testing will work. Then when A/B test is done, we will remove the middleware, make one of the two static pages be the official one, and re-enable the caching header.
author: zmarty

body:
Yes you could prevent the a/b tested page from caching but the `no-cache` Cache-control directive doesn't prevent caching. It tells the browser and cdn to revalidate.

`Cache-control: private, no-store` will prevent caching on a CDN

Alternatively if the CDN obeys the Vary header, then you could try something like this in the middleware:

```
X-Variation: <variation name>
Vary: X-Variation

body:
I managed to work around it by adding the formatjs eslint plugin. It can autofix the ID's and if added, there is no need for te plugin (at this point).

source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/30174#discussioncomment-1922431
author: joggienl

body:
Thanks, it wasn't immediately clear to me that this was possible based on the docs.

It also wasn't immediately clear how it works, so I hope this helps as a quick resource to someone in the same boat:
Once installed, to get autofix working you'll need to specify in your eslint config the interpolation pattern that would have otherwise been in your .babelrc like so:
```
    "formatjs/enforce-id": [
      "error",
      {
        "idInterpolationPattern": "[sha512:contenthash:base64:6]"
      }
    ],
```

author: MDmubarak786

body:
From [Vercel's documentation](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#static-files-caching)

> [Static Files Caching](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#static-files-caching)
Static files are automatically cached at the edge after the first request and are cached for up to 31 days on Vercel's Edge Network.
If a static file is unchanged, the cached value can persist across deployments due to the hash used in the filename
Optimized images cached will [persist between deployments](https://vercel.com/docs/image-optimization#caching)
The default Cache-Control header contains public, max-age=0, must-revalidate to prevent clients (e.g. browsers) from caching the file locally
[Browser](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#browser)
max-age=N, public
max-age=N, immutable
Where N is the number of seconds the response should be cached. The response must also meet the [caching criteria](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#how-to-cache-responses).

This ensures that the Caching happens on Vercel's Edge Network (which may be seen in this case as a CDN) and not on the browser.

However it would be great if we could customise this behavior further for those files that do not change at all (and could be cached on the browser)
author: nicolopadovan

@nicolopadovan https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/static-assets specifically says it doesn't.

> Next.js cannot safely cache assets in the **public** folder because they may change. The default caching headers applied are:
> Cache-Control: public, max-age=0

body:
## Custom Solution

I decided to host my static files on an external blob storage (like S3), configure it behind a CDN (such as CloudFront), and set up custom caching rules there. Although the initial setup was somewhat frustrating, this approach allows for modifications—such as updating images, localization files, and configuration data—without the need to redeploy the entire application. While this setup makes sense for large projects like mine, it might be an unnecessary extra cost & effort for smaller applications.

body: Figured it out, had to with how Github pages work. Basically need to add a `.nojekyll` file in the `out` folder to tell Git to serve the _next files. See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61450307/js-and-css-not-loading-when-hosting-next-application-on-github-pages
author: bmarshall511

body:
For those who run `npm run deploy` directly instead of using yml file, here is the fix:
```js
"scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start",
    "export": "next export",
    "deploy": "npm run build && npm run export && touch ./out/.nojekyll && gh-pages -d out -t true"
  },
```
This will copy the .nojekyll file to the root folder and serve the static files.

body: Yes, I recently had a call with a Solution Architect at Stripe and there is no way to 100% prevent redirects when using `confirmPayment`. 3D Secure will open in a modal and not redirect, but for other payment methods the only way to prevent/avoiding coding it is to disable non-card payment methods.

author: muhamedh

body: So, I have done some thought about this and if you truly don't want to ever have redirects -- because maybe you want to maintain the client-side state which gets killed by redirects -- there are a couple of ideas with varying pros & cons: - Set up a more robust client-side state system so that entered data is stored in LocalStorage, for example. This way, when a redirect does occur, when the user comes back, all the data entered is still there. - Create your own JavaScript pop-up window to handle Stripe logic. The basis of this is the `window.open(URL)` function. Problem is, most browsers disable these kind of pop-ups by default, and they might have to click an Allow pop-up button which isn't a great experience. Supposedly if the window.open call is in an onClick handler, it has better chances of working. - Similar (and perhaps more reliable) to the previous, is instead of using `window.open`, you can just have an anchor tag like `Click me`. Upon clicking, the user will get sent to a new browser tab. At the end of the process you can use `window.close()` (which actually might be prevented with security restrictions). Or you can instruct the user to close the window to continue. In any of these cases, you'll probably need to maintain some server side logic that the client side can communicate with asynchronously about the user's payment & checkout state, perhaps through periodic polling as your server will be getting callback hooks directly from Stripe's servers.

body:
Just want to say that I'd really love to see this. Next engages in some aggressive caching of components, which can cause a lot of confusion during development. I would really like to see a solution for opting out of said caching during development without having to add code that will mess up the caching on production.

It can be an especially bad issue when switching between branches of a codebase.

author: RichardODonoghue

body:
Wild this is not a thing as of yet, especially as projects become larger and have aggressive back-end caching. Us front-end people  can't debug why a page hangs on first render then fine for X+ hours while the cache re-validates, so you can only test on every deployment cause the dev side takes too long to render to know if the page is taking a while cause of the page await or cause the dev environment takes like 10 seconds to render out again.
Would be nice to just disable caching entirely while keeping the "use cache" tags so we don't gotta go through each backend route, disabling the dozens of cached queries just to find that we needed one Suspense here and there.
Makes the user experience a hassle when a page takes a while to load after they click on a link and nothing happens while their data loads. While the developer is left scratching their head on why a route takes a while to load once and then fine until the cache becomes stale again...

body:
10 Pound Gorilla | Senior Full-Stack Engineer | Remote (US, Canada) | Contract | $30-$90/hr | [Link](https://www.indeed.com/viewjob?t=nextjs+react+developer+contractor+ecommerce+application&jk=dc45085e68d47c50&_ga=2.53118888.1263963683.1643646783-752049390.1643214935)

10 Pound Gorilla is building a multi-tenant eCommerce application which supports limited content management. We’re utilizing .NET APIs, NextJS, redeux, and serverless for CI/CD. 

Currently we have all of the HTML and CSS mark-up coded and ready to be integrated into Next/React components and pages. We have a couple developers working with the Next/React integration at this point. Nearly all of the APIs have been developed, we have 2 currently wrapping up development.

Our goal is to find additional developers who can help fill the shortage we have in react development and who can help us get the project through the finish line.

We have a mixture of areas where we are looking for support, from wiring up react components and pages, redux and state management, code deployment, payment gateway integrations, and identity provider integrations.

We are looking to hire the right candidates immediately and anticipate the project to be 3-6 months when factoring in the MVP launch and post-launch production support.

If this project sounds of interest and you feel you or your organization would be a good fit, we ask that you [check out our job posting on Indeed](https://www.indeed.com/viewjob?t=nextjs+react+developer+contractor+ecommerce+application&jk=dc45085e68d47c50&_ga=2.53118888.1263963683.1643646783-752049390.1643214935) and apply for the position. At that point we’d like to schedule an introduction call and to discuss the project and your team's capabilities in more detail.

body:
Genie AI | Frontend Developer (React) | London OR UK Remote | Full-time | [https://apply.workable.com/genie-ai/j/8166DCED3C/](url)

Genie AI is a deep learning based software company with a mission to open source the law.

We are disrupting the legal industry by changing the business model ground up.

What Github did with open source code, Instagram and TikTok with entertainment, Airbnb with hospitality, and Uber with travel, Genie is doing with Legal contracts.

We are building a community-based AI law platform.



Requirements
About the role
We're looking for an experienced Senior React developer who has a passion for building an elegant & modern interface to solve complex real-world problems.

You are a product-focused, design thinking and curious developer who wants to turn user needs into simple yet clever solutions
You are excited to develop a community-based platform web application in React
You are interested in creating a rich, beautiful and elegant user interface to perfection
You enjoy and find it fun to build re-usable components
You are keen to protect your code with solid tests ranging from unit to automation
You care to collaborate and participate in solution design and performing experiments with the team
You are not afraid to take risks and learn advanced topics
You are eager for friendly, coherent spoken and written communication to exchange complex ideas
You build coding standards and peer review code to ensure code is neat and clean
What we need
Experience in building SaaS web-based products using;

Solid knowledge of React or NextJS framework
Typescript and Javascript
Proficient in HTML, CSS
What we would like
GraphQL
Knowledge of Design Patterns in Web development
Experience with test frameworks for unit, integration, and automation testing

What would be nice

Working with real-time collaboration applications & browser based editors
Experience with server-side rendering, web workers & service workers
Built apps with Micro-services architecture
Solid experience in responsive UI development

About our stack
Micro-services architecture, React, Next.js, Angular 12, Playwright, NodeJS, RabbitMQ, GraphQL, Redis, MongoDB, Python, AWS, Docker, Kubernetes, TensorFlow, PyTorch, ProseMirror. You're not expected to know all of this, but you will get the opportunity to learn based on your interest!


Benefits

What do Genies get?
Substantial equipment budget for you to set up a home working environment, including a top of the range laptop, monitor, wireless keyboard, mouse, and office chair
Generous stock options - we want everyone to share in our success!
Unlimited budget for business, law, or technology books
Unlimited holiday
Fully flexible working
Cycle to work scheme
Regular meditation and wellbeing sessions
Employee benefits platform where you can buy plants, food deliveries, yoga, wellbeing sessions, and more on us!
Optional pre-tax charity giving
Regular team lunches and dinners after lockdown (and plenty of social activity in the meantime!)
The opportunity to join a bright, fast-growing startup at a grassroots level and genuinely make a difference

About Genie
Genie AI was created at Entrepreneur First in November 2017. We're now a team of 16 incredibly talented and fun Genies from 7 countries split across Business and Technology.

We’re currently flexible on work from home versus being in the office; our long-term plan is for everyone to work 2 days a week in the office, and the rest from home, so we get the best of both worlds. Our office is based in Shoreditch, London, with lots of great places to eat and drink nearby.

We are backed by world-leading experts such as Lord Neuberger (former President of the Supreme Court), Charlie Songhurst (former Head of Strategy at Microsoft), Khosla Ventures (founded by Vinod Khosla, co-founder of Sun Microsystems, the inventors of Java), Connect Ventures (also funded Citymapper and Typeform) and other investors with serious pedigree.

We have been featured on [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2019/07/23/genie-ai/) and on [Forbes](https://www.forbes.com/sites/mirunagirtu/2019/09/09/29-of-the-uks-most-exciting-ai-startups-race-to-scale/?sh=26fe97d2a6dc) as one of the top 29 UK AI startups.

We're poised for rapid expansion, and we want you to be our next Genie. Come and shake up the £100bn global legal industry with us!

body:
> ## Problem
> Hello, I'd like to be able to split my source code into modules/domain contexts, like so:
> 
> ```
> src/
> ├── auth/
> │   ├── api/
> │   │   ├── login.ts
> │   │   ├── logout.ts
> │   │   └── signup.ts
> │   ├── components/
> │   │   ├── LoginForm.tsx
> │   │   └── SignupForm.tsx
> │   └── pages/
> │       ├── login.tsx
> │       └── signup.tsx
> ├── core/
> │   ├── components/
> │   │   ├── Form.tsx
> │   │   └── LabeledTextField.tsx
> │   ├── layouts/
> │   |   └── DefaultLayout.tsx
> |   └── pages/
> │       ├── _app.tsx
> │       ├── _document.tsx
> │       ├── 404.tsx
> │       └── index.tsx
> ├── posts/
> │   ├── api/
> │   │   ├── createPost.ts
> │   │   ├── getPost.ts
> │   │   └── getPosts.ts
> │   ├── components/
> │   │   ├── Post.tsx
> │   │   └── Posts.tsx
> │   └── pages/
> │       └── posts/
> │           ├── index.tsx
> │           └── [slug].tsx
> └── users/
>     ├── api/
>     │   └── getUser.ts
>     ├── components/
>     │   └── User.tsx
>     └── pages/
>         └── users/
>             └── [id].tsx
> ```
> 
> Is there any known pattern for accomplishing something like this?
> 
> This is purely an organizational change; the application would still be built as one cohesive unit. In my experience, this kind of organization is necessary once your codebase grows above ~50k lines of code.
> 
> The Next.js automatic file-based routing would still work as expected, combining `pages/` and `api/` for all modules, and it would be up to the user to ensure that there are no conflicts (i.e. two different modules defining the same page route).
> 
> ## Examples
> [Blitz.js](https://blitzjs.com/) (pre framework-agnostic pivot) supports this [by default](https://blitzjs.com/docs/file-structure).
> 
> > The file structure nested inside `app/` can be anything you want, but we recommend the following:
> > 
> > * First layer folders to be a "domain context", like `core/`, `projects/`, `users/`, `billing/`, etc.
> > * All other folders like `components/` and `hooks/` go inside one of the above domain context folders
> 
> ## Existing issues/discussions
> I couldn't find much in Next.js issues or discussions that addresses this. #6040 is related, but focuses on complete build separation rather than simple organizational changes:
> 
> > Each part should be deployable separately
> 
> ## Explored Solutions
> The article [Dividing Your App Into Modules](https://www.parthkabra.me/nextjs/custom-routing) describes the issue well, and explores a few options:
> 
> 1. [Custom Page Extensions](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/custom-page-extensions) + [Rewrites](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/rewrites) + importing/exporting
>    This works, but it's very tedious to maintain the original structure in `pages/` and `api/` for the importing.
> 2. [Multi Zones](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/multi-zones)
>    Much like [Feasibility of micro frontends #6040](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/6040), this treats each module as a separate build context.
> 
> I also considered using [Turborepo](https://turborepo.org/), but this also treats each module as a separate Next.js application.

body:
For anyone still looking for a solution to this, I've been using this project structure with a lot of success for a ~50k LOC project:

```
src/
├── modules/
│   ├── Contact/
│   │   ├── create.ts
│   │   ├── delete.ts
│   │   ├── list.ts
│   │   ├── Table.tsx
│   │   └── update.ts
│   └── User/
│       ├── create.ts
│       ├── delete.ts
│       ├── list.ts
│       ├── Table.tsx
│       └── update.ts
├── pages/
│   ├── contacts.tsx
│   └── users.tsx
├── ui/
│   ├── Button.tsx
│   └── Table.tsx
└── utils/
```

`src/modules/` contains the vast majority of the business logic and feature-specific code.

`src/pages/` is mostly just used for the file-based routing, and more-or-less just serves to import components from `src/modules/`.

author: madfcat

body:
The real answer is that using structured (JSON) logging makes more sense out of dev and especially in production. All pino-pretty is doing is taking structured data and turning it into a more easily scannable string. However it is also  annihilating the well structured data that you could have stored in your logs (and could be consumed by other tooling).

Given that you can use pino-pretty in dev, I think the best solution is to check if the app is running locally (or that it isn't running on vercel, by using the envs vercel exposes) and if it's running locally use pino-pretty, and if not, don't. 

That way you only use pink pretty in local dev, and staging is production-like in that it uses structured logging. 

If you really want pretty logs out of dev, it's very easy to write your own formatter that doesn't use workers, and therefore will work on vercel. However there are performance considerations there (which is exactly why pino-pretty uses workers).

Hi,
You will need to patch Next.js for this, here's one for next@12.1.6
You can use https://www.npmjs.com/package/patch-package to auto install the patch.

[next+12.1.6.patch](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/files/11009343/next%2B12.1.6.patch)

For this part
```js
+    delete props1.pageProps.node
+    delete props1.pageProps.i18n
+    delete props1.pageProps.menus
```
I'm removing only the props i don't wanna keep in the HTML, If you are using NextAuth for example, you want to keep `session` coming from SSR, and this may vary depending on your use case.

And finally on the _hydrate part, i just restore the props i removed.

Hope this helps.

author: vilasp

body:
I will mark this as the answer since it shows how it should work. In my case thought it was an issue with understanding the underlying mechanism of how `Rails` works. It turned out that the call I was making from Next did get through the `Rails` request pipeline which showed that the cookie was attached to the `set-cookie` header in the response of the controller. It was however later in a middleware stripped because it did not passed the `CSRF` check which was in place for `Rails`.

Lesson learned 📖🎓: Check your middlewares/response pipelines and don't be lazy if you don't understand how to properly debug the libs you are using (looking at you `Rails`), the truth is out there 👽 .
author: vilasp

body:
Note... using:
```
response.headers.get("set-cookie");
```

will result in getting mangled cookies.

If your server responds potentially with multiple cookies you will need to use:

```
response.headers.raw()['set-cookie']
```
which outputs cookies as an array of strings.

author: juanbzz

body: Great, thanks for your suggestion. I've never heard of AWS SQS, so I did a little research on it. Based on what I've found, I assume the architecture should be the following: as the 3rd party transaction/trade gets created, we add a message containing the transaction id to the queue. SQS will then trigger a Lambda Function that receives the id and tracks the transaction. Am I right?
author: tavindev

body:
You need to decouple both processes with a message queue. Like @j0lv3r4 already suggested AWS SQS is a great option for that.

>  need to track a 3rd party transaction/trade and check if it is completed within 5 minutes.

How do you check that? Are you checking some DB entries or calling some REST API to check if it is finished?

Using webhooks would be perfect for that but that depends on the third party. Then it would simply call your API when the third party is finished. This will probably not be supported so let's look at another way with a message queue.

>  as the 3rd party transaction/trade gets created, we add a message containing the transaction id to the queue. SQS will then trigger a Lambda Function that receives the id and tracks the transaction. Am I right?

Yes, kind of. You've got two more issues I think:

1. Lambda timeout
2. You don't know when it will be finished

So you need to regularly call a lambda function to check if it is finished and can't leave it running in a loop (this is an anti-pattern and expensive). 

For that, you would also need a cron-job to call your lambda function every 5 minutes for example. For the cron-job, you could use something like GitHub actions.

For the message queue, I'd recommend (shamelessly) the service I've built: https://serverlessq.com 
You can simply reuse your existing API routes within Vercel and send your messages to your message queue. I am adding support for Cron Jobs pretty soon as well.
With that, you don't need to add another huge component (i.e. AWS) to your application and you don't need to learn all about SQS. 

Let me know if you have any other questions I hope that helps 🙂

body:
This is probably what you looking for
```ts
const searchTerm = req.query.searchTerm as string;
```

BUT it's possible to pass multiple query with same name in the URL... e.g.: 

`/api/search?q=1&q=2&q=3` 👉 `q = ["1", "2", "3"]`

`/api/search?q=1` 👉 `q = "1"`

Just use 
```
router.events.emit("routeChangeError",  "your error message", "your-url", { shallow: false });
```

instead of
```
router.events.emit('routeChangeError')
```

author: tubbo

body:
Hi, Webpack 5 introduced caching for faster builds, which Webpack 4 lacked.

`node_modules` are heavily cached for the best performance, and are only invalidated if your package manager sees a change through one of these files:

- `package.json`
- `yarn.lock`
- `package-lock.json`

A possible solution could be that you use `externalDir` and let Next.js transpile your dependencies in development: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/26420

This will add HMR to your packages as well, improving the feedback loop during development.
author: balazsorban44

body:
> node_modules are heavily cached for the best performance, and are only invalidated if your package manager sees a change through one of these files:
> 
> package.json
> yarn.lock
> package-lock.json

Is there any way to configure this, to add to the list of files that are watched and which invalidate the cache if changed?

For example: we use `yalc` for pre-publish testing of bundled packages outside their repo: `yalc publish` in repo A and `yalc add` or `yalc update` in repo B (which includes a NextJS app). Then repo B can test a realistic published bundle in a NextJS app before publishing to live registries, and spot bundling-related issues such as large size, broken treeshaking, implicit dependency/versioning errors, etc. Each yalc update updates a `yalc.lock` file where the dependency bundle has changed. 

If we could locally add `'yalc.lock'` to the above list in our NextJS app's webpack config somewhere, it'd probably all just work fine, but as is we have to manually wipe the caches each update.
author: AlanSl
morhekil

body: This has made things very difficult for users of bit.dev components. Since bitdev creates symlinks into node_modules you effectly lose any hotreload capabilities when working on your bit components. Only option to view a small change in your app is to delete the .next folder and wait a year for your application to fully recompile:(:(
author: brandoncollins7

body:
This answer on stackoverflow solved it for me: [link](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71345874/should-changes-in-node-modules-invalidate-the-webpack-build-cache/72576675#72576675)

```js
snapshot: {
    managedPaths: [/^(.+?[\\/]node_modules)[\\/]((?!your-modules)).*[\\/]*/],
},
```

Related Webpack docs: https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#managedpaths

body:
@eric-burel . We are running Nextjs on our own infra. I have some code in my middleware that requires to encrypt something using the crypto nodejs package. Since the middleware file runs on Edge (or at least the Nextjs build says so. We get some error when building if crypto is directly used: `The edge runtime does not support Node.js 'crypto' module.`), I can't call my crypto function directly there. So, I created an endpoint on app/api/encrypt/route.ts. When I run locally or on Vercel, everything works correctly because I can call that endpoint like this:
```
const response = await fetch(`${request.nextUrl.origin}/api/channel/encrypt?channel=${channelName}`)
```

Now, my problem is with Docker. `request.nextUrl.origin` is the container name and when I run the app, it calls this URL `https://booking-ui-86cd7b6dd5-tvnwk:3000/api/channel/encrypt?channel=blabla` 

Is there another way to call these endpoints without using the  `request.nextUrl.origin`. 

`const response = await fetch(`/api/channel/encrypt?channel=${channelName}`)` doesn't work either because it's not a valid URL. Getting ` Failed to parse URL from /api/channel/encrypt?channel=blabla`

body: @nicholasio , yeah. It worked when using a variable. Being inside Kubernetes we had to use the service name in the env variable because the origin was the pod ID initially. Either the service name or the main URL of the website works. The issue with using the final URL of the website is that it will go outside the network and then again inside which is a bit slower. Not the end of the world, but using the service name instead, which keeps the request inside the network, is better.
author: IonelLupu

body:
Late to the party here, but a small note on how we did this:

1) We never use the `nextUrl` property, due to Next.js' modifications. We found it more reliable to use the WebStandard properties on the  `request` object, like the `url` and the `headers`.

2) For most of our middleware host logic, we just grab the `Host` header: `new Headers(request.headers).get('Host')`. Not sure if that is useful here. 

Are you seeing an app slowdown, due to fetches in middleware? I am considering adding a small redis fetch in middleware, but worried it will slow middleware down. It is being used by prefetches and so on, so needs to be fast.

body:
This is more of a workaround that is better than your current method. (In my opinion of course)
You could use a fetching data library like SWR also by Vercel.  

```js
useSWR('/api/data', fetcher, { fallbackData: prefetchedData })
```
Where prefetched data would be the applicable props from getServerSideProps 
And then just have an api page that calls the same internal code as getServerSideProps and returns the applicable props.

When you need to update it, you can just call mutate. It will also automatically re-fetch it on window focus after a few seconds (by default)

Here's a link to client side rendering.
https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/client-side

body:
This is amazing, thank you!

After having switched over an application of mine to the new `edge` runtime (which required upgrading Next.js, of course), I noticed that the size of the bundles generated for edge SSR have increased in size dramatically, though: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34185.

This means that the "1 MB" threshold mentioned above has now been crossed:

  | Node (server) | Node (lambda) | Edge
-- | -- | -- | --
Cold Boot | / | ~250ms | Instant
Code Size | / | 50MB | 1MB

Have been digging into Next and really liking it so far in the context of evaluating it for our business needs and the Middleware API is great!  However, [the limitations they impose as per the Edge Runtime restrictions](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime#unsupported-apis)... not so much.  😅

body:
@shuding I've successfully tested the edge runtime to work with other V8-based (edge) providers like Cloudflare Pages / Workers, but I feel like the build output could be optimized to provide more flexibility and efficiency on where you deploy Next.js on the edge.

In the current implementation of the build export of Next.js, each function is implemented as its own individual server with only the routes for that one specific page or API endpoint. On platforms like Cloudflare, they require you to bundle everything into one single Worker. This means for it to run properly there, you'd need to write your own, custom router. 

These individual functions also cause side-effects, currently in `enhanceGlobals`, which adds `__import_unsupported` to the global scope. A second SSR page definition in your bundle will crash the function since it can only be defined once: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/4cd8b23032895ab18878d443625e671b291c87d6/packages/next/server/web/adapter.ts#L217
 
In an experimental implementation of an edge function that bundles these, I essentially import all the edge functions into a single one based on `middleware-manifest.json`. The imports luckily add an entry to the `_ENTRIES` global scope, which in turn we can use to forward requests within our custom router implementation.

Preferably, I'd love to see these pages exported more efficiently, maybe as truly individual pages with a separate file for the router/server, so that other tools and platforms can provide their own routers for it.

The Vercel Build API has this issue too, but worse since the Vercel Build Output API doesn't do chunking.

Would love to get your thoughts on supporting other platforms and what good methods forward would be.

Here's an example implementation https://nextflare-demo.pages.dev/ (source code is available on request).
author: ItsWendell

body: The individual server bundling was an incremental path to implement this new runtime, over time it will become a lean wrapper on top of the actual component renderer. Then another bundle pass can combine these endpoints and add a router layer there. Another reason is to make it really lightweight and performant (size limitation and cold boot speed, differs on different platforms), so it's not ideal to concat all endpoints by default.

author: mlagergren

body:
+1 for this, not being able to use a full Node runtime in middleware blocks some legitimate use cases. Not being able to use `Buffer.from` for instance limits the ability to decode JSON Web Tokens, which I'd like to be able to do for various reasons. My workaround for now is to use `new Buffer`, which is deprecated and won't be a viable solution forever. I may need to start offloading Node-dependent logic into API routes, which adds more complexity and unnecessary network requests.

Extending what the Edge runtime supports is probably counter to the reasons it was created in the first place, so being able to opt into forgoing the performance advantages and using Node instead would be nice to have.

Edit: I've realized that `import { Buffer } from 'buffer'` solves my particular issue. From what I read about Edge, I did not expect this to work. So my immediate pain is gone, but the general argument still stands.

author: lukebrandonfarrell
karlhorky

body:
@karlhorky Of course, so yesterday I was experiencing an error when running `next build`:

```
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
Failed to compile.

./node_modules/graphql/error/GraphQLError.mjs
Dynamic Code Evaluation (e. g. 'eval', 'new Function', 'WebAssembly.compile') not allowed in Edge Runtime 
Learn More: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/edge-dynamic-code-evaluation

Import trace for requested module:
./node_modules/graphql/error/GraphQLError.mjs
./node_modules/graphql/error/index.mjs
./node_modules/graphql/index.mjs
./node_modules/@apollo/client/core/LocalState.js
./node_modules/@apollo/client/core/ApolloClient.js
./node_modules/@apollo/client/core/index.js
./node_modules/@apollo/client/index.js
./src/utils/check-user-onboarding-status/check-user-onboarding-status.ts
```

This was due to creating a middleware in our application and using `check-user-onboarding-status.ts` (an internal util) which used `@apollo`, which uses `graphql` which apparently uses Dynamic Code Evaluation (e. g. 'eval', 'new Function', 'WebAssembly.compile').

Since we don't run on Vercel, but we run on AWS this wasn't a problem, so to bypass these build issues, we exported `config` from `middleware`:

```
export const config = {
  runtime: 'nodejs', // for Edge API Routes only
  unstable_allowDynamic: [
    '/src/utils/check-user-onboarding-status/check-user-onboarding-status.ts',
    '/node_modules/graphql/**',
  ],
};
```

Maybe this doesn't solve your specific issue, but I wanted to share what problem we were running into, how we can to this thread, and what solved it for us.

Hope that makes sense :)

author: andresgutgon

body:
Imagine building a framework for building web apps with server side rendering that does not support running simple functions on every http request on the server (to handle cross cutting concerns like logging, tracing, auth, cookies, headers). This restriction that middleware only runs in the edge runtime is awful. I spend a lot of time configuring middleware to work with my auth logic just to find out that I can not use it with my simple node.js custom deployment. I dont want to be forced to ANY hosting provider, I want to choose myself where I want to host the server that runs my app. This is the problem with a profit-oriented company really locking the community to a particular deployment platform. AT LEAST stating that middleware can only run in edge runtime which is only really supported by vercel should be the first thing in the middleware documentation, so everybody knows what they sign up for. Middleware running in node.js alongside the other server code should be the default and running on the edge should be the opt-in. Not the other way around! And the other way is not even supported... Man I had a bad gut feeling with next. How this got 100k stars? Delusional.

For all folks struggling with this and don't have the biggest performance requirements: Spin up a reverse proxy that handles your middleware stuff and route it to your actual pure node.js next.js server. And for folks who are not yet locked in: Really think about using something different than next :)

body:
What I'd suggest:

- In NGINX (or whatever web server you have in front of your next application) pattern-match calls to .json endpoints
- Check the "Sec-Fetch-Mode" header (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Sec-Fetch-Mode)
- If it's "navigate", return 403 and add the response header "X-Robots-Tag: noindex" (https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag)

body:
I believe this is a common setup issue.

The "easiest" way to resolve is to link `node_modules/react` from your app back to the component lib. But this might not be the best solution.

It is better solved using a workspace setup instead, which will hoist React and other common dependencies to the top level that will be shared across your workspaces (your app and component lib in this case). No need for symlinking manually.

Without any special config, moving these two into a common directory you are able to just add one extra file:

```js
// package.json
{
  "name": "my-workspace",
  "private": true,
  "workspaces": ["app", "components"],
  "scripts": { "dev": "cd app && yarn dev" }
}
```

Where `app` is your Next.js app, and `components` is your components library.

This could be further optimized to reduce the developer feedback loop in development.

Hope this helps!

author: thure

body:
Most likely Next.js can't do a lot about the issue. 
First of all Next.js is able to resolve `react/jsx-runtime` without `.js` extension during build time. 
The problem we see here happens in runtime when Next.js running a server (or does SSG) on Node.js which is trying to import an `external` from a library. Due to [Node.js resolution algorithm](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v14.x/docs/api/esm.html#esm_resolution_algorithm) it won't try to add `.js` to your imports and throw "module not found error".
You have two ways to solve this on library level:

- Upgrade to React 18 where they [added](https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/34aa5cfe0d9b6ec4667e02bf46ab34d83dfb2d6d/packages/react/package.json#L22) `exports` instructions to their `package.json`
- Change your Rollup configuration to add `.js` to the import statement of your `external`. You can do this by adding `output.paths` config block, like:
  ```
  paths: {
    "react/jsx-runtime": "react/jsx-runtime.js",
  }
  ```
- (Just a blind idea/question) Is it possible to have a custom runtime Node.js resolver which can be installed as a plugin in a project?

Hi,
You will need to patch Next.js for this, here's one for next@12.1.6
You can use https://www.npmjs.com/package/patch-package to auto install the patch.

[next+12.1.6.patch](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/files/11009343/next%2B12.1.6.patch)

For this part
```js
+    delete props1.pageProps.node
+    delete props1.pageProps.i18n
+    delete props1.pageProps.menus
```
I'm removing only the props i don't wanna keep in the HTML, If you are using NextAuth for example, you want to keep `session` coming from SSR, and this may vary depending on your use case.

And finally on the _hydrate part, i just restore the props i removed.

Hope this helps.

author: riceboyler

body:
I am very happy to say we have finally found a fix for this. For people who use Google Fonts or Adobe Fonts exclusively, you'll probably never see this issue as Next.js properly optimizes those fonts and pre-fetches for performance. In our case, we use the Circular Pro font which has to be loaded in from our CDN, and following the Chakra-UI documentation was actually the problem here. We should have followed the Next.js documentation for loading in fonts, and as soon as we switched to this, the problem has gone away.

The key document is here: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/font-optimization and the key to this is to load your fonts in the Head of either your `_document.js` file or individual files as needed, NOT to use the `Global` tag provided by emotion.

I hope this helps other people who encounter this VERY strange issue!

body:
Hi! I don't think this has anything to do specifically with nextjs, but I have a few suggestions for your code.

First, I would put your check for a valid token in useEffect. I'm not 100% sure it has to be in a useEffect hook, but I always have problems with conditional rendering based on an startup variable check if its just in an 'if" statement.  

Second, I would move your "router.push('/admin') out of the render and put it where you put the check for a token. I'm not sure it won't work in the render, but logically you don't even want to get to the render part if you already determined you don't have a token.

Third, what's up with using hooks and functional components to return a class component? I've never seen that.

Good luck!

author: GabenGar

body:
I would also say you need to decouple these two a bit. Even with Next.js API routes, you'll face some limitations.

I am not sure if they function per sé should take 20 minutes to get followers but in general it is good practice that users only wait for synchronous processing on things they really need. For you function `addFollowing` it could for example only be some counter if it returns fast or only a message "Following initiated".  All other tasks should run asynchronously in the background.

I'd use a message queue for things like that. You could pass the `followings` to a message queue and let it run in an async way.

For example: 

1. User clicks on Button _add following_
2. Function in Vercel sends the message to a queue
3. Async flow starts
4. Function returns "process started" 

The user now sees a response and the function `addFollowings` can be processed in the background.

This only makes sense in case you don't need this data right away but if it takes 20 minutes anyway I don't think you want your user to wait for that.

You could also split up your data into more messages to not reach the timeout. E.g. just 10 `followings` per message. 

You need to define a separate consumer (either in Vercel or in another computing environment like AWS / Heroku). 

Your message queue could be something like RabbitMq, SQS, or similar.

I've built https://serverlessq.com which is a hosted message queue solution that lets you decouple your applications exactly like I described above only with Vercel functions for example. It won't help you with the timeout but you could send a lot of messages and stay within your Vercel environment 🙁 

I hope that helps!

body:
Suggestion: Add a `transformations` prop to the Image component that accepts and object with cloudinary transformation values. Merge this object with the default transformations added by Image.
```jsx
<Image {...otherProps} transformations={{ b: 'rgb:ffffff', e: 'art:hokusai' }} />
// appends 'b_rgb:ffffff,e_art:hokusai' to the transformations in the cloudinary url
```
I'm not sure how this prop would work with other image loaders, perhaps they have need for a similar transformations option.

body:
Hey @lucashfreitas, I had the same problem as you so thanks for answering your question.

On your point:
> whatever path is considered "index" in the list.: that's my question, what path is considered index when using a catch all route? I tried to pass index, ``, / and none of them worked.

While looking into the same question I found that we can pass `null`, `[]`, `undefined` or `false` to make `getStaticProps` generate the index page for a route.

> If the page uses an [optional catch-all route](https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/dynamic-routes#optional-catch-all-routes), use null, [], undefined or false to render the root-most route. For example, if you supply slug: false for pages/[[...slug]], Next.js will statically generate the page /.

(From the final point on https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#paths)

body:
Hello.

Middlewares are run on your server.
It intercepts incoming requests and allows you to run code before those requests are completed.

The client isn't aware of what is being done on your middlewares but will be affected if you modify any headers or response.
author: thibautsabot

body:
Hello.

When using a custom loader, the provider must be able to handle next/image features.
For example you can use it with cloudflare: https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/image-resizing/integration-with-frameworks

If you want node to handle this, you don't have to specify a custom loader.
The [squoosh](https://github.com/GoogleChromeLabs/squoosh) library (or [sharp](https://github.com/lovell/sharp)) will be used to perform images transformations.

More infos here: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#built-in-loaders
author: thibautsabot

body:
I had the same problem. 
I solved it with the following:

1. **First set output to 'standalone' just to replicate production build - to show 'url paramter not allowed' in my local build.**
This can be removed after testing.
**next.config.ts**
```ts
const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  output: "standalone"
}
```
2. **Transpiile the customLoader before next build**
**Dockerfile**
```sh
RUN npx tsc --allowJs --outDir . --target esnext --skipLibCheck true --esModuleInterop true --module commonjs ./imageLoader.ts
```
3. **Reference the transpiled javascript file: imageLoader** **.js**
**next.config.ts**
```js
{
  output: "standalone",
   images: {
     domains: [
       "image.domain.etc",
     ],
     loader: "custom",
     loaderFile: "./imageLoader.js",
     remotePatterns: [{
        hostname: "image.domain.etc"
      }],
   }
}
 ```
4. **Do Next build AFTER the transpile of customLoader.ts AND include transpile of next.config.ts to next.config.js to be COPIED to your production build.**

**Dockerfile**
```sh
RUN npx tsc --allowJs --outDir . --target esnext --skipLibCheck true --esModuleInterop true --module commonjs ./imageLoader.ts
RUN npm run build
RUN npx tsc --allowJs --outDir . --target esnext --skipLibCheck true --esModuleInterop true --module commonjs ./next.config.ts
```

5. **When copying to production for your custom next server**
cp public
cp .next
cp next.config.js
cp imageLoader.js

**Working example**
https://github.com/inspiraller/docker-wolfi-nextjs-s3

Inside the `app/` directory, there is a powerful new way to fetch data with React’s `use()` hook and the extended Web `fetch()` API. This allows you to colocate data fetching directly inside components for the most flexibility, including inside layouts.

body:
Crawlers will be able to fetch your pages, even when they aren't generated yet.

Crawling the pages won't update the cache for the static pages, so there won't be any revalidation happening yet.
However, it will get the right content. I think it just bypasses the cache layer.

body:
You can use `await res.unstable_revalidate(fullPath);`.

I have a custom server and when the server starts, will call a next.js api, that calls `await res.unstable_revalidate(fullPath);`.

Your custom server can fetch all your pages, and for each page just call the api. 

You can revalidate the most popular pages first to deploy faster.

body:
Issue was caused by wrong `<a>` tag nesting, for `next/link` component, In our case we had `<a>` tag not as direct child of `next/link` but as a child of a functional component without `React.forwardRef`, it looks like this kind of wrong usage triggers `getStaticProps` function to run  during `next/link` `prefetch`, so every time we have some link to another page in viewport it triggers `getStaticProps` of that page and several functions of that type in same time overload CPU. 

Propper usage of `<a>` tag fix the issue.

body:
Good morning.  The [id].js is for a dynamic route. Meaning if you have '/blog/[id].js' as your pages structure, any url hits to '/blog/1', '/blog/something', will be routed to the file [id].js.

So using a hook like useRouter.

if you check the value of

`const queryParameters = useRouter()`

and 

`console.log(queryParameters.query)`

the value printed out is the path name. So '1' or 'something', as an object with the form {id: '1'}.

This is the same behavior if you have another file in your pages directory like '/blog/dashboard.js'. Any hits to '/blog/dashboard' will use the file dashboard.js. And assuming you only have [id].js, index.js, and dashboard.js, any paths that are not 'blog/dashboard' will use [id].js. "dashboard" will still be assigned to query.

I hope that helps.

body:
Can't you just have your getStaticProps and getStaticPaths dynamically generate the pages? Meaning you use an api call that returns an array of posts, and if the length of the array changes, then you render more pages in getStaticProps and getStaticPaths. In the fallback example the pre-rendered pages are hard coded, which I don't think is necessary.  

edit: Setting fallback to true will let any page not prerendered be rendered in your [...slug].js page. I guess my point was I assume you don't have the pages you want prerendered typed out in your getStaticPaths. If you do then just change fallback to true and you shouldn't have to do anything else. If you don't have your paths hardcoded, you might have to many pages to prerender them all, so you might want to still set fallback to true, and maybe only prerender some, maybe in your data fetch you add some logic to only fetch and prerender the most popular that are probably linked on the front of the blog.

For your example, I don't think you need to use getServerSideProps since the data won't change much and you can just use getStaticProps for the blog, and also set a revalidate prop if needed.

body: Bonjour! That issue looks pretty close! Yes you could test things out on a canary branch. But I meant to try the easiest things first. It might help out to do or think about the following and add it to that open issue. - Change to standard HTML . Since you don't have hundreds of images this should be quick to do. If you use standard HTML and still have a problem, then it's probably your code/config that is wrong. (doing this might break your styling, so the page might look ugly with huge images, but as long as they load should be ok. Or you could inline style the img tags) - React is a SPA, single page application. Meaning if you click on a page, everything should load, and things that are not changing should not load again. So I don't think you would want your images to constantly reload as a user clicks around. They should only reload if the page refreshes or if the images change. Next.js gives you more control over how and when your data refreshes. So I would just make sure that in your code, the images only load the first time a user goes to the page. (unless they are dynamic and should change). You wrote "However, after a while (and this is reproducible by just clicking around in the application)". So I'm not sure if you mean "I click around ten times and it breaks", or "I click around really fast 100 times and it breaks". So if user clicks on your Homepage, and images load, then they click on another page and that page and images load. Then they click on the Homepage again, the images should not have to reload, they should already be loaded. But if you have it this way so the Homepage images don't reload, but they break anyways... Just trying to figure out what exactly is happening when the app stops working. -does using v12.0.9 for a week solve the problem.

body:
@rajatdhoot123 Hello! I'm. not sure I understand your question, but you can call getServerSideProps from your page files. So yes you can call an api from getServerSideProps that runs on the server. There is nothing in the api docs that has a config that you can specify that it runs once. But you can put some caching logic so that although the function will be called many times the logic will only once; similar to how you can configure useEffect in React.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props

The other option is to just use the getStaticProps and pass 'false' to the 'revalidate' config options. This will only run the getStaticProps function once on loading. Now I think there are some caveats I don't get yet, but this code also runs server side, but is intended for data that doesn't change often.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props

Here is a good video/blog post of the differences:
https://medium.com/eincode/next-js-data-fetching-getstaticprops-vs-getserversideprops-fcbf43d0ccac

author: Milo123459

body:
Hi, haven't tested this thought yet, but I came to think of a solution you could try.

Let's assume you have one "View Component" containing all your components, both the shared ones as well as the components you wish to toggle between. This View component would take a parameter determining which layout should be used 
```
<ViewComponent initialLayout={"grid"} />
```

You would then have two page files to generate the routes you want:
- pages/items/list.tsx
- pages/items/coverflow.tsx

Each of these would return the "ViewComponent" with the appropriate "grid"/"list" as the `initialLayout` parameter value.

This will give you the basic function when routing to switch between the layouts.

Now for the good part! 
When you want to toggle between the layouts you can use shallowRouting to update the URL without actually triggering a route change, this would mean that the URL will change but the user will remain on the current page.
You can then use a local state variable to manage the actual layout when toggling between the layouts. 

The initial value of this state would be the `initialLayout` param value. This means that if the user toggles from the list to grid view the page will not re-render but the URL will update so if the user manually refreshes the page the initialLayout will be set by the correct route.

Example state:
```
const ViewComponent = ({initialLayout}:{initialLayout: "grid" | "list"}) => {
  const [layout, setLayout] = useState(initialLayout)

return ...
}
```

You can find more info about shallow routing here: https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/shallow-routing

Let me know if something wasn't understandable/confusing and I'll try my best to clarify.

Wrote this on my smartphone so might have missed something.

✌🏻😃
/Simon

My guess is that it would work, haven't had time to test this right now but fairly certain that I've made this work earlier on a similar mission ✌🏻😃

I often use this technique for pages that have some sort of search/filter components where I use shallow routing to update the current URL with the search & filter state represented as query params. This means that on first load I hydrate the search and filter state from the query params and in this way users never have to "loose" state if they accidentally refresh the search page and many of our customers also like to bookmark/save the search URL for searches they frequently visit.

Hello.

I think what you are looking for is `redirects` and not `rewrite`.

> Rewrites act as a URL proxy and mask the destination path, making it appear the user hasn't changed their location on the site. In contrast, [redirects](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/redirects) will reroute to a new page and show the URL changes.

I was able to reproduce your issue when using `rewrite` (the middleware was not triggered), but it's working fine with `redirects`

body:
## Update

With the assistance from a fellow dev in [Developer DAO](https://www.developerdao.com/), we've confirmed that the issue was that the frontend of my DApp was trying to reference the abi files that were created when I compiled my smart contracts. But because I did not commit my '_artifacts_' folder (and subsequently the .json files therein) when I pushed my code to GitHub, I essentially had to find a way to reference those abi files without pushing my artifacts folder to my repo.

## Steps
1. Created a new folder at my root directory called 'utils'
2. Next, I added 2 new .json files to this utils folder - one for each of my smart contract's corresponding abi file
3. I then copied + pasted the content in each abi file, into those new .json files I created
4. Finally, I imported those .json files under their new directory, in each JavaScript file that needed to reference my smart contracts

`import NFT from '../utils/NFT.json'`

`import Market from '../utils/NFTMarket.json'`

## Outcome

**I've managed to successfully deploy my Next.js site, and so I consider this issue resolved** 🤖

body:
Hi, thanks for reporting.

Just to understand your use case, in which scenario would you like to run the dev server in a CI environment?

Otherwise, I can see that `npm run build` will also fail as it also tries to touch that file, but I think it should be safe to assume that the `tsconfig.json` file should have been correctly configured before it reaches CI.

Next.js sets/overrides certain options for the most optimal output, and I can see no immediate changes to fix your problem, other than pushing a commit with the updated `tsconfig.json` file.

We could make the error message nicer, but in reality, it really should have been caught already in the development process.

Do you have a proposal for a fix/change?

body:
Thank you for the extra info!

Here is the reasoning of why Next.js does this currently:

> Next.js sets/overrides certain options for the most optimal output

I'll discuss with the others if we should either allow continuing the build with a warning that tells the user the output might not be optimal, or we should still crash but add a more clear error message with a link to a page that gives an actionable way to fix it.

body:
Hi,
If you which to completely disable JS, you can use `unstable_runtimeJS `

But if you need to remove some data from `__NEXT_DATA__` and reduce your page size, you will need to patch Next.js for this, here's one for next@12.1.6
You can use https://www.npmjs.com/package/patch-package to auto install the patch.

[next+12.1.6.patch](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/files/11009343/next%2B12.1.6.patch)

For this part
```js
+    delete props1.pageProps.node
+    delete props1.pageProps.i18n
+    delete props1.pageProps.menus
```
I'm removing only the props i don't wanna keep in the HTML, If you are using NextAuth for example, you want to keep `session` coming from SSR, and this may vary depending on your use case.

And finally on the _hydrate part, i just restore the props i removed.

Hope this helps.

@a7medmo7med9 From your description, my guess is that your production server goes to sleep if you don't have traffic. Which is why your custom express server works to speed up your page load. The resources serving your website go to sleep after not being used, but your express server wakes it up so that it can respond to incoming traffic immediately. 

Other problems could be how much data fetching you have in getStaticProps and getServerSideProps.

And as you build out your code at what point does the first load time go up. I understand this might not be feasible depending on the size of your app, but this line of thinking is how I would troubleshoot it.

Good luck!

revalidatePath('/(main)/post/[slug]', 'layout')
```

body:
This does indeed revalidate dynamic pages's static paths,
but it doesn't revalidate dynamic paths (`fallback: 'blocking, paths; []`),
which is what I think we're all asking for.

Currently there is no way of knowing which dynamic paths have been cached (as far as I know),
which makes revalidating them impossible.

Since we can't know which dynamic paths have been cached we would need to be able to pass either `/article/[articleId]` or `/article/*` to `unstable_revalidate`, which currently isn't supported.

body:
Thank you for this! I didn't know there were actual html files being saved for dynamic routes.
I've tried it out and it seems to work fine.

Unfortunately it seems like Next.js revalidation works by fetching the page with a special header, causing it to be re-rendered.
So for every page being revalidated a request is made, meaning that if you want to revalidate thousands of pages you will be sending thousands of requests to your server. I would much prefer it if the cache for the page is simply cleared, and the page gets re-rendered when a request comes in. With the current implementation I guess I'll have to keep using `getServerSideProps`, but thank you for the help!

body:
I have the same problem. I would expect a `trustProxy` setting that is not tied to i18n, but instead works on a global level: It should affect all `request` objects in middleware and API routes.

When running behind a reverse proxy like nginx, the `trustProxy` setting should consider all `x-forwarded-{host,port,proto}` headers and modify request URLs accordingly.

This behavior is described and already implemented by [Express.js](https://expressjs.com/en/guide/behind-proxies.html). Maybe Next.js can take some cues from their implementation.

**Next.js v12.0.9 added some urgency to this,** as [absolute URLs are enforced at a middleware/edge function level](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33410). I now need to somehow generate absolute URLs and will get errors when using relative URLs.

There are two possible workarounds:

1. Use a static `APP_BASE_URL=https://app.domain.xyz` env variable to construct absolute URLs, e.g. for middleware redirects
2. Construct absolute URLs using `x-forwarded-{host,port,proto}` inside `req.headers`

body:
Hello!

When you say:
> it takes a time to be rendered

what do you mean?

Are you using SSG, SSR or client side fetching?

The answer will be different based on what you are using.

The most straightforward approach would be to have a "skeleton" of your page with your loading animation that will be statically generated at build time.
Then, on the client, you can do all the data fetching that you need inside a simple useEffect.

Your page will return some conditional rendering like:
```
return (!data ? <YourLoading> : <YourFullComponent>)
```

author: seakkus

body:
Hi @seakkus,

In my scenario, the SSR was fast but client rendering was slow. A simple tweak in font loading did the work.

If you're running the newer Next.js versions with React 18, you can archive it using `Suspense` to *streaming* chunks of your content to the client while the component is rendering on the server side.

You can read more about it at:

- [Next.js / React 18 Streaming](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/react-18/streaming)
- [React.js / Suspense](https://reactjs.org/docs/react-api.html#reactsuspense)
- [React.js / Streaming Architechture](https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/130)

Otherwise, if you can't use React 18, you might need to split some of your fetchings at the client side to reduce the time of SSR and also use `<Suspense>` to wait for fetching operations complete at the client side.

I really recommend you consider upgrading to React 18 and newer Next.js releases because it will make your work easy-peasy.

body:
Hey folks, wanted to swing back here with an update. After digging through many different issues and discussions, I've [**made a new page in the documentation**](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy) ([PR](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/54601)) specifically for Content Security Policy and nonces. This docs page: 

- Explains how to generate a `nonce` with Middleware
- Shows how to consume the `nonce` in a route with `headers()`
- Shows a complete CSP without needing to use any `unsafe`
- Shows how to ignore the `nonce` Middleware from running on prefetches / static assets

Further, we've patched some bugs and made improvements to `nonce` handling in Next.js that will be available in the latest `canary` version (for those of you time traveling from the future, upgrade to Next.js 13.5). We also updated the `with-strict-csp` example in the `examples/` folder, which is backlinked from the [new documentation page](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy).

Really hope this helps out, thank you all 🙏 I'll be closing this discussion out. To continue the discussion, [please go here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/54907).
author: leerob

body:
Definitely agree, currently you have to exactly specify every route to be revalidated,
which just isn't feasible for dynamic routes where there might be thousands (millions?) of combinations.

For example, we have a search page hosted on Vercel which we would like to switch from SSR (getServerSideProps) to ISR (getStaticProps + `fallback: 'blocking'`).

The search page's pathname is currently `pages/search/[[...params]]`, with thousands of possible combinations.

We would love to be able to clear every combination at once, either with `res.unstable_revalidate('/search*')` or with `res.unstable_revalidate('/search/[[...params]]')`. Right now this isn't possible, which means we have to use `getServerSideProps` with an external CDN for caching, and revalidate pages using wildcard invalidations.

Are wildcard or pathname revalidations something that might be possible to implement?
If this were to be implemented we wouldn't need to use an external CDN anymore and the Vercel Edge Network would meet all of our needs!

I'm running into the same issue with a Yarn PnP monorepo setup, and it's forcing me to run `next start` on the 1.2GB builder image instead of `yarn node server.js` on the 110MB runner image. Is there any chance that this could be picked up soon?

I've managed to reduce the impact on size from 1.2GB to 660MB by doing removing `./.yarn/cache` and adding the entire cache from the build state instead.

```
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.pnp.cjs ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/packages/hq/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/packages/hq/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/packages/hq/public ./public

# Copying the entire yarn cache because of an issue with next / @vercel/nft
# https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/34600
RUN rm -rf ./.yarn/cache
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.yarn/./.yarn/
```

body:
Perhaps one option is to set up a script or run a set of commands to insert and replace the contents of a ready-made server.js file into the server.js file that gets built in the standalone directory.

```
#!/bin/bash

# Specify the source and destination file paths
source_file_path="./source_file.js"
destination_file_path="./destination_file.js"

# Read the contents of the source file
source_file_contents=$(cat "$source_file_path")

# Replace the contents of the destination file with the source file contents
echo "$source_file_contents" > "$destination_file_path"
```

body:
To get a custom server running with `output: standalone`, extra dependencies and Docker I came to the following conclusion:

1. Create your custom `server.js` like described in the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/building-your-application/configuring/custom-server)
2. Make sure your `next.config.js` has `output: 'standalone'`
3. Create your `Dockerfile` like in [the example](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-docker/Dockerfile)
4. Add lines to copy your `server.js` file (after copying the standalone output)
5. Add a line to copy `node_modules/next`
6. Add lines to copy any other required dependencies or files (like `ejs` and templates in my case)
7. Build

Alternatively, you could bundle your server file (like @sadg [mentioned](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/34599#discussioncomment-3583106)).

So the `Dockerfile` would contain something like this:
```
...

# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size
# https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

# Custom server
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/dist/server.js ./server.js
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/node_modules/next ./node_modules/next
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/node_modules/ejs ./node_modules/ejs
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/src/views ./src/views

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000
# set hostname to localhost
ENV HOSTNAME "0.0.0.0"

CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

Dupflo

body:
You can potentially use Vercel's nft library (https://github.com/vercel/nft) to get a list of all dependencies. I think they use this under the hood to trace all dependencies for standalone mode and just include what's actually used.

Create some Nodejs script that uses this library under the hood and run that Nodejs script at some point during your build process. Point the library to some entry file (maybe your custom server.js file?) and it should give you a list of all dependencies that it detected as being used by that entry file and it's dependencies. It's recursive. Then you can copy all those files into some appropriate output folder in that same Nodejs script.

I used this library on a project that had a custom server.js and used "standalone" output mode. I cannot remember all the specific details how I used it and unfortunately I don't have access to that project anymore to look, but I remember using it at some point in the build process to get all proper dependencies into the project output folders. Wish I could help you more!

body:
I find a call to `tsc --project <my-project>.json && node ./install.js ./dist/<my-server-file>.js ./dist` as part of the build to be simple, but effective. It first transpiles the TypeScript into JavaScript (with `tsc` - if you are using TypeScript) and _then_ it adds dependencies with `node ./install.js` into `./dist` (i.e. copies used modules into `./dist/node_modules`).

If anything is missing in the target `./dist/node_modules`, you can call for example `node ./install.js ./node_modules/extensionless/src/register.js ./dist` (to include the [extensionless](https://www.npmjs.com/package/extensionless) module, which I use for startup to work with the JavaScript imports without file extensions and call Node.js as `node --import=extensionless/register <my-server-file>.js`).

author: thibautsabot

body:
Hello.

I don't believe there is an "automatic" way of doing this.

As you mentioned, you can programmatically force an update.
You could periodically check the version of a user (by storing it in a cookie or something on the browser) and refresh the page when you feel you need to push an update.

That being said, interfering with the browser behaviour is often considered bad UX, so I'd only use this under specific circonstances.
author: thibautsabot

body:
Hi, this is because Middleware is run in the Edge Runtime, which is closer to the Browser environment in terms of APIs. (since browsers are following the standard Web APIs)

This is mentioned here:
https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime#unsupported-apis

We are working on making this clear.

But to note, your Middleware does **not** actually run in the browser.

body:
Hello.

I'd say it depends on how often your redirects change and how you want to handle them.

It they are likely to stay the same you could use them in `next.config.js` (see: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/redirects).
Note that there are created at build time. You'll need to re-build if you want to update them.

If they change a **lot**, then getting them in a middleware could make sense, but as you pointed out, they'll be fetched on every request, which is far from ideal 😕  .
author: thibautsabot

body: The solution is to fetch and cache redirects in a custom API endpoint and call the API from within the middleware (you get access to cached and updatable redirects). The problem is that fetching data from the API endpoint from within the middleware works in dev but after building the app it throws the ECONNREFUSED error. I try to solve that problem for few days now, help would be appreciated :)

body:
Hi, I had the same problem and I found the solution here: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/compiler#modularize-imports
I realized MUI imports were the problem. Specially the default imports with "@mui/material", that I used in the whole app.
When I applied modularized imports to MUI, the compiling time reduced a lot. I saw there is also a babel plugin that works the same. Hope you find it helpful.

body:
Hello.

Did you check out shallow routing? https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/shallow-routing

> Shallow routing allows you to change the URL without running data fetching methods again, that includes [getServerSideProps](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props), [getStaticProps](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props), and [getInitialProps](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-initial-props).

author: parlay96

body:
Shallow routing only works for same page URL changes. For example, let's assume we have another page called pages/about.js, and you run this:

router.push('/?counter=10', '/about?counter=10', { shallow: true })
Since that's a new page, it'll unload the current page, load the new one and wait for data fetching even though we asked to do shallow routing.

body:
I ended up going the functional way (if that's the right way to describe it):
In a specific file:
- Use "let" to declare your empty variables and methods 
- Instead of creating a class instance, call a "setup" function, as if it was the constructor of that class
- Set the value to the variables and methods above. 
- Instead of this, simply call the methods and variables.
- Export the methods/variables you need to access from other files.

body:
Hi,
You will need to patch Next.js for this, here's one for next@12.1.6
You can use https://www.npmjs.com/package/patch-package to auto install the patch.

[next+12.1.6.patch](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/files/11009343/next%2B12.1.6.patch)

For this part
```js
+    delete props1.pageProps.node
+    delete props1.pageProps.i18n
+    delete props1.pageProps.menus
```
I'm removing only the props i don't wanna keep in the HTML, If you are using NextAuth for example, you want to keep `session` coming from SSR, and this may vary depending on your use case.

And finally on the _hydrate part, i just restore the props i removed.

Hope this helps.

body:
Three solutions for anyone having the same issue:
1. Try updating `next` and `eslint-config-next` to `12.1.1-canary.3`.

2. If the previous one doesn't help you, try adding the following to your `package.json` (You need yarn for this to work):
```
"resolutions": {
    "eslint-plugin-react": "7.28.0"
}
```

3. If the above two doesn't work for you you can [ignore the eslint errors during builds](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/ignoring-eslint)

body:
Hello.

Yes! SWC is a great improvement from babel.
See: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/compiler#why-swc and https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12#faster-builds-and-fast-refresh-with-rust-compiler

Your build will be must faster and expect more coming to SWC in the following months.
SWC is now a big focus for the Next.js team!

`Link` from `next/link` will only prefetch pages inside your application (client-side transition).
It won't work for **external** domains!

Suggested documentations: https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/introduction and https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link

body:
I'm afraid it won't work with subdomains 😕.

As you can see here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L223
the router will check if the origin of the request is the same as the destination.

In your case, the `locationOrigin` will be "http://example.com" but the `resolved.origin` is "http://abc.example.com".

Next.js will determine that this is **not** a "local" URL and won't prefetch it: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/link.tsx#L267

Hello.

`outputStandalone` is experimental because it is a fairly new feature that may have breaking changes in the upcoming releases.
It will stop being experimental when the Next.js team think it is ready for wider adoption.
It has been introduced by this PR: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/31003

`with-docker` added this flag two weeks ago with: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/34083.
You can find more context in the PR description.

body:
It's not really an alternative though...even directly on the environment variables page it states:
>Note: In order to keep server-only secrets safe, environment variables are evaluated at build time, so only environment variables actually used will be included. This means that process.env is not a standard JavaScript object, so you’re not able to use [object destructuring](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Destructuring_assignment). Environment variables must be referenced as e.g. process.env.PUBLISHABLE_KEY, not const { PUBLISHABLE_KEY } = process.env.

So it's still not useful for runtime configuration.

body:
I wasted some time on this as well. Sometimes it baffles me how long issues with interest get ignored... My workaround was to add the .env files to .dockerignore and create a custom docker-entrypoint.sh

```shell
#!/bin/sh
printenv > /app/.env.production
exec "$@"
```

and modifying the Dockerfile to use the entrypoint like so 

```
ENTRYPOINT [ "/app/docker-entrypoint.sh" ]
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```
