body:
my 2 cents hack in dockerfile.

```
RUN sed -i 's/"publicRuntimeConfig":{"processEnv":{}}/"publicRuntimeConfig": { "processEnv": Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(process.env).filter(([key]) => key.startsWith("NEXT_PUBLIC_"))) }/' /app/server.js
```

body:
Hey everyone – we have just published new documentation on self-hosting, including how to configure a custom cache handler for use with ISR / Data Cache in both the Pages and App Router. These docs go in depth about how other features like Image Optimization, Middleware, Environment Variables, Build Caching, and more work when self-hosted. There's also updated Docker based examples.

Specific to this discussion, we have provided guidance on how to use runtime environment variables.

https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying
author: leerob

body: Tl;Dr; Next.js no longer recommends using the [runtimeConfig](https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/api-reference/next-config-js/runtime-configuration) option, as this does not work with the standalone output mode. Instead, Next.js recommends [incrementally adopting](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/upgrading/app-router-migration) the App Router and using `process.env.MY_VARIABLE` directly.

It depends if it's a secret value, as the output of `getServerSideProps` is serialized and sent to the component. Versus with the App Router, since it's a Server Component, the rendering can remain entirely on the server.

body:
> ### Maybe a solution.
> * Default option in next.js for removeViewBox is true. If you change in .svg height or width the view-box from .svg will be rest to default value.
> * If you use in your next.config.js webpack and set removeViewBox to false .svg resize start work.
> * A working example: [stackblitz-resize-svg-next.js](https://stackblitz.com/edit/nextjs-mbppty?file=next.config.js)
> 
> ```js
>  webpack(config) {
>     config.module.rules.push({
>       loader: '@svgr/webpack',
>       options: {
>         prettier: false,
>         svgo: true,
>         svgoConfig: {
>           plugins: [
>             {
>               name: 'preset-default',
>               params: {
>                 overrides: { removeViewBox: false },
>               },
>             },
>           ],
>         },
>         titleProp: true,
>       },
>       test: /\.svg$/,
>     });
> 
>     return config;
>   }
> ...
> ```
> 
> * **If you have in .babelrc set "inline-react-svg" this solution will not work.**

Thanks a lot... This works for me...

author: habovh

body: Had a look at this and discussed with @habovh, their app is taking between 15 to 40 seconds per `.scss` file, nearly all of that time is spent on sass-loader which just runs `sass` or `node-sass` depending on which is installed. They're using `prependData` which includes some files but it's unclear why it's that slow to compile the files. It is clear however that this is  an issue with the app and not something we can change in Next.js to make this particular app compile faster. I'll convert this to discussions 👍
author: timneutkens

body:
Hi @timneutkens, following up the ultra long build time issue here. I managed to track down the root cause and actually address it.

After "mocking" the `prependData` file using empty mixins instead and imports, the build time went from 30 minutes to a little bit over 3 minutes.

I gradually added back our actual mixins and the culprit for ultra long build times was an import of some [Foundation](https://get.foundation/index.html) mixins files that in turn imported a whole lot of other `.scss` files.

Compiling the original `prependData` file using either `node-sass` or (dart) `sass` was fairly quick when run standalone (~1.5 seconds), but I guess that since `prependData` must be compiled again with every `*.module.scss` file, it lead to a lot of concurrent fs open and read files, eventually slowing things wayyyyyy down and maxing out IO capabilities.

Now our app builds completely, linting and everything, in **175.08s (2 minutes 55 seconds)**. That's a decrease of **over 90%** of the whole build time. I'm still in shock btw.

Thank you so much for your help and for telling me about the trace-to-tree tool that I didn't know existed. I must've missed something in the docs or didn't pay enough attention, but this tool is definitely a gem.

To anyone encountering performance issues during build time, I strongly suggest you have a look at [this script](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/scripts/trace-to-tree.mjs) included in the Next.js repo.

Here goes to another day loving Next.js so much!
author: habovh

body: @habovh how were you using that trace-to-tree tool? I'm wondering if you run it independently or you run `next build` w/ some sort of flag.
author: Miikis

body: Remove your `.next` folder to remove any kind of caching, then run the next command you wish to debug. This will create a trace file in the `.next` directory. Then use the trace to tree tool from the Next.js repo to analyze it!

author: jayantbh

body:
i managed to get it running locally by:
1. cloning https://github.com/vercel/next.js locally
2. using npnpm to install all packages
3. building next.js via `npm run build`
4. running the trace via `node scripts/trace-to-tree.mjs <<file_name>>`

author: Wassap124

body:
Hi, All you have to do next is: 1. Add scripts: ``` "build": "next build", "dev": "next dev", "start": "next start", ``` 2. Touch `tsconfig.json` 3. Install types for React, Node, and install TypeScript itself ```shell npm install --save-dev typescript @types/react @types/node ``` 4. Make sure to have something like `src/pages/index.tsx` ```tsx export const Home = () =>
Home
; export default Home; ``` 5. `npm run dev` - and you ought to be done. ```shell ➜ npm run dev > test-ts-upgrade@1.0.0 dev > next dev ready - started server on 0.0.0.0:3000, url: http://localhost:3000 We detected TypeScript in your project and created a tsconfig.json file for you. event - compiled client and server successfully in 2s (110 modules) ```

body:
https://vercel.com/guides/is-sqlite-supported-in-vercel

> SQLite needs a local file system on the server to store the data permanently when write requests are made. In a serverless environment, this central single permanent storage is not available because storage is ephemeral with serverless functions. As a function receives more concurrent traffic, the serverless environment will create new instances of the function and each instance will not be able to share the same storage.

author: benkingcode
icyJoseph

body:
This is an exact usecase we're looking for. On our platform, "friendlyName" is user generated content, and the pages/content/title are able to be changed. The permanent link is based on the ID, with the friendlyName being the latest/correct version (which we redirect to).

However, any UTM params or other query params are lost on this redirect as mentioned above and we need a way to keep them. API routes or middleware essentially removes the caching feature and we might as well do getServerSideProps instead unless static + keeping params is able to work.
author: J-Gonzalez

body:
@benkingcode - Curious to see your PR for this. It's also a critical blocker for us - with the alternative being we use getServerSideProps and forget ISR/SSR

The idea and approach seems simple enough. The getStaticProps function doesn't need to "know" and do logic on the query params (which wouldn't be possible as it's pre-generated), it just needs to append them to the redirect (which it should fully have access to the full path anyways)

This architecture is excellent if your company must manage multiple JavaScript applications that may share some common code.

:man_technologist:  Demo here: https://github.com/VulcanJS/vulcan-npm

I can help you moving your company to a similar Monorepo architecture (among other things :)).

My hourly rate: 120$. I am located in France and can work in French or English speaking environments.

You can reach me out via mail: eb@lbke.fr

Looking forward to working with you!

Location: France
Remote: Yes
Willing to relocate: No (I am a freelance)
Technologies: Next.js, Remix, GraphQL, React, Python, Electron
Resume/CV: My publications: https://medium.com/@eric.burel ; my GitHub with OSS projects:  https://github.com/eric-burel and my previous freelance experiences (in French :fr:) https://www.lbke.fr/eric-burel
Website: https://www.lbke.fr
Email: eb@lbke.fr

body:
**[Ought](https://ought.org/)** | **Frontend engineer** | **Remote, or San Francisco** | Full-time | Competitive salary | [Link](https://ought.org/careers/frontend-engineer)

We are using artificial intelligence to help researchers be more effective. We think AI is the keystone technology which will accelerate scientific progress, but we're also profoundly concerned by the risks transformative AI brings with it.

Out current focus – a GPT-3 powered research assistant named [Elicit](https://elicit.org/) – is our proposal for how to capture the upside of artificial intelligence, while building the system in such a way as to mitigate the risks. [Quick demo](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rjOHcfU-XvA).

This is an extremely challenging frontend problem, because our goal is to distil insights from hundreds of millions of research papers into a straightforward interface 🙃 . We need excellent engineers to help with this! Design sense is a big plus (although we do have a designer on the team too); ML experience is not required.

body:
[Glossi](https://www.glossi.io) | Senior Frontend Developer | Remote | Full-time | Competitive salary | USA Only

# Our Company
Glossi has set out to empower everyone, from amateurs to professionals, entrepreneurs to big brands, to create gorgeous photos and videos of their products in a fraction of the time it traditionally takes (weeks to minutes). We’re building a creative studio-in-a-box that dramatically simplifies the 3D rendering content creation process, and even makes it fun! Think Canva, but 3D. We are creating content for everyone from Kickstarters to Fortune 500 companies. With our 3D model management system, virtual photo studio, and video templates, we eliminate the need for agencies, freelancers, and the outsourcing of work which enables our clients to create everything they need to sell across socials, storefronts, and more. Come join us in defining the future of visual marketing!

# Our Squad
- We enjoy using the latest Saas, libaries, and frameworks where it makes sense
- We have a collaborative work environment where we brainstorm and plan together
- We help each other accomplish our team and company goals
- Growing each team member's career goals and aspirations is important to us
- It is important to us to have a laid back culture
- Feedback is always welcome at all stages of the SDLC

# Responsibilities
- You will build and maintain beautiful, snappy, and native-feeling UI experiences across all of our product teams from video creation to enterprise content management features.
- You will optimize and set new standards for application-level tooling to measure product stability and speed.
- You will build product features end-to-end: through conception, exploration, iteration, maintenance, and polish.
- You will own the features that you build and become the domain expert on those areas within the organization in time.
- You will help collaborate and guide feature sets with UI/UX and Glossi leadership.
- You will help with interviewing other talented engineers that want to join Glossi.
- Manage a high output team through mentorship and teaching other developers.
- Identify ways to improve and guide company planning and design decisions.
- E2E ownership and resolution of bugs
- Clearly communicate with peers and drive feedback leadership team
	- Reviews
	- Documentation
	- Looms & Recordings
	- Presentations & Demos
- Receive and give constructive feedback based on work
- Step into leadership roles as needed (take over)
- Participate in the design process and identify the cost of implementation and workarounds
- 1v1 Mentorship with the leadership team 

# Business Highlights
**Our founders are experts in 3D and advertising**  
Founders are ex leadership of leading advertising and VR agencies in Los Angeles, doing work for Marvel, Disney, Riot Games, and more

**We’ve found product-market fit with  $14Ok+ in revenue**  
User base spans single product start-ups, multi-product businesses, and agencies.

**Our target market is $4B+ and growing**  
Every business selling a product online needs video  
to stand out and convert.

# If this sounds like a great fit
I’m on the founding team of Glossi ([glossi.io](http://glossi.io/)), a start-up using real-time rendering to create a cloud-based photo/video studio. Our software is built for entrepreneurs to e-commerce brands, blending game engine tech (unreal engine) with a native web experience to create a new category of product. We currently have a solid stable of customers - Epson, Playstation, Marshall, and more - with many more in the pipeline.

Please reach out to me: 
thomas.underwood@glossi.io

### About

WDS is one of the oldest WordPress agencies out there and a [VIP Gold Partner.](https://wpvip.com/) Lately, we've been shipping headless websites powered by WordPress and Next.js. The engineering department is inclusive, supportive, and fosters an environment of growth. 

**Benefits**

- Work from anywhere, in any time zone
- Generous PTO: 15 days per year + all US Federal Holidays
- 5 days paid sick leave per year
- Flexible work schedule
- 100% paid medical for single employees (US only)
- 401K + employer match (US only)
- Continuing education opportunities via LinkedIn Learning
- Parental leave

### The Role

You'll be collaborating with an amazing project team lead by a Project Manager and Lead Engineer as they partner with enterprise level clients like [Microsoft](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/business/security-insider/), [Monster.com](https://hiring.monster.com/resources/blog/), and the [NBA](https://thebal.com) to build and maintain websites. You'll also have monthly 1:1's with the Engineering Manager and Employee Experience Coordinator, Quarterly-Check-ins, the opportunity to hang out on our weekly engineering scrums, plus paid training opportunities. 

If you're interested, check out our careers page! If you have any questions, feel free email me at: greg@webdevstudios.com

Thank you! 🍻

[RMRK](https://www.rmrk.app) | Front-end/Full stack engineers | Remote | Full-time | Competitive salary plus bonuses | [Link](https://github.com/rmrk-team/rmrk-web/wiki/RMRK-Jobs)

RMRK is a next-generation NFT protocol of composable, evolvable, forward-compatible, multi-chain NFTs. Founded in August of 2020, the team was formally created in February 2021 and has released several platforms and products since. Notably, [Singular](https://singular.rmrk.app/), an NFT marketplace and [Kanaria](https://kanaria.rmrk.app/) an RMRK 2.0 product demo of RMRK 2.0 technology, together facilitate over 3 million USD in volume per month.

RMRK's goal is to build the infrastructure to build awesome UX-centric things. Thus, the RMRK Association is the steward and main developer of the open-source RMRK specification, and its chief user.

Ultimately, we want to see NFTs go far beyond static media, and RMRK is about making that a reality. Come re-learn everything you know about NFTs with us!

learn more about RMRK [here](https://url.rmrk.app/rmrkcc) (video) or [here](https://url.rmrk.app/dawn) (text)
learn more about Kanaria [here](https://url.rmrk.app/kanariacc) (video) or [here](https://url.rmrk.app/demobird) (text)

We are looking for a Full-Stack Typescript developer, with strong front-end skills. Namely React/Typescript/Next.js

About you
Ideally, you will have...

4+ years experience working with Typescript and React.
Experience working with Next.js and Node.js.
Experience in building high-performance web applications.
CSS best practices and CSS in JS approach (Chakra-UI, Fela, Glamor, etc).
Excellent communication skills, solid work ethic, and a strong desire to write efficient, scalable, and clean code.
Experience working in a high-paced environment.
Nice to have

Graphql/Apollo
PostgreSQL
Familiarity with Polkadot stack (Substrate, Polkadot blockchain)
Polkadot JS API
Solidity / Rust / Ink!
Additional info

Apply via [jobs@rmrk.app](mailto:jobs@rmrk.app)

### **[Axiom](https://axiom.co)** | Next.js/Typescript Engineer | Remote - US (Eastern), Europe, United Kingdom) | Full-time | [Apply](https://www.axiom.co/careers/5977017002)

**About Axiom**

Axiom’s mission is to empower developers to get the best insights into their data, as fast as possible. We are a remote-first and globally distributed team building a cloud native, serverless data analytics platform. Axiom completely changes the way in which developers and organizations think about their data: they can now send unlimited data with cost-effective storage and lightning-fast querying.

**About the Role**

We are seeking a Next.js/Typescript engineer to join our growing team to build an incredible observability experience for Next.js users. You will be designing and developing open source libraries that provide a fully integrated experience for Next.js users and Axiom.

**What You Will do**

- Design and implement robust and testable Typescript libraries
- Work closely with product and design teams to define the scope and scale of upcoming features
- Develop and maintain open source libraries
- Engage with Next.js developers from across the globe
- Troubleshoot, bug fix, and improve existing code
- Identify bottlenecks and increase the efficiency of software
- Communicate technical design decisions with the wider team
- Publish and maintain packages on npmjs

**Must-Have Qualifications** 

- 3+ years experience with Javascript, Typescript, and React
- Experience of the Next.js ecosystem
- Experience building complex libraries
- Experience designing and communicating technical architecture
- Experience with debugging and profiling tools for javascript code

**Bonus Qualifications**

- Experience building Next.js/React libraries in open source
- Experience publishing and maintaining public javascript packages
- Experience with observability/monitoring tools
- Experience working with globally distributed and remote teams


Feel free to [apply here](https://www.axiom.co/careers/5977017002)!

For further questions please reach out to me:
[seif@axiom.co](mailto:seif@axiom.co)

body:
[Made In](https://madeincookware.com) | Senior Full-Stack Engineer | Remote (Continental US) | Full-Time | Competitive Salary + Benefits | [Apply Here](https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/2967860036/)

Made In is the leader in the digitally-native kitchen space. We bring Chef expertise and centuries-old cookware manufacturing techniques to craft professional-quality kitchen tools for the home and professional cook. We are backed by some of the best chefs in the world and have been featured in publications and shows such as the TODAY Show, NY Times, Fast Company, Business Insider, and more.

We’re seeking a motivated Senior Full Stack Engineer who is committed to helping us build a best-in-class eCommerce experience. In 2021 we moved to a cutting-edge headless architecture, and this year we have big plans to scale and enhance our new fully-owned NextJS frontend. We’re looking for generalist web developers with deep roots in the browser, who thrive in an atmosphere that’s nimble and ambitious. If you have natural initiative, curiosity, some hustle, and a sense of humor, then you might be a great fit at Made In.

In this role you will work regularly with our product team and designer, as well as fellow engineers. You’ll be the second most-senior technical resource on the team, and will report directly to our Lead Engineer.

## Responsibilities:

* First and foremost, build and ship user-facing features on our core NextJS eCommerce site
* Contribute to backend / infra projects to support the frontend
* Provide ongoing code review and mentorship to other engineers
* Collaborate with product / design resources on project status, concerns, and roadblocks
* Surface bugs, elevate best practices, and opportunities for improvement as they appear
* Contribute to the growing engineering team culture at Made In

## About You:

* You have 4+ years in frontend or full stack web development
* You can demonstrate career progression to a senior level of contribution
* You have a broad functional understanding of web fundamentals such as Javascript development, browser APIs, debugging and dev tools, CSS, performance, etc.
* You are able to communicate and collaborate effectively – with technical and non-technical parties
* You can operate at different zoom levels – can think holistically about the big picture, and can also attend to fine-grained implementation details
* You are interested and able to function in a fast-moving startup environment – you can wear multiple hats and adapt to roadmap changes

## Preferred (Bonus) Experience:

* Server-side rendering technologies e.g. Gatsby, Next, etc.
* Ecommerce platforms, specifically Shopify
* Content Management Systems like Sanity or Contentful
* Google Tag Manager and third party data platforms

## Benefits:

- Remote eligible*
- Competitive salary
- Equity grant
- Healthcare, Dental, and Vision
- Flexible vacation policy
- Generous employee discount

*Made In headquarters is based in Austin, TX. This position is remote eligible within the continental United States.

Must be eligible to work in the United States. We are unable to provide Visa sponsorship for employment at this time.

Made In is an equal opportunity employer. We celebrate diversity and are committed to creating an inclusive environment for all employees.

author: thibautsabot

body:
Hello!

While using React or even Next.js in all projects is possible, ask yourself "is this the right tool for the job?".

Do you need a Single Page Application? Are you reusing components a lot? Is your website complex?
If the answer to these questions are "no", you might want to consider Vanilla JavaScript.

If "yes", do you need SSR, code-splitting, ISR, Image optimization, API routes, etc..
In this case, Next.js could be a good fit for you.

You **can** use Next.js for everything, it's an amazing framework and works like a charm in a lot of situation.
But do you **need** it? That'll greatly depend on your project 🙂.
author: thibautsabot

body:
This is likely because you are either not using the import or using it in the page component. 

Next.js will try to eliminate imports from the client bundle that is used in data fetching methods like `getStaticProps` etc.

Otherwise, it will fail the build as `fs` is not an API in the browser. Make sure you use the import, and that it's only used in the data fetching methods.

You can test this on this page: https://next-code-elimination.vercel.app/

This is described on each data fetching method, example: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props

body:
@AkifumiSato Thanks for the idx feature it is very useful one, but can it be persisted in the session storage? as of now if the user refresh the page it will be rested to 0

#### Case Study
A user open the login page and enter his email, click continue and then he redirected to the otp page. If he decided to return back to the login screen we need his email to be persist. He can use  browser back or button with router.back(). 

#### Implementation
The idx was a saver for me.  I used it to build a full navigation history array in the session storage. The idea behind that is to keep some state for the current page based on the uid of the page in the history stack. So once the user navigate back to it, I can show the page with its state where he left it. 

I had a useEffect in the _app to listen to the next router routeChangeComplete to maintain an array of the visited pages with its state if any in the session storage. Using that I was able to push/pop items from my state array based on the idx value as unique key.

Saving that state based only on the url the value and not based on the idx, the persist value will be shown to the user whenever he navigate to login page until we clear it based on some event like a clear button. 

The problem is once the user refresh the page the idx counter reset to 0 so the whole structure is collapsed.

author: AkifumiSato

body:
Hi,

This is a problem with how `fetch` works, which, I assume, is what you are using.

Simply put, the browser won't do `document` follow on a redirect originated from a fetch request. It will make the call though, so it is possible to be redirected to get more data, as far as I understand, but not to change the current document.

You can see this in Chrome Network tab, the type of your request is `fetch/Redirect`, and those won't do the trick. Why? Well if any script had the ability to just change the document like that, it'd be chaos. 

I think perhaps the docs, you point to, could use some more clarity, though I think it is ok, https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/response-helpers#redirects-to-a-specified-path-or-url.

> Taking a form as an example, you may want to redirect your client to a specified path or URL once they have submitted the form.

So there's the solution, do this (you could change the method to `GET` too):

```jsx
 <form method="POST" action="/api/redirect-endpoint">
        <button type="submit">Redirect</button>
 </form>
```

Further reading:

- https://stackoverflow.com/a/67479058
- https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/2021/fetch-and-3xx-redirect-status-codes/

body:
Right, I see, this has been mixed expectations. 

Scrolling to `some-id` on a url like `https://example.com/#some-id`, has been in browsers for a very long time, and it has not much to do with `scroll-behavior`. This CSS property will make it, well, smooth, as opposed to the sort of immediate scroll downward/upward to the link.

I mean to say that this has nothing to do with Link. The nice thing about using Link is that the entire page doesn't have to navigate to the new URL, instead you get the SPA behavior.

Here's a little video I made showing the behavior, [link](https://webm.red/I4IA).

Strange, but yeah, hopefully you get it working later on.

author: marko-hologram

body:
Hey!

Did you check Incremental Static Regeneration? https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration
(And its [section on error handling](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration#error-handling-and-revalidation))

With it, you can keep using `getStaticProps` but "revalidate" its content every few seconds / minutes.

> Next.js triggers a regeneration of the page in the background.

(after the time window you set is expired)

> Once the page generates successfully, Next.js will invalidate the cache and show the updated page. If the background regeneration fails, the old page would still be unaltered. 

This way, you would get a static, cached page that will be mostly always up to date.
If for some reason, the generation of the page fails, you'd still get the old working one.

body:
Recently, I encountered a similar problem, but I solved it with a native script.

It seems to work.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/53572196/177237846-4f546358-84a5-44dd-affe-68a3815c25ab.png)


```js
import { Html, Head, Main, NextScript } from 'next/document'
import React from 'react'

export default function Document() {
  return (
    <Html>
      <Head></Head>
      <body>
        <Main />
        <NextScript />
        <script
          async
          type="module"
          src="https://unpkg.com/ionicons@5.5.2/dist/ionicons/ionicons.esm.js"
        />
      </body>
    </Html>
  )
}

```

body:
> You shouldn't need a native script in `_document` as you are doing. A simple `next/script` should be able to take care of this. For instance:
> 
> ```tsx
> import "../styles/globals.css";
> import type { AppProps } from "next/app";
> import Script from "next/script";
> 
> function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) {
>   return (
>     <>
>       <Script
>         async
>         src="https://unpkg.com/ionicons@5.5.2/dist/ionicons/ionicons.esm.js"
>         type="module"
>       />
> 
>       <Component {...pageProps} />
>     </>
>   );
> }
> 
> export default MyApp;
> ```

Hi @icyJoseph 
We cant use any next script in _document file??
author: tofuwu-dev

body:
> ### [beforeInteractive](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/script#beforeinteractive)
> Scripts that load with the beforeInteractive strategy are injected into the initial HTML from the server and run before self-bundled JavaScript is executed. This strategy should be used for any critical scripts that need to be fetched and executed before any page becomes interactive. This strategy only works inside _document.js and is designed to load scripts that are needed by the entire site (i.e. the script will load when any page in the application has been loaded server-side).

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/script#beforeinteractive

> We cant use any next script in _document file??

I didn't say that, `beforeInteractive` scripts ought to go there, `_document`. If you put a `next/script` without strategy in the `_document` file, it won't work.

If you specify `beforeInteractive` then this is loaded as part of the critical chain of resources.

If you specify `afterInteractive`, or `lazyOnload` (even though strategy is `afterInteractive` by default), then it will load, but the `onLoad` callback won't happen because it is inside `_document`.

So, why put those in `_document` if not absolutely necessary, `beforeInteractive`.

At least this is state of things at 12.2.0, let's see how this evolves together with concurrent rendering.

body: # Couple notes after some testing ## TL; DR: When I use "afterInteractive" strategy and place my component in some other component besides _document it gets evaluated. Otherwise it doesn't. "beforeInteractive" strategy seems to cause my Script element not to render at all because there is no HeadManagerContext defined at build time. Any ideas why placing my component in _document would cause the "afterInteractive" effect not to fire in script.js at runtime (breakpoint at line 185 in image below)? In fact the Script component never runs a render cycle at all (let alone the useEffect for the "afterInteractive" strategy). This is not the case when placing the component somewhere besides _document. Furthermore, should I not be able to use "beforeInteractive" strategy? ## Details I used the following trivial Script definition to test with: ``` ``` I ran my app with the element above using different strategies and placing it at different locations in the app (in/outside of _document) and got different results. ### Reference image for breakpoints (script.js in next library): ### Note I use "_document.page.tsx" instead of "_document.tsx" naming scheme (*.page.tsx for all my pages) ### Tests Conditions: * Place element in _document * Use beforeInteractive strategy using next export or next dev * Build time (unexpected result occurs): * Expected result: _headManagerContext.HeadManagerContext is defined and one of lines 196, 206, or 208 would be hit. * Actual result: _headManagerContext.HeadManagerContext is undefined and none of the lines are hit, resulting in a null script (breakpoint at line 211 hit) * Run time result (unexpected result occurs): * Expected result: Script is rendered as part of the raw page and debugger; statement inside Script element breaks. * Actual result: Script is not rendered at all. * Use afterInteractive strategy (next dev or next export) * Build time (expected result occurs): Breakpoint at 184 is hit at build time. This seems expected since its "afterInteractive" strategy nothing would get evaluated at build time. * Run time (unexpected result occurs): * Expected result: Breakpoint at line 185 hits because the effect fires. * Actual result: Breakpoint at line 185 never hits, and in fact no render cycle happens for the Script component at all (breakpoint at line 181 is never hit either) * Place element in some other top level component (not _app or _document). Forced to use "afterInteractive" because "beforeInteractive" is intended for SSR/build time. Using next dev or next export. * Build time (expected result occurs): Same result as having the element in _document. (described above) * Run time (expected result occurs): Breakpoint at line 185 hits because the effect fires. (The same expected result of placing the element in _document at runtime described above). ### Summary Unexpecteds: * Placing the element in _document w/ beforeInteractive strategy has unexpected results for both run time and build time. * Placing the element in _document w/ afterInteractive strategy has **un**expected results for run time. Can anyone speak to these unexpected results? I.e. for bullet point 1: Is it expected that HeadManagerContext always be undefined when running next dev or next export? I faced the same problem with version `12.2.1`, my second script was not even rendered on the page.
What solved my issue was to set `strategy="lazyOnload"`.

```jsx
// _document.tsx
<Html>
  <Head>
    <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="any" />
  </Head>
  <body>
    <Main />
    <NextScript />

    <Script id="onload-script" strategy="afterInteractive">
      {`console.log('Hello');`}
    </Script>

    <Script
      id="analytics-script"
      src="https://static.analytics.com/101375946.js"
      strategy="lazyOnload"
    />
  </body>
 </Html>
```

body:
Nested `a` elements are forbidden in both HTML4 and HTML5 and you should avoid them anyway.

HTML4

> Links and anchors defined by the A element must not be nested; an A element must not contain any other A elements.

https://www.w3.org/TR/html401/struct/links.html#h-12.2.2

HTML5
> The [a](https://www.w3.org/TR/2011/WD-html5-author-20110809/the-a-element.html#the-a-element) element may be wrapped around entire paragraphs, lists, tables, and so forth, even entire sections, so long as there is no interactive content within (e.g. buttons or other links).

https://www.w3.org/TR/2011/WD-html5-author-20110809/the-a-element.html

author: damMota

body:
@damMota - you can run it fine on a bare metal server, VM's, cloud instances. The biggest thing is using a web server in front of it to act as a reverse proxy so the production server itself doesn't have to deal with outside connections directly. Nginx is a really common example, but also Apache, IIS, and others would work just fine too.

The basic flow is then:

[internal web traffic] -> [web server] (determines where to route request, deal with HTTPS as needed, etc) -> local Next server

I'm not 100% sure about configuring it so it's purely internal network traffic, but I would guess the companies firewall and the web server you choose can restrict it at that point.

body:
Only solution I have been able to come up with myself, is to use regex to avoid re-rewriting URLs that already start with /hotels/:

```
{
  source: '/:country((?!hotels).*)/:market/',
  destination: '/hotels/listing/?country=:country&market=:market&rule=13&rule13=true'
}
```

body:
Using `create-next-app` with an example will just get files from the examples folder inside the repository.

For `with-tailwindcss`, it'll fetch this folder: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-tailwindcss

As you can see, there is a tsconfig.json and the files are in TS.

There is another example in JS called `with-tailwindcss-emotion` ([link](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-tailwindcss-emotion)).

Adding `--typescript` to the command line will install more dependencies ([see here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/create-next-app/create-app.ts#L235)), but the other way around is not possible (removing TS from an example).

I may not have been clear enough on the issue.

But if one wants to use `SWC`, which one should,  removing `babel.config` file will enable it. 

But then `eslint` will not work, as the `@babel/eslint-parser` will not find any `babel.config`.

body: NextJS Image component is in nutshell, a TRASH
author: thawancomt

body:
ha, it's not *that* bad — but the DX is rough, no argument there. The part that bit me hardest is `sizes`: if you don't set it, Next just assumes `100vw`, so even a tiny 64px avatar can end up shipping a huge image. And writing `sizes` by hand means maintaining media-query strings that just duplicate what your CSS already says.

I got tired of that and built [tw-next-image](https://github.com/PaulRBerg/tw-next-image) — it parses the Tailwind classes you already put on the image and infers the correct `sizes` for you:

```tsx
<SmartImage src="/avatar.webp" className="size-25 lg:size-30" />
// → sizes="(min-width: 1024px) 120px, 100px"
```

It won't solve everything in this thread (art direction / different images per breakpoint still needs `<picture>`), but it kills the most tedious part of using `next/image` in a responsive Tailwind layout.
author: PaulRBerg

body:
Hello.

`getStaticProps` works on a **page**, **not** a component.
Since it is generated at build time, Next.js needs to know in advance where it'll be used.

A component, by definition, could be used anywhere, making it impossible to predict.
See: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props

In the future, you might be able to do so thanks to [React Server Components](https://vercel.com/blog/everything-about-react-server-components) but it is still in experimental stage at this point.
author: thibautsabot

body: I understand the purpose on not using getStaticProps on a component, I do think it would be wise to make components that can be cacheable that vary on the input parameters.  Imagine having a list component that has options returned from the server.  This component could be used on many places.  It would be nice to be able to cache that component based on the predefined list instead of having every page needing to potentially make the call to get the data to pass it down.  Instead, it would be that when you include the component any page, you can render out the final version of the other component, then when you go to load a new page that also uses it, the server already has that data and can immediately output the component as being cached to the front-end.

author: thibautsabot

body:
This is a probably a separate issue, but it seems revalidation will randomly take >10s to complete simple DB queries (eg finding 6 MongoDB documents).

I've decided to stop using the revalidate feature for now and instead use getServerSideProps for my leaderboard page. It takes a bit longer to load the page but at least it's consistent.
author: sspenst

body:
I don't think revalidate is necessary to do this, nor do I think it is the use case for it, or least it shouldn't be used alone.

There's no guarantee on the duration and result of a revalidate option, and trying to build a UI that relies on the revalidate process being done "quick", does feel problematic. For example, the CPU might be busy, and hence the revalidate takes longer, or there's no ongoing connection to the DB, so opening a new one adds overhead, etc, these are all just things that one must think of when it comes to asynchronous work.

It does feel like `getServerSideProps` and a client side data fetching solution should do the trick for you (like SWR or React-Query), and perhaps you need an additional `api/route`, or refer to this trick by Josh Comeau, https://www.joshwcomeau.com/nextjs/refreshing-server-side-props/.

A client side data fetching solution, with revalidate (GetStaticProps), could also work, helping the UI follow the server state, while also statically rendering updated versions from the revalidate process. 

However, as said before, there's no guarantee on how long it will take, so even if the UI agrees with the server data, if the client does a page refresh, you might flash the user with old data, unless you don't pre-render the dynamic data, only the App Shell, and always get all the data from the client, using an `api/route`, at this point then revalidate is no longer necessary.
author: icyJoseph

body:
Thanks for the response! It seems like my main issue was relying too heavily on `unstable_revalidate`. I'm now using your suggestion of client-side data fetching with revalidate and it seems to be much more stable. For reference my setup is now:

- `getStaticProps` to generate the leaderboard page in advance
- Call `unstable_revalidate` when the leaderboard needs to be updated, but don't await the response (allowing for potential failures when revalidating)
- `useSWR` on the leaderboard page so that you eventually end up with the latest data
  - [This page](https://swr.vercel.app/docs/with-nextjs) was helpful in getting SSG set up with SWR
author: sspenst

body:
My solution was to change my approach to the following:

- `getStaticProps` to generate the leaderboard page in advance
- Call `unstable_revalidate` when the leaderboard needs to be updated, but don't await the response (allowing for potential failures when revalidating)
- `useSWR` on the leaderboard page so that you eventually end up with the latest data
  - [This page](https://swr.vercel.app/docs/with-nextjs) was helpful in getting SSG set up with SWR
  - Note this also allows using `Link` for the leaderboard page since the data will be retrieved with SWR

body:
Hello.

I don't think `rewrites` would be an issue for performance.
Having this key in your config file will generate new entries in `.next/routes-manifest.json`.

Next will then use these informations to know where a link should point to.
Since rewrites are applied to client-side routing, you shouldn't notice a difference.

body:
Hello.

There is no "options" within Next.js to achieve this.

One way to do it would be to create a custom server and prettify the response yourself.
While I don't advocate to make a custom server just for this, some might have found success in doing [so](https://stackoverflow.com/a/45297699/6645017).

I find it "weird" to prettify HTML for SEO reason though.
Search engines are well capable of parsing minified HTML and "prettifying" the output will add weight to the page.

More height means less performance and that could impact SEO in a negative way.

body:
>By render correctly in browser: I was initially checking the page as seen by chrome developer tools which (somehow) shows all of the correct headers even though they do not appear in the page source.

Dev tools shows you the state of DOM which is a parsed html with all eligible scripts in it activated. If you want to test things like opengraph tags and server-side render in general you have to turn off client javascript in your browser settings. AFAIK there is no way to do this on per-page/site basis without extensions.

>If thats true does this imply that Next JS is not rendering page metadata on the server?

No, but you have to be very careful how you write the code for server-rendered pages. Specifically a lot of react hooks do not work server-side, outside of initial state set by `React.useState()`. Chances are `useRouter()` is a client-side hook and, due it being a part of nextjs, it is treated "smartly" by the framework. Try to get `pathname` from `getServerSideProps`, pass it as props to the page component and see if it fixes the issue.

body: @adamjarling , you can use `next-seo` on the `_app.js` and then use `getStaticProps` on other pages and pass the required props. That way, when it renders, it'll go through `_app.js` so you're meta will get picked from there. If you place the meta in individual pages and have a seperate meta set in `_app.js` your app will override it.

body:
Current next version: `"next": "^11.1.3"`

The problem for me was like @GabenGar mentioned: I had some conditional logic in my component.

My component with dynamic route is in `pages/article/[slug].tsx`, where I used to fetch the article using the slug on component mount. I used to use `useRouter` to grab the slug in the URL → pass the slug to another `react-query` hook to fetch the article (`useFetchArticle`). This hook returns states like `isLoading`, so I used to do 

```
if(isLoading) {return <LoadingComponent/>}
```
This resulted in all of my overriding open graph tags to not show up. I changed the logic to fetching the article in `getServerSideProps` and removed all conditions in the component and everything worked.


I also used the `next/head` component to override the open graph tags defined in `_app.tsx`

```
<Head>
        <title>{title}</title>
        <meta property="og:image" content={hero_image} key="image" />
        <meta property="og:title" content={title} key="title" />
        <meta property="og:description" content={preview} key="description" />
      </Head>
```

body:
Even with the workarounds posted in this thread, I cannot get media previews working for any of my NexTJS websites using next-seo, or even hand-entered per page meta. Only the meta in my `_app` and `_document` show when viewing soure. This makes it nearly impossible to have a NextJS website with SEO.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1910461/230956380-f9887dda-073a-4818-8a0d-fc46c860bcbb.png)

The tags appear to be populated by javascript after load, making them nearly useless for search engines.

I cannot delete all the meta in `_app` (for one of these workarounds), since NextJS __requires__ your viewport be set there, and absolutely refuses to allow you to set it in `_document` (or pages for that matter) **

```
Warning: viewport meta tags should not be used in _document.js's <Head>. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-docume
nt-viewport-meta
Warning: viewport meta tags should not be used in _document.js's <Head>. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-docume
nt-viewport-meta
Warning: viewport meta tags should not be used in _document.js's <Head>. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-docume
nt-viewport-meta
```

---

The `getServerSideProps` route would force **all pages to be dynamically rendered, significantly reducing performance and completely disabling the ability to statically render the site as a PWA.**

How can this possibly be an acceptable outcome?

To quote the build log, when building **without** standalone:

```
Route (pages)                                             Size     First Load JS
┌ λ /                                                     1.74 kB         245 kB
├   /_app                                                 0 B             243 kB
├ λ /pricing                                              3.18 kB         249 kB
├ λ /profile/[id]                                         15.4 kB         275 kB
├ λ /ref                                                  253 B           292 kB
├ λ /ref/[id]                                             213 B           292 kB
├ λ /server-sitemap.xml                                   257 B           243 kB
└ λ /test                                                 2.19 kB         245 kB

... [all pages were forced server-side rendered]

λ  (Server)  server-side renders at runtime (uses getInitialProps or getServerSideProps)
```

When building **with** standalone/export:

```
Error occurred prerendering page "/". Read more: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/prerender-error
Error: Error for page /_app: pages with `getServerSideProps` can not be exported. See more info here:
 https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/gssp-export
    at .\node_modules\next\dist\export\worker.js:333:23
    at async Span.traceAsyncFn (.\node_modules\next\dist\trace\trace.js:79:20)
```

[To quote the documentation
](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props)

---

Further, `_document` requires a `<Head>` even if it's empty:

```
Your custom Document (pages/_document) did not render all the required subcomponent.
Missing component: <Head />
```


---

Is static export deprecated, or is this a bug in need of fixing to restore a supported feature?

body:
You have a project dependency called `services` at version `0.0.3`, which is causing a collision with your projects module called `services`. 

When name collisions like this is inevitable, people often scope their own modules to the name of the project by telling TypeScript/JavaScript (through `jsconfig.json`) how to resolve them. For example `@speed-dating/services` should resolve to `<root-of-project>/services`, and `services` should resolve to `node_modules/services`. Or just rename your `services` folder.

author: thibautsabot

body:
Hi,

I am not sure this is entire possible, as proposed anyway. Code included, and only used by `getServerSideProps` is removed on the client. That should be clear.

Now, that should give you an idea that client state changes, can't simply be passed to the `getServerSideProps` function, because in production these happen in two different machines. Data is often shared from `getServerSideProps` to the client as JSON serialized, but it is not trivial to flow in the other direction.

However, you can invoke `getServerSideProps` once again. It requires reading and understanding this article, https://www.joshwcomeau.com/nextjs/refreshing-server-side-props/.  

To fit your case though, I think `startYear` has to be a query on the URL, which if undefined, should fallback onto the value you use as initial value right now.

Another, simpler, option would be to use the `getServerSideProps` for what they are, initial data, and from the client make a call to `/api/all_events` (you'd need to implement this endpoint in `pages/api/all_events`), to you POST with the client side changed `startYear`. This `/api` route can connect to your events provider safely, because it happens on the server, and respond with update data to the client. Look into `SWR` and fallback data, https://swr.vercel.app/blog/swr-v1#fallback-data.

body:
Hello.

It seems that this is the indented behaviour (see: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30187#issuecomment-955803271).
I agree that the docs should probably mention it.

I suggest directly contributing to the RFC (see [this comment](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/29750#discussioncomment-2122618) also related to your issue).

In the meantime you could check for file extension inside your middleware like [some examples](https://github.com/vercel/examples/blob/main/edge-functions/i18n/pages/_middleware.ts#L10) do.

Ok I've made some progress. This is a use-case specific issue.

I'm importing remark in a file (/lib/posts.js) and then importing that file to a page (/pages/index.js).

author: LNFWebsite

body:
Bingo! Fixed it.

The class file was exporting via

```
module.exports = Posts
```

which I guess somehow conflicts with loading ESM modules down the road. I switched it to

```
export default class Posts {
...
}
```

And it's working now.
author: LNFWebsite

body: Awesome! Yeah I see, perhaps you needed to do `export = Posts` also, iirc, but I'd say it is best to have the same `import/export` syntax across your own modules.

body:
Thx @icyJoseph for your reply.

Basically I would like to use some "temporary" embedded GraphQL server during the Nextjs build process.
(Similar as Gatsby but just for some relational model of my own; no need to have full exposure of all Nextjs intern types/nodes).

If I would run the build process locally I could fire up a separate GraphQL server (e.g. Apollo Client using Prisma) but I want to the building using Github Action, so firing up a separate GraphQL server process is not an option as far as I know.

One of the benefits using GraphQL to driver my data needs - e.g. compared to using Prisma for direct db queries - is that the GraphQL plays nicely with JSON as output. E.g. when using Prisma.io I face a lot of issues with date types and decimals (as not natively supported by json).
And I could even use graphql-codegen to generate typesafe GraphQL types (again fully compatible with JSON hydration) that I could use in getStaticProps.

To give the bigger picture what I'm trying to achieve: I'm generating a static website of ~5K pages (various types) which source is either a json file or relational (sqlite) database.

Alternative is to use the sqlite database as source and pre-generate a json for each page with all data needed.
But one drawback is that it less typesafe than e.g. using a GraphQL schema.
author: marceloverdijk

body:
What about MSW? You could set it up to catch requests going out to, say, `localhost:3001`, even though there's no real server there, and then it is up to you to write your own resolvers. https://mswjs.io/docs/api/graphql for more information, and here for a repo using it,  https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-msw.

Again, at build time you enable the MSW `require` and initiate the server, then it will catch requests going out to a given endpoint. There you take over, and write your own resolvers.

body:
Hello.

To my knowledge there is two main ways of using a source image with the Image component.

The first one would be to leverage [Static File Serving](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving) for images inside the `public` folder.
In your case simply:
```js
<Image src='/img/logo.svg'
```

The other one is to first `import` the image and then pass it as src.
```js
import img from '/../public/img/logo.svg'

<Image src={img}
```

The mixing of both might be the reason you are seeing this error.

Hello!

I think the big focus of the Next.js team is on SWC (which is enabled by default)
SWC does support optional chaining, making it easier for people to use it by default.

See https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/compiler and https://swc.rs/docs/migrating-from-babel#comparison
author: thibautsabot

If you need to include any other babel plugins/presets, you'll need to make sure you include `next/babel` in your config as noted here: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/customizing-babel-config

author: zanzlender

body:
The question is - how long do we need to transpile that?

All browsers support `optionalChaining` since 2020:

https://caniuse.com/?search=optional%20chaining
![_optional_chaining____Can_I_use____Support_tables_for_HTML5__CSS3__etc](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4113649/167602759-c1e7c46b-c782-4734-ad12-3bdaf0410519.jpg)

adding `optionalChainging` with Babel or SWC increases the bundle size a little bit. 

for eaxmple

```js
console.log(window?.some?.property?.value);

const x = window.foo?.bar ?? null
```
is transpiled to:

```js
var ref, ref, ref;
console.log(window === null || window === void 0 ? void 0 : (ref = window.some) === null || ref === void 0 ? void 0 : (ref = ref.property) === null || ref === void 0 ? void 0 : ref.value);
var ref;
const x = (ref = (ref = window.foo) === null || ref === void 0 ? void 0 : ref.bar) !== null && ref !== void 0 ? ref : null;
```

author: Mic1

body:
Hey!

Yes, if you have heavy computations to do, the client will have to wait for them to be over before rendering anything.

Indeed, CSR with a useEffect could work fine in this case. You first load the skeleton on your page (with your header, footer and a loading screen for example), and then call the API from the client.
Once you have all the data, you can change the state and display it.

It's not ideal, since you'll have to do some work on the client, but you could pre-rendered your page (with SSG) to display the loading state very fast.

React Server Components would allow to "stream" data, giving you the ability to render new elements as you receive them. But it's still a very experimental feature.

body:
Good that you got it, I also missed the before interactive option. It goes out of the visible area on my screen... 

I guess you have your CLOUDINARY_ID configured in `next.config.js`?  https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/environment-variables#exposing-environment-variables-to-the-browser

Before the days of Script, I would have loaded the second script using `useEffect`, but it is a bit more complex. You'd basically, run the `useEffect` when the triggers were ready, check `document.scripts` to make sure the `src` is not already present. Then create a script node manually, and add an `onload` or attach an event listener, to register when it does load, and attach it to the DOM.

However, I'd say stick, to `Script`.

`sharp` is installed. I've checked in the container, it's there in node_modules.
Setting `NEXT_SHARP_PATH` makes no difference.
I've also tried other `node` base-images i.s.o. `node-alpine`, like the debian or buster-slim variants, as I read that alpine & sharp don't work together, but makes no difference.
And tried al kinds of changes to `next.config.js`, that I found here and there, too many to mention, but also makes no difference.

All I find is a lot of people struggling with this, but never a solution.
Any help or suggestions are highly appreciated.
author: CodeblackNL

body:
As it turns out, I found the solution myself :-)
It is the `--ignore-scripts` that is part of the `npm install` command.

Change this:
`RUN npm install --production --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts --prefer-offline`
to this:
`RUN npm install --production --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline`
author: CodeblackNL

body:
What works for me, is to change the version of Alpine Linux in the Dockerfile:
`FROM node:18-alpine AS deps` (instead of node:16)

I didn't add node-sharp to my `.env` file.

author: giovanifranz

body: I figured it out! The reason is that I used different architecture and platform of Node.js at build and runtime. My dockerfile: ``` # alpine version here FROM node:18.10.0-alpine AS build RUN npm build # base linux version FROM nginx:1.23.1 AS runtime RUN curl -sL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_18.x | bash RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y nodejs ... ``` Ref: https://github.com/lovell/sharp/issues/2312 > The architecture and platform of Node.js used for npm install must be the same as the architecture and platform of Node.js used at runtime. The sharp bin version built in first build stage doesn't match `sharp-linux-x64` version required in runtime: ``` # ls build/Release sharp-linuxmusl-x64.node ``` So I just use the `slim` node instead of `alpine` version. ``` FROM node:18.10.0-slim AS build ``` Hope it helps 😄

author: spiderneo

body:
The problem is that the example in [with-docker](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-docker) repo is incorrect. 
```Dockerfile
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
```
First it copies node_modules from deps and then it overrides them by copying from local directory `COPY . .`

There is my version of slightly modified Dockerfile which works correctly:
```Dockerfile
# Install dependencies only when needed
FROM node:16-alpine AS deps
# Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies based on the preferred package manager
COPY package.json yarn.lock* package-lock.json* pnpm-lock.yaml* ./
RUN \
  if [ -f yarn.lock ]; then yarn --frozen-lockfile; \
  elif [ -f package-lock.json ]; then npm ci; \
  elif [ -f pnpm-lock.yaml ]; then yarn global add pnpm && pnpm i --frozen-lockfile; \
  else echo "Lockfile not found." && exit 1; \
  fi


# Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM node:16-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY pages/ ./pages/
COPY public/ ./public/
COPY server/ ./server/
COPY src/ ./src/
COPY .eslintignore            \
     .eslintrc.json           \
     .gitignore               \
     next-env.d.ts            \
     next.config.js           \
     tsconfig.json            \
     ./
COPY --from=deps /app/package.json ./package.json
COPY --from=deps /app/package-lock.json ./package-lock.json
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
# Add env for production
# COPY .docker/production/.env.local .env.local

# Next.js collects completely anonymous telemetry data about general usage.
# Learn more here: https://nextjs.org/telemetry
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during the build.
# ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

RUN npm run build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM node:16-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV production
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during runtime.
# ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

COPY --from=builder /app/.env.local ./.env.local
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public

# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size
# https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000

CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

P.s. also note that it copies only the required dirs and files from local directory to speed up the build
author: coderNeos

body: Thanks for this! I think the root cause of this issue is correctly identified and it's related to overwriting ./node_modules from local. In my case I solved it by adding the missing .dockerignore from the mentioned above repository [with-docker](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-docker)

author: singleseeker

body:
One more solution is to install `sharp` with specific args:

```dockerfile
RUN npm install -g --arch=x64 --platform=linux --libc=glibc sharp
```

and then copy this global package into `runner`:

```dockerfile
ENV NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp
COPY --from=deps --chown=nextjs:nodejs /usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp /usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp
```

Example:

```dockerfile
FROM node:21.4-alpine AS deps
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
WORKDIR /app

COPY package.json ./
COPY package-lock.json ./

RUN npm ci

RUN npm install -g --arch=x64 --platform=linux --libc=glibc sharp

FROM node:21.4-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app

COPY . .
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules

RUN npm run build

FROM node:21.4-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1
ENV NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

COPY --from=deps --chown=nextjs:nodejs /usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp /usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

COPY .env.production /app/.env.production
RUN chown -R nextjs:nodejs /app/.env.production

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 8080

ENV PORT 8080

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

```

author: bharad22

body:
I just found that we don't need to specify NEXT_SHARP_PATH in env and don't need to install sharp by specifying --arch, --platform, --libc. We can directly install sharp by specifying --ignore-engines.

```
FROM node:20-alpine

WORKDIR /out
ARG configuration

COPY package*.json yarn.lock ./

RUN yarn install
RUN yarn add sharp --ignore-engines

COPY . .
RUN cp /out/${configuration} .env

RUN yarn build

EXPOSE 3000

author: deadcoder0904

body:
I had the same issue and couldn't figure out how to solve it with `pnpm` as a package manager. For reference, I'm using `next@14.1.0`.

Turns out that the equivalent to the command
```dockerfile
RUN npm install -g --arch=x64 --platform=linux --libc=glibc sharp
```

with `pnpm` is 
```dockerfile
RUN pnpm i -g --config.arch=x64 --config.platform=linux --config.libc=glibc sharp
```

After more tinkering, pinning the `sharp` version to an older one and just installing it normally instead of globally, I thought about running `node` directly inside the docker container and try to `require` sharp (as in the [source code](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/da616e56d265d2db1c9808129bd28c6d1884d3a9/packages/next/src/server/image-optimizer.ts#L50)).
I got the following (very informative) message:

```bash
/app $ node
Welcome to Node.js v18.19.1.
Type ".help" for more information.
> sharp = require("/app/node_modules/sharp")
Uncaught Error: 
Something went wrong installing the "sharp" module

Cannot find module '../build/Release/sharp-linuxmusl-x64.node'
Require stack:
- /app/node_modules/.pnpm/sharp@0.32.6/node_modules/sharp/lib/sharp.js
- /app/node_modules/.pnpm/sharp@0.32.6/node_modules/sharp/lib/constructor.js
- /app/node_modules/.pnpm/sharp@0.32.6/node_modules/sharp/lib/index.js
- <repl>

Possible solutions:
- Install with verbose logging and look for errors: "npm install --ignore-scripts=false --foreground-scripts --verbose sharp"
- Install for the current linuxmusl-x64 runtime: "npm install --platform=linuxmusl --arch=x64 sharp"
- Consult the installation documentation: https://sharp.pixelplumbing.com/install
```
Turns out that I'm using alpine linux, so apparently forcing the usage of `glibc` breaks the installation, because the runner expects a `musl` version. Additionally, as suggested in this discussion https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/40125#discussioncomment-8254222, I opted to install a pinned version instead of the latest one. Thus, my `RUN` command was the following:

```dockerfile
RUN pnpm i --config.arch=x64 --config.platform=linux --config.libc=musl sharp@0.32.6
```

Notice the removal of the global flag, the specific `libc` and the version pinning.

After confirming the fix, I tried to go back to the latest version (0.33.2) by removing the version pinning, but it broke again with that single change, confirming what was suggested by @vvbbnn00, who links back to #59516.

For reference, my final Dockerfile looks like the following:

<details>
  <summary>Expand Dockerfile</summary>
  
 
  ```dockerfile
# https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-docker/Dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine AS base

# Install dependencies only when needed
FROM base AS deps
# Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies based on the preferred package manager
COPY package.json yarn.lock* package-lock.json* pnpm-lock.yaml* ./
# ENV PNPM_HOME=/usr/local/bin
RUN corepack enable pnpm && pnpm i --frozen-lockfile
RUN pnpm i --config.arch=x64 --config.platform=linux --config.libc=musl sharp@0.32.6

# Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM base AS builder
WORKDIR /app
# https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/36935
# Uncomment the following line if there are permission issues with .next/cache directory
# RUN mkdir -p /app/.next/cache && chown nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/cache
RUN mkdir -p /app/.next/cache
# Persist the next cache in a volume
VOLUME ["/app/.next/cache"]
COPY . .
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules

# Next.js collects completely anonymous telemetry data about general usage.
# Learn more here: https://nextjs.org/telemetry
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during the build.
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

ENV NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/app/node_modules/sharp

RUN corepack enable pnpm && pnpm run build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM base AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV production
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during runtime.
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

# COPY --from=deps --chown=nextjs:nodejs /usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp /usr/local/lib/node_modules/sharp
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public

# Set the correct permission for prerender cache
RUN mkdir .next
RUN chown nextjs:nodejs .next

# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size
# https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000
# set hostname to localhost
ENV HOSTNAME "0.0.0.0"
# server.js is created by next build from the standalone output
# https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/api-reference/next-config-js/output
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
  ```
  
</details>

body:
thanks @viantirreau, i faced the same error 2 weeks later. also, found out to save next cache so thanks for sharing the dockerfile as well.

Bdw, I just added `NEXT_SHARP_PATH` environment variable before `pnpm build` (`next build`) happens. I've already installed `sharp` as a dependency in my `package.json` bdw.

Turns out, commenting `RUN pnpm install --config.arch=x64 --config.platform=linux --config.libc=musl sharp@0.33.2` doesn't work.

### Dockerfile

```bash
RUN pnpm install --config.arch=x64 --config.platform=linux --config.libc=musl sharp@0.33.2

ENV NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/app/node_modules/sharp
RUN pnpm build
```

Next.js doc has [a simple guide on it here](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/sharp-missing-in-production).
author: deadcoder0904

body:
Glad that it worked!

> Turns out, commenting RUN pnpm install --config.arch=x64 --config.platform=linux --config.libc=musl sharp@0.33.2 doesn't work.

I ended up removing `sharp` as a dependency in my `package.json`, because my system's `libc` is `glibc`, while `alpine` uses `musl`, so I was kind of forced to side load it just on the Dockerfile. 
I believe that's why commenting that line doesn't work: it leaves you with the `sharp` version indicated by the `package.json`, which is incompatible with `alpine`. Because it has to be overridden anyways, I opted to remove `sharp` from the `package.json` altogether.

#### Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS base

# mostly inspired from https://github.com/BretFisher/node-docker-good-defaults/blob/main/Dockerfile & https://github.com/remix-run/example-trellix/blob/main/Dockerfile

# Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
RUN corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@8.15.4 --activate 
# set the store dir to a folder that is not in the project
RUN pnpm config set store-dir ~/.pnpm-store
RUN pnpm fetch

# 1. Install all dependencies including dev dependencies
FROM base AS deps
# Root user is implicit so you don't have to actually specify it. From https://stackoverflow.com/a/45553149/6141587
# USER root
USER node
# WORKDIR now sets correct permissions if you set USER first so `USER node` has permissions on `/app` directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies based on the preferred package manager
COPY --chown=node:node package.json pnpm-lock.yaml* ./
COPY --chown=node:node /src/app/db/migrations ./migrations

USER root
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline
RUN pnpm install sharp

# 2. Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM base AS builder
WORKDIR /app
# Inspired by https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/36935
RUN mkdir -p /app/.next/cache && chown -R node:node /app/.next/cache
# Persist the next cache in a volume
VOLUME ["/app/.next/cache"]
COPY --from=deps --chown=node:node /app/node_modules ./node_modules

COPY --chown=node:node . .

# This will do the trick, use the corresponding env file for each environment.
COPY --chown=node:node .env.production .env.production

# Copied from https://stackoverflow.com/a/69867550/6141587
USER root
# Give /data directory correct permissions otherwise WAL mode won't work. It means you can't have 2 users writing to the database at the same time without this line as *.sqlite-wal & *.sqlite-shm are automatically created & deleted when *.sqlite is busy.
RUN mkdir -p /data && chown -R node:node /data

ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1

# 3. Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM base AS runner
USER node
WORKDIR /app

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000
ENV HOSTNAME 0.0.0.0

ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
# ENV NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/app/node_modules/sharp

# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size

COPY --from=builder --chown=node:node /app/litestream.yml /etc/litestream.yml

body:
alright, i finally solved it.

i didn't do anything different other than deleting `node_modules` & reinstalling dependencies again.

the solution with `pnpm` was to normally install it afterwards:

```dockerfile
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline
RUN pnpm install sharp@0.32.6
```

i think putting this in my `package.json` helped:

```json
	"pnpm": {
		"supportedArchitectures": {
			"os": [
				"current"
			],
			"cpu": [
				"x64",
				"arm64"
			]
		}
	}
```

# Move the drizzle directory to the runtime image
COPY --from=builder --chown=node:node /app/src/app/db/migrations ./migrations

# Move the litestream binary to the runtime image from the litestream image
# You can use a specific version of litestream by changing the tag
# COPY --from=litestream/litestream:0.3.13 /usr/local/bin/litestream /usr/local/bin/litestream

COPY --from=builder --chown=node:node /app/scripts/run.sh ./run.sh
RUN chmod +x run.sh

CMD ["sh", "run.sh"]
```

body:
I think easiest solution is installing sharp in docker before running the server.

I completing every step to build my app, after that I am installing sharp before running my server.
Also don't forget to remove sharp from your `package.json`.

<details>
  <summary>Full Docker</summary>
  
  ```Dockerfile
# Install dependencies only when needed
FROM node:20.7.0-alpine AS deps

RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat

WORKDIR /app

COPY package.json ./ 

RUN npm i

# Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM node:20.7.0-alpine AS builder

ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .

RUN npm run build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM node:20.7.0-alpine AS runner

ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1
ENV PORT 3000

WORKDIR /app

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

# You only need to copy next.config.js if you are NOT using the default configuration
COPY --from=builder /app/next.config.js ./
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./package.json
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

# Install sharp
RUN npm i sharp

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENTRYPOINT ["node", "server.js"]
  ```
</details>

Thanks for the suggestion @kristijorgji. The organization I was most satisfied with was putting all my source code in a `src/` directory and doing nothing more than export on the files in `pages/`.

So the files are structured like this

```
pages
  _app.ts
  foo.ts
  index.ts
src
  _app
    index.tsx
    _appComponent1.tsx
    _appComponent2.tsx
  foo
    index.tsx
    fooComponent1.tsx
    ...etc
  ...etc
```

Then on `pages/foo.ts` I would only do an export from `src/foo`, like this

```ts
// pages/foo.ts
export { Foo as default } from '../src/foo';
export { getStaticPaths } from '../src/foo/getStaticPaths';
export { getStaticProps } from '../src/foo/getStaticProps';
```

Thanks for your answer @thibautsabot.

I do have a follow up question :) If the "gate" requires external data to decide wether or not the user should be redirected, how would you approach this?

A naive approach would be to call an external endpoint to retrieve the data everytime the middleware is executed but that might be heavy on the endpoint and create latency when navigating between the app pages.

Some ideas we have:
- Add Cache-Control headers to the endpoint
- Use a cache provider like Redis to store the value for the condition (not very much happy about adding another infrastructure piece thought)

Thank you for your help.

body:
Hey @thibautsabot 

Our current gate system have access to a Redux store.

It can't be hardcoded. It doesn't change frequently but still, it shouldn't be too long before the UI reflect a change.

For example, in one of our apps we use a gate to catch a new user which haven't completed the second step of it's onboarding and redirect the user to the proper page. If this information is not up to date, the user will be stuck in a loop.

For this specific gate we thought about persisting the onboarding state in a cookie to prevent subsequent remote calls.
author: patricklafrance

body:
Oh yeah, in this case, I'd probably handle a redirect in the specific onboarding page instead of going through a middleware.

Middleware are ran on isolation and don't have context about a user execpt for its cookies.
author: thibautsabot

body:
That's what I thought we would do at first but the user can come from any url so we can't take for granted he will come from a specific onboarding page.

Specifically:

1- The first onboarding step is on our marketing website
2- Once completed, the user is redirected to the second onboarding step which is in our app
3- The user can complete the second onboarding step or come back later
4- If the user come back later we can't take for granted it will be a direct hit on the second onboarding page so we need a gate.

Our use case is super specific. There are a lot of solutions depending on the use cases. Thanks for the discussion!
