author: thibautsabot

body:
Going from CSR to SSR, is not trivial, there's a few things to consider, those that concern Next.js I think are solved in a very efficient way.

## No Window (during SSR)

Unguarded access to window or any DOM elements, might cause problems during SSR. Next.js itself can't really help here, but the framework never interferes with React components, so you can guard yourself inside `useEffects` or through `typeof window` checks.

## JavaScript Contexts

Because the same code base outputs client side and server bundles, it is often possible to mix things up, however Next.js does a very good job at eliminating code from the client, https://next-code-elimination.vercel.app/, and it uses data serialization to pass initial data during hydration.

As a developer, you gotta also consider that, roughly speaking, global/shared objects on the server have that quality to all clients, whereas global/shared data on a client, is for that client only. Next.js does a good job by providing separation of concerns, in `api` routes, data fetching functions, or even middleware.

## Memory Leaks

When you CSR application has memory leaks, these usually go away with a page reload. Doing that on the server is not trivial, and small memory leaks accrue into a server crash, usually over the weekend.

Next.js server side functions offer very clear methods to handle incoming requests and respond to them, and even expose declarative Redirect, Not Found, and "Continue" return types for `getServerSideProps`/`getStaticProps`. Api routes are lambdas that do not hog memory. Of course as a developer you have to do your part to, and not leave open handlers, ever running timers, etc.

Hello.

You cannot use React hooks outside of React components.
Here `getFractals` and `getWallet` are simple functions, you cannot use `useRouter` here.
If you need it in your functions, you can pass it as an argument.

If you meant for getFractals to be a React component, it needs to start with an uppercase letter.
author: thibautsabot

body:
Not sure if you fixed this, but I think your components should start with uppercase. 

```
React has built-in convention on how to name your components:

Capitalized — component names with first letter uppercase are treated as a React component (e.g. <Foo />);
Dot Notation — component names with a dot in the name, regardless of the case, are too treated as a React component (e.g. <foo.bar />, <Foo.Bar />, etc.);
Lowercase — component names starting with lowercase letters are treated as DOM elements (e.g. <div>, <span>, etc.).
```

And

```
ui = <Capitalized /> // React.createElement(Capitalized)
ui = <property.access /> // React.createElement(property.access)
ui = <Property.Access /> // React.createElement(Property.Access)
ui = <Property['Access'] /> // SyntaxError
ui = <lowercase /> // React.createElement('lowercase')
ui = <kebab-case /> // React.createElement('kebab-case')
ui = <Upper-Kebab-Case /> // React.createElement('Upper-Kebab-Case')
ui = <Upper_Snake_Case /> // React.createElement(Upper_Snake_Case)
ui = <lower_snake_case /> // React.createElement('lower_snake_case')
```

body:
> How would you specify loading components for each? or turn off ssr?

In my mind this would be used for using multiple components of the same time. EG: All components loaded do not need to be ssr, and as such having the single options object as the second parameter applies those options to all provided loaders

>How should this behave if one fails to dynamically load?

This is a bit of a more tricky one to answer. We could return `null` & rely on the dev implementation to add a `.catch` or `try {...} catch {...}` to handle the errors. Alternatively handle it the same way [`Promise.all` currently handles it](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Promise/all#rejection) by instead rejecting with the value of the rejection?
If anyone has any better ways / ideas of how to handle that, feel free to throw them out.

What exactly did you try out?
The first thing that comes to my mind is to lazy/dynamic load your components.

You could delay the map or the carousel for exemple until the skeleton has been loaded.
Another way would be to leverage SSG to pre-render your pages and only serve HTML.

If your page are dynamic, maybe you could still statically render an "app shell" and then CSR your data.

I suggest running a Lighthouse and WebPageTest to see what comes up how your website behave.

What's your LCP, FCP, etc...? Are all pages slow? How much JS or images are being downloaded?

body:
Hello!

`getStaticProps` is called at build time and has no request-specific knowledge.
You cannot have multiple rendered HTML for a same page. Everyone will get the same output.

You'll ever need to have some conditional rendering handled by the client, or use something like `getServerSideProps` to process the output for each user.

body:
Use CSS, if the `useEffect` solution is not good enough.

Sorry for being simplistic, but the **initial server and client HTML has to be the same**. Such hooks usually read directly on the window. At build time there's no window, because the build happens on the server, so there's NO WAY to know which device will render the content, by definition, and the hooks are forced to make a decision, often assuming to be on desktop.

body:
> @Flavien-Pensato We are providing a static file in `./public/_next/static`, and the same problem occurs. What's the problem? Please help me

I can provide the command line if you show me your monorepo structure and gave me the build folder (by default .next)
author: Flavien-Pensato

body:
The problem is in a monorepo setup, Next.js produces a `node_modules` folder in the root directory and in the /.next/standalone directory.

If you `COPY` files normally like this:
```
COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs /packages/dashboard/.next/standalone ./
COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs /packages/dashboard/.next/static ./packages/dashboard/.next/static
COPY /packages/dashboard/public ./packages/dashboard/public
```
The root `node_modules` folder will be missing so that a missing next packages error occurs.

The possible workaround for this is to change the Dockerfile commands in a way that includes the root `node_modules` folder and you would copy the other files inside the root `.next` folder.

The final folder structure should be:
```
/.next
  /standalone
    /packages
      /dashboard
        /.next
        /public
        package.json
        server.js
  /node_modules
  package.json
/node_modules   <-- you're missing this one
package.json
```

This workaround is cumbersome and feels hacky. This goes against the idea of the "standalone" directory which can be deployed as is. I think Next.js has to produce the same output as it would in a non-monorepo setup.
author: jezsung

body:
In case it helps anyone, here's a dockerfile for a turbo monorepo using the "single dockerfile in the root of the repo" guidance.  
- web is a nextjs web app
- worker is a bog-standard webpacked nodejs app
- my-sdk is an ubuntu docker image with my dev tools pre-installed
- my-node is the node base image + doppler
- the special dotenv copy and the launch.js and package.*.json are just for calling doppler to configure the environment and launch server.js

the redundant `apps/web/apps/web` is a minor cosmetic annoyance but I'm not losing sleep over it.  
thanks to @pstoica for posting this, I was having trouble figuring out where to copy public and static content.

```
ARG TARGETPLATFORM
FROM my-sdk:dev AS build
COPY ./ /my-app
WORKDIR /my-app
RUN bun install --frozen-lockfile && \
    bun prisma:generate && \
    bun run build

FROM my-node:dev AS web
COPY --from=build /my-app/apps/web/.next/standalone /my-app/apps/web
COPY --from=build /my-app/apps/web/public /my-app/apps/web/apps/web/public
COPY --from=build /my-app/apps/web/.next/static /my-app/apps/web/apps/web/.next/static
COPY --from=build /my-app/node_modules/dotenv /my-app/apps/web/node_modules/dotenv
COPY --from=build /my-app/scripts/launch.js /my-app/apps/web
COPY --from=build /my-app/scripts/package.web.json /my-app/apps/web/package.json
WORKDIR /my-app/apps/web
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "launch.js"]

FROM my-node:dev AS worker
COPY --from=build /my-app/node_modules/dotenv /my-app/apps/worker/node_modules/dotenv
COPY --from=build /my-app/apps/worker/dist /my-app/apps/worker
COPY --from=build /my-app/scripts/launch.js /my-app/apps/worker
COPY --from=build /my-app/scripts/package.worker.json /my-app/apps/worker/package.json
WORKDIR /my-app/apps/worker
CMD ["node", "launch.js"]

```

So, as a recap, when building the application with `standalone: true` in a monorepo, some dependencies are installed in `.next/standalone/node_modules`, outside of the next app package folder, which break the application: I hope this thread does not remain dead for another 2 years...
author: nx-alejandrolacasa

body:
Well, I've found the solution to the issue myself, which might be helpful for you all here. It all boils down to how the dependency is "registered" in the package-lock.json.

When the dependency is registered as an "exclusive" dependency of the `site` package in my case, everything functions correctly. The `next standalone + turbo` combo resolves it perfectly and places the package in `standalone/apps/site/node_modules` where it should be:
```json
"apps/site/node_modules/next-intl": {
      "version": "3.5.1",
      ...
    },
```

However, when this dependency is shared with another package, for example, another app that runs Storybook in the same monorepo, the dependency is placed at the root level, as it is common to both packages.
```json
"node_modules/next-intl": {
      "version": "3.5.1",
      ...
    },
```

In such a scenario, `next standalone + turbo` will place the package in `standalone/node_modules` and attempt to extract from the dependencies of that package everything required for it to function. In my case, this involved some components from `next`. The issue arose during this extraction of dependencies, as it failed to correctly identify them, causing the app to crash at build time.

Ultimately, to resolve the issue, I kept the dependency in `site` alone and exported it from there to the other package, allowing it to be used there without being a direct dependency:
```ts
/* apps/site/libs/next-intl/index.ts */
export * from 'next-intl'
```

```ts
/* apps/storybook/.storybook/preview.tsx */
import { NextIntlClientProvider } from '@site/libs/next-intl'
```

While not the most elegant solution, it works sufficiently well for me. I hope I've managed to explain myself clearly and that this helps others.

body:
Hope this helps someone. 
For my monorepo setup, I tried copying the standalone build to docker and that was working, whereas when I was trying to directly copy the build output to a folder (with `cp`), zip it and then extract it elsewhere on my machine, I kept getting errors for some missing packages (cannot find styled-jsx). 
What I figured out (and is related to what @nx-alejandrolacasa says above) is that the nested node modules have symlinks and the way to go by was to preserve them. So copying with `cp -P` and zipping with `zip -y` worked in my case. I am on `next 14.2.4`.

body:
i was working with the turbo repo with standalone next app, i got it working👍
```docker
# Base stage with Alpine image
FROM node:20-alpine3.17 AS base
WORKDIR /app

# # # Install necessary packages
RUN apk update && apk add --no-cache libc6-compat openssh git

# # # Install pnpm and turbo globally define you specific version according to the package.json
RUN npm install -g pnpm@9.7.0 turbo  
RUN pnpm config set store-dir ~/.pnpm-store

# # #############################################################
# # # Stage 1 - Pruning unnecessary files                       #
# # #############################################################

FROM base AS pruner

# Copy all files to the container and Prune files that are not needed for the specified project
COPY . .
RUN npx turbo prune --scope=web --docker

# # #############################################################
# # # Stage 2 - Installing dependencies and building            #
# # #############################################################

FROM base AS builder
WORKDIR /app
# # Copy lockfile and package.json's of isolated subworkspace
COPY --from=pruner /app/out/pnpm-lock.yaml ./pnpm-lock.yaml
COPY --from=pruner /app/out/pnpm-workspace.yaml ./pnpm-workspace.yaml
COPY --from=pruner /app/out/json/ .

# Install dependencies using pnpm, using a cache for faster builds
RUN --mount=type=cache,id=pnpm-store,target=~/.pnpm-store pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

# # Copy source code of isolated subworkspace
COPY --from=pruner /app/out/full/ .
RUN turbo build --filter=web
RUN pnpm prune --prod --no-optional

# # Cleanup: Remove source files that are not needed
RUN rm -rf ./**/*/src
RUN rm -rf apps/web/.next/standalone/node_modules


# # #############################################################
# # # Stage 3 - Run the application                             #
# # #############################################################

FROM base AS runner

WORKDIR /app/

ENV NODE_ENV="production"

# Copy built files from the builder stage
# COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/ .     

COPY apps/web/public apps/web/.next/standalone/public
RUN mkdir -p apps/web/.next/standalone/public/_next/static
RUN cp -r apps/web/.next/static/* apps/web/.next/standalone/public/_next/static

CMD node apps/web/.next/standalone/server.js

```

body:
My working dockerfile for nx monorepo:

The key is to copy `public` and `.next/static` into the `dist/apps/steps` folder instead of `apps/steps` folder.


```
FROM node:20-alpine AS base

# Install dependencies only when needed
FROM base AS deps
# # Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
# RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies based on the preferred package manager
COPY package.json yarn.lock* package-lock.json* pnpm-lock.yaml* ./
ENV CYPRESS_INSTALL_BINARY=0
RUN corepack enable pnpm && pnpm i --frozen-lockfile

# Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM base AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .

# Next.js collects completely anonymous telemetry data about general usage.
# Learn more here: https://nextjs.org/telemetry
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during the build.
# ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1

RUN npx nx run steps:build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM base AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV=production
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during runtime.
# ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs


# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size
# https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/dist/apps/steps/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/dist/apps/steps/public dist/apps/steps/public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/dist/apps/steps/.next/static dist/apps/steps/.next/static

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT=3000

# server.js is created by next build from the standalone output
# https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/api-reference/next-config-js/output
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"

CMD ["node", "apps/steps/server.js"]
```

body:
I didn’t dig too deep into that issue, but from what I can see, it looks like the problem is tied to where the lock file is and what’s in it. In a monorepo, if there’s a lock file in the package, the standalone output goes right under standalone. But ideally, the lock file should be at the root of the monorepo, which means the standalone folder will have subfolders like /app/docs.

Most of the time, I fixed this by deleting some lock files that were probably misplaced in the packages.

body:
Hello.

Yes I believe this is the intended behaviour.
If you are redirecting the request to another URL in the first middleware, it makes sense that the second one is not called since the destination URL will not match the route.

However, if the redirected route also has a middleware, it'll be executed. 

For example:
```
Request to https://xxxxxx.com/blog.

* pages/
     _middleware.tsx -> First middleware invoked. Redirect to /other.
        index.tsx
        blog/
            index.tsx
            _middleware.ts -> This one will be skipped since the new destination URL doesn't match this route.
         other/
            index.tsx
            _middleware.ts -> This middleware will be called at the end of the chain.
```

author: candidodmv

body:
Hi,

Actually..., according to https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/36772 this will be deprecated, so it is best to not rely on it, as of now (before the deprecations) what you could've done is to redirect to the page instead, that'd trigger the nested middleware. Of course there's some disadvantages with that, such as loss of masking URLs, and client side page reload.

Here's a bit more info: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/36879#issuecomment-1125825500.

What you can do is, from the top level middleware, given by the pathname, apply different functions (which would've been the nested middleware).

Even with `unstable_revalidate`, only the requested **page** will be rebuilt, not the whole **app**.

The "build process" is often referring to the step when the entire app is built (with `npm run build` for exemple).

It is true that page can be re-build on some occasions, but it's probably not what is being mentioned in this section of the tutorial 🙂.

body:
Hello!

You cannot access browser information inside a middleware.
Middlewares are ran on the [Edge Runtime](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime) and are server-side code.

What you can do however, is to leverage cookies since there are passed on every request.
See https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/server

body:
Hey 👋.

There are a lot of way of styling things with Next.js!

Out of the box, Next supports [CSS modules](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/built-in-css-support#adding-component-level-css) and [SASS](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/built-in-css-support#sass-support), it should be enough for most apps 🙂.

If you prefer some abstraction, then [tailwind](https://tailwindcss.com/) seems to be the more popular approach at the moment.

You mentioned [chakra-ui](https://chakra-ui.com/) for a "design system" but you also have [antd](https://ant.design/), [MUI](https://mui.com/), [react-bootstrap](https://react-bootstrap.github.io/), etc..

Then if you prefer CSS-in-JS you can use [Styled-components](https://styled-components.com/) (supported by SWC), [emotion](https://emotion.sh/docs/introduction) or even [styled-jsx](https://nextjs.org/blog/styling-next-with-styled-jsx) which is also maintained by Vercel.
You could even extract your CSS-in-JS at build time with something like [Linaria](https://github.com/callstack/linaria).

Every solution has its benefits and drawbacks. There is no "perfect" solution, but some of them might fit best for specific projects!

body:
Hello.

As you can see here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/build/webpack-config.ts#L408 SWC will be used if any kind of babel config is present.
And that makes sense, because you cannot compile a library with babel and the rest of your application with SWC.

Everything need to use the same bundling methods to be compatible as the output will be different for both tools.



Do you get an error when trying to compile with SWC? It seems that the package you linked needs `'@babel/preset-react', '@babel/preset-typescript'` which SWC supports. _(But the internal of the package seems to be very linked to babel so I doubt it'd work 😕)_

Hi. 
Sorry that I didn't clarified this in question:  utility library is not used during build process itself, it's invoked via separate command (i.e. it is not part of app's dependencies graph): 
```
    "scripts": {
        "start": "next",
        "build": "next build",
        "extract-messages": "extract-messages <...args>"
    },
```

> Do you get an error when trying to compile with SWC?

No, I don't get any errors when trying to compile project (`next build`) via SWC or Babel, both cases works OK.

I just need to have `babel.config.js` inside project folder so  `yarn extract-messages` can run without errors, but now I forced to delete this file each time I want to compile project using SWC (otherwise next.js will pick up babel config and will not use SWC).

body:
Thanks for pointing me to right direction. So I was able to fix it by moving `babel.config.js` to `./translations` subdirectory and changing script to this : 
`"extract-messages": "cd ./translations && extract-messages <...args>"` 

 in this case `cd ./translations` is required to change CWD (so `extract-react-intl-messages` can see Babel config but not `next.js`)

body:
Hello.

The "only" way of achieving it would be to follow https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/custom-page-extensions#including-non-page-files-in-the-pages-directory and use custom pages extensions.

Basically your "real" pages ends up with a pattern (such as '.page.tsx') and your tests files do not.

This request has been kind of controversial over the years, with some [merged solutions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/3195), [closed ones](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/16464), some [revert](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/3578), [questions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/3728), and [opinions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/8454#issuecomment-523418536).

body:
Hello!

`fill` is more suited when you want your children to take all of the parent available space.
It's often used when you don't know the size of your image https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/image-optimization#what-if-i-dont-know-the-size-of-my-images

If you already know the size of your image, then it's better to use `intrinsic` or `responsive`.
If you want your image to scale up on larger viewport, use `responsive`, if not, use `intrinsic`.

See: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#layout

Example with fill: https://image-component.nextjs.gallery/layout-fill
Example with responsive: https://image-component.nextjs.gallery/layout-responsive

body:
Hello.

> SSR is slower compare to CSR for the same content.

It'd greatly depend on the client IMO.
If there are heavy computation and multiple endpoints to call, having this load on the server might be beneficial.

> can I use SSR when I first enter the site, so the SEO is good. And use CSR for all following interactions and data loading

This is how it work by default.
When you enter the website for the first time or refresh the page, SSR will kick in.
Then if you use client-side navigation (with [next/link](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link)), the client will do the rest. This is the "standard" way of Single Page Application (SPA).

In the case of Next.js, you might want to use [shallow routing](https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/shallow-routing) to avoid calling data fetching methods.

body:
Hi,

First of all, without metrics, these could just be perceptions. Also, never overestimate mobile speeds and computing power, all kinds of weird stuff can happen in the real world.

That being said, when you say SSR, which one do you mean... GetServerSideProps (GSSP), or GetStaticProps, or GetStaticProps + GetStaticPaths (GSPx2) with fallback set true.

I have a demo site that generates views for products, which could be hundreds, so I use GSP with fallback set to true, and at build time we pre-render only a few (the most sought ones).

IN PRODUCTION, when a `next/link` points to one of these products and it is visible in the viewport, if it was not generated at build time, the server generates the page, so that when I click on it, there's zero time to show it. 

It must be said that directly going to a product that was not created at build time, does have a small time window where a fallback UI is shown, but this fallback UI is not shown to crawlers and bots. https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-true.

> Web crawlers, such as Google, won't be served a fallback and instead the path will behave as in [fallback: 'blocking'](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-blocking).

This is fundamentally different than GetServerSideProps which runs only on demand, it waits until the user really wants it. GetServerSideProps fits best for pages that need to be calculated with as most recent data as possible, for example, decode a cookie/token on the incoming request, to generate especial content. 

I think, maybe in your case you want to do GSPx2 (getStaticProps + getStaticPaths).

Well, the first time you visit a page with `getServerSideProps`, the server is indeed rendering the entire HTML string, but if you jump from other page to the getServerSideProps page by `<Link>`, it does not, it just send a json back, not doing any rendering on serverside.
So if the nextjs want to implement this function, it'd be very easy to do the blocking false thing when jumping from `<Link>`, and can inject a prop to the page's component to indicate it is loading.

For suspense, it seems that data fetching does not work with suspense, only work with lazy load of component. Accroding to [this](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/react-18/streaming)

body:
I just tried out the raw layout.

### What layout / styling behavior were you previously unable to accomplish with next/image?

I had a menu dropdown that overlayed some next/image instances. The next.js Image component created its own stacking context independent of the absolute-positioned dropdown, and no amount of cajoling or convincing made the stacking context of the images appear lower in the 3d layout of the page than the menu. I skimmed the source of next/image and it seemed like it wasn't configurable enough to remove the style data responsible for creating the independent stacking context. I found other bugs where the recommendation was to make the other element have a different position type, which I could not do because my dropdown requires `absolute`.

### What layout modes did you try?

I cycled through all of them, but I did not read the documentation on any of them ; I was sick of wrestling with z-indices and stacking contexts at this point and was just hoping one would insta-work.

### Did the experimental ”raw” layout mode solve your problem?  - If not, what changes would allow it to solve the problem?

`raw` layout worked as a drop-in replacement (visually).

### Do you have any additional feedback about the new mode or its documentation?

This worked as a drop-in replacement for my specific use case, so it's hard to say.

{clients.map((client, index) => ())}

If I provide the src property as an string referencing an external image from which I don't have the correct width and height values, it wont work because it'll refuse to compile if I don't provide that two parameters. Providing arbitrary values will make the image to resize to aspect-ratios other than its original. If I render the Image as an Static Import, but I don't provide any value to the sizes parameter, it'll cause the image to not fit correctly into my layout. If I use it as a Static Import, and provide a sizes parameter, it works as expected. > If not, what changes would allow it to solve the problem? I would expect a raw layout to not interfere with the underlying component and just provide the correct srcset and scaling

body: This isn't 100% related to the raw layout, but wanted to mention it here as I close out the discussion. We have extracted the core logic from `next/image` into a new `unstable_getImgProps()` function. This allows usage outside of ``, such as: 1. Working with [`background-image`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/background-image) or [`image-set`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/image/image-set) 2. Working with canvas [`context.drawImage()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Canvas_API/Tutorial/Using_images) or simply `new Image()` 3. Working with [``](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/picture) media queries to implement Art Direction or Light/Dark Mode images ### Example ```tsx import { unstable_getImgProps as getImgProps } from 'next/image' export default function Page() { const common = { alt: 'Hero', width: 800, height: 400 } const { props: { srcSet: dark } } = getImgProps({ ...common, src: '/dark.png' }) const { props: { srcSet: light, ...rest } } = getImgProps({ ...common, src: '/light.png' }) return ( ) } ``` PR: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/51205

body:
Hello!

You cannot fetch from **localhost** in production environment, you need to specify a valid URL.

If you want to call your API routes on the same app, you can simply omit the domain `axios.get('/api/auth/'`.

**Edit**: As icyJoseph pointed out, inside `getServerSideProps` you have to provide a complete URL.

But, you can directly get the infos from your server instead of calling an API route, since `getServerSideProps` is also called on the server and should have access to everything.
See: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props#getserversideprops-or-api-routes

> This is an unnecessary and inefficient approach, as it will cause an extra request.

API routes are more often called client-side, where omitting the domain is possible.

body:
Browsers have issues with scrolling iFrames and there is a "hack" to fix, but it needs the iFrame'd web page to return CORS headers.

This worked:
```
  async headers() {
    return [
      {
        source: '/',
        headers: [{ key: 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin', value: '*' }],
      },
    ]
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/api-reference/next-config-js/headers

Full context:
To address the issue where scrolling inside an iFrame doesn't continue to scroll the parent page once the bottom of the iFrame is reached, you can use JavaScript to detect the scroll event within the iFrame and then apply a scroll to the parent page. Here's a basic approach to achieve this:
Detect Scroll Event Inside the iFrame:
Add an event listener to the iFrame's content window for the 'scroll' event.
Check if the iFrame's content has been scrolled to the bottom.
Scroll the Parent Page:
Once the bottom of the iFrame is reached, trigger a scroll on the parent page.
Here is a sample code snippet to illustrate this:
javascript

``` 
   // Assuming you have an iFrame with an ID 'myiFrame' var iFrame = document.getElementById('myiFrame')
     // Ensure the iFrame is fully loaded before adding event listeners iFrame.onload = function() { var iFrameContent = iFrame.contentWindow || iFrame.contentDocument
     // Add scroll event listener iFrameContent.addEventListener('scroll', function() { var scrollTop = iFrameContent.pageYOffset || iFrameContent.document.documentElement.scrollTop
     var scrollHeight = iFrameContent.document.documentElement.scrollHeight
     var offsetHeight = iFrameContent.document.documentElement.offsetHeight
     // Check if scrolled to bottom if (scrollTop + offsetHeight >= scrollHeight) { // Scroll the parent page window.scrollBy(0, 10)
     // Adjust the value '10' as per the desired scroll amount } }) 
```
Remember to adjust the code according to your specific use case, especially the part where you decide how much to scroll the parent page. The value '10' in window.scrollBy(0, 10) is just an example and can be adjusted based on how smooth or fast you want the parent page to scroll.
**Additionally, be aware of cross-origin policies. If your iFrame is loading content from a different domain, you may face security restrictions that prevent access to the content of the iFrame from the parent page. In such cases, you will need cooperation from the iFrame content provider** (they would need to implement a postMessage system or similar to communicate scroll events to the parent page).

body:
Hi,

I don't know if this would help at all, but I could try and guess a few things. 

Let's, see it's been a while since Next 10.2.9, did you also upgrade to Node 15 around the same time? Perhaps something is going on with unhandled promises? Although those should break the entire application.

Do you use `getStaticPaths` with `getStaticProps` with fallback `true` to generate pages on demand, at runtime?

Are you using `next/image`? Maybe it is accumulating optimised images in memory?

Do you have any globals? Maps, Sets, arrays, plain JS objects that could potentially prevent garbage collection, or just keep on increasing memory consumption. I know this is a vague one, and hard to track, but still possible. 

Are you properly closing handlers to databases? outgoing requests, etc? In general, is asynchronous work properly finished, cleaned up and deleted?

I think you can check this out through `process._getActiveHandlers` and `process._getActiveRequests`. I don't want to straight up recommend packages, but there's npm packages that could help out debugging this section. There's also packages that can help getting heap dumps.

body:
I ran into this problem as well - pretty much the exact memory curve and behaviour - and took me ages to track down. 

Not sure this would be the same cause for everyone, but in my situation this stemmed from a combination an async call on the server side - to fetch auth data - and a health check configured in AWS.

The health check just gets the header response code, but doesn't load the site so the promise never settles.

Changing the health-check away from a page performing that async request - ie. the index page of the site - to a specific static health check page immediately resolved this problem.

I could be wrong about the cause described above, but as this is a no-code change it might be worth trying out to see if it alleviates the problem.

body: Hi, SPA routing is a bit of a make-belief situation. Most SPA techniques require you to use their anchor, to let their internal state keep track of pages, etc. In the Next.js case, the `next/link` component, needs to wrap an actual anchor tag: ```jsx anchor ``` And it will render an anchor tag with its `href`, with natural browser behavior, including context menus, etc. For example on MacOS cmd and tap on track pad opens links on a new tab, and the Link + anchor tag combo does just that.

body: Aha! You are misusing the link then. `next/link` has to wrap an anchor tag. What is your `next/link` component wrapping? How are you using it? If you are wrapping a custom component you need to use `passHref` in `next/link` ## **this is how you use the Link component!** it needs to wrap an `a` tag! ```jsx Hello ``` ## With a custom component ```jsx // It has to wrap an `anchor` tag! const RedLink = styled.a` color: red; ` function NavLink({ href, name }) { // Must add passHref to Link return ( {name} ) } ``` Again, this is because of how SPA routing works, read the documentation carefully https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link, the `` by itself will not work! You need to include an anchor tag as children. --- This is a screenshot of a site I made, the highlight is a `next/link`, you click it and it does the expected behaviour, if you right click on it, it opens the context menu. **Notice** that it has `children: ` If you could share some code snippet or reproduction to point the error that would be great. --- ## Edit I've tried out now, and I am pretty sure you are doing something like: ```tsx // ❌ ❌ ❌ ❌ Home ``` But should do this instead ```tsx // ✅✅✅ Home ```

body:
Personally, I wish this would be given a higher priority. 
Client side carts are certainly the best way to lock up a website while still allowing for access from anywhere. 

This is appealing for any private use website and business use cases. And, it could be potentially useful during development for when you don’t want anyone snooping your dev-website.

Without any support for this in next.js *vercel hoisting* becomes irrelevant… in addition to the inconvenience of using a custom server

Also, in addition to the suggested access / no access behavior of mTLS, it could also be interesting to use client certificates to serve different versions of pages than ones served to anonymous visitors. 
i.e. use client certs as an alternative method of authentication to logging-in (explicit username-password prompts).

author: alyssadicarlo

body:
Hey all, I haven't done any work towards this, but I have been able to make it work in an environment where TLS client certificate authentication is required:

- for incoming requests to your Next.js app, use an https proxy (like nginx or httpd) that requires client cert auth to my Next.js node container
  - for docker, I've done this using docker-compose where the only entrypoint into the docker network is through this proxy
  - for k8s, you can set it up so that your cluster ingress proxy requires client cert auth.  another option is to make your deployment run two containers in a pod, one being the proxy and the other being your node container
- for outgoing requests (using server components or pages router functions like `getServerSideProps()`) you can provide credentials to whichever http client you're using.

```ts
// alternatively you can use pfx + password as well here
const httpsAgent = new https.Agent({
  cert: fs.readFileSync(process.env.PUBLIC_CERTIFICATE_PATH),
  key: fs.readFileSync(process.env.PRIVATE_KEY_PATH),
  ca: fs.readFileSync(process.env.CA_CHAIN_PATH)
})

axios.get(url, { httpsAgent })
```

Keep in mind you'll be making this request with the server's TLS credentials.  If you are making a request on behalf of a user, you'll need to come up with some sort of mechanism to pass that credential along, like a custom HTTP header, or setting a cookie, both of which you can access with [`headers()`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) or [`cookies()`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies).

A downside is you can't use native `fetch()` on the server and [get the automatic caching and revalidation Next.js provides](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/data-fetching/fetching-caching-and-revalidating#fetching-data-on-the-server-with-fetch) because it's impossible to pass your TLS credentials to `fetch()`.  The API simply doesn't support it.  You can [cache responses yourself with React's `cache()` function](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/data-fetching/fetching-caching-and-revalidating#fetching-data-on-the-server-with-third-party-libraries) though
author: ealexhaywood

author: kchojhu

body:
I believe the Node.js's fetch() implementation is based on [`undici`](https://github.com/nodejs/undici), which you can pass a custom agent configured with a cert, key, and ca through their `dispatcher` option, however [you would need to install undici yourself](https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/48977#issuecomment-1658324420).  

The `dispatcher` option is non-standard though and may opt you out of Next's patched fetch, and [Node.js seems to want their implementation to remain true to the original browser-based Fetch API](https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/47592#issuecomment-1659853023).  I'd be curious to know if it works though, I haven't tried

author: Ren-Logronio

body:
When you build a Next.js application, actual HTML is generated, so at build time, the build step runs functions such as `getStaticProps`, to pre-render pages, it also tries to figure out which pages can be build already, and does it. It is an optimisation that's welcomed.

If you don't want that to happen, use `getServerSideProps` on the page that's trying to collect build time data, and then you should be fine. However, at run time, DATABASE_URL and all things necessary to render that page, better be present!

This is actually a good thing Next.js does with `getStaticProps`, it sort of type checks and makes sure things are in place do that it can work well in production. `getServerSideProps` runs on demand only, if at runtime there's no DATABASE_URL, the page will fail to render.

body:
Still, I think there's two equally right sides to this discussion, whether or not something is wrong with Next.js or are you doing something wrong. 

I'd like to really look into how can one build an application that forces `getServerSideProps` to run at build time.

Looking at the docs for when should `getServerSideProps` render

> -When you request this page directly, getServerSideProps runs at request time, and this page will be pre-rendered with the returned props
> -When you request this page on client-side page transitions through [next/link](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link) or [next/router](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router), Next.js sends an API request to the server, which runs getServerSideProps

I think that keeping things professionally is as important as understanding that, unless we can definitely prove that this is a major issue, that requires code changes, and not a user-land faulty implementation, we should not make any judgments on other people's actions, or lack thereof. 

I'd like to be able to boil this down to a minimal reproduction case, but none of the people with the issue is willing to offer one, or shade more light into how could we replicate the problem.

body:
It makes no sense to enforce this on build time when the backend is not there. If the backend is not there then there is no reasonable data and this is enforcing a design where people need to mock with crap all over the place just to get the build to work.

That is not how you warmup a cache. If there is no reasonable data then there should be no prerender. Render the first time when there is data and not earlier. This makes absolutely no sense and encouraging people to add boilerplate all over the place is not good design.

body:
> @ovidiuc Yeah, I am using something similar to that method.
> 
> Have you found a way to instantiate Prisma properly while also avoiding the "10 instances" warning?

@neongreen 
I'm replying outside of that thread to have my findings a bit more visible, Github interface collapses the newest discussion items.

My setup might not apply to you, I'm running `next build` from a `Dockerfile` that is executed by `docker build` within a Github Action runner.

The reason I was getting the `DATABASE_URL` was because I was setting the environment variable in my Github workflow file for the environment in which `docker build` was being ran, not for the `docker build` process itself. Found  2 solutions for this problem. 

* Dynamically create the `.env` file in the github workflow (https://stackoverflow.com/a/63350136/4724998):
```yaml
- name: Create env file
        run: |
          touch .env
          echo 'DATABASE_URL="${{ secrets.PRISMA_DATABASE_URL }}"' >> .env
```
I prefer this solution.

* or pass the the value as `--build-arg` :

```
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --build-arg DATABASE_URL=${{ secrets.PRISMA_DATABASE_URL }} -f Dockerfile.production .
```
and use it in `Dockerfile` to set the `ENV`:
```dockerfile
ARG DATABASE_URL
ENV DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL
```

**For the "10 instances" warning**, I've reverted back to the solution in the article I've mentioned, since I've fixed the environment variable issue:
```ts
// client.ts
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client'

declare global {
  // allow global `var` declarations
  // eslint-disable-next-line no-var
  var prisma: PrismaClient | undefined
}

export const prisma =
  global.prisma ||
  new PrismaClient({
    log: ['query'],
  })

if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') global.prisma = prisma
```

author: neongreen

body:
I hate to be repetitive, but that's just how modules work... this is not a Next.js issue.

Consider this CRA example: https://codesandbox.io/s/eloquent-grass-q7m9kq?file=/src/moduleA.js

Importing from `moduleA` causes the logs to happen, just like it makes it so that the `2+2` evaluates to `4`. 

I am not sure why is this so hard to understand, the top level of a module, is in a way, like the constructor of a class, as soon as you invoke the module, the top level code runs.

This is also observed in tests. If you import from `testA` into `testB` (a constant or helper), and run `testB`, then the entire `testA` suite runs too! Independently of the test runner!

Even simpler, consider these two files `moduleA.js` and `index.js`

```js
// moduleA.js
const value = 2 + 2;

console.log("Hi from Module A");

module.exports = {value};
```

and 

```ts
// index.js
const {value} = require("./moduleA");

console.log("Value:", value);
```

And guess what, running `node index.js` causes the console.log, from `moduleA` to print!

```shell
➜ node index.js
Hi from Module A
Value: 4
```

Does it start to make sense? It is important to get these concepts right!

author: jakekugel

body:
Nope, because that one fails on module loading - 

As the JavaScript module for `pages/index.js` is processed we see these two lines:

```js
import Head from 'next/head'
import clientPromise from '../lib/mongodb'
```

So we, eventually, step into `../lib/mongodb` and that module has a top level statement:

```js
if (!process.env.MONGODB_URI) {
  throw new Error('Invalid/Missing environment variable: "MONGODB_URI"')
}
```

And that throws right?

The legit question I take from this thread is, why does the file even execute? And, after all of this time, I think, I can perhaps give a better answer.

Let's side step to middleware matchers. As of time of posting, this section of the docs: https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/middleware, contains this bit:

> Note: The matcher values need to be constants so they can be statically analyzed at build-time. Dynamic values such as variables will be ignored.

This is because the file that contains the middleware is actually parsed into an AST tree, from where the `export const config = /* and so on */` bit is now a data structure, and that information is used to build the matcher. That's what the note means with `statically analyzed at build-time`.

What does this have to do with the main topic? Well that, in the pages directory things work a bit differently. As the builder walks each route, it loads the page module, and with its all of its dependencies, and then it takes the default export, config, and data-fetching functions, and uses those to see what type of page are we dealing with:

```js 
// ❌Pseudo-code

const {getStaticProps, getServerSideProps, getStaticPaths} = await import("/path/to/page");

if (typeof getServerSideProps !== 'undefined') { /* prepare an on-demand rendering bundle */ }

/* and so on... */
```

> https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/src/server/load-components.ts#L132

This is also done because, if you were to find a `gSP` function, you could call it and generate the initial JSON payload. Do remember that this was all made in Next 9.3, about 3-4 years ago, with a different tooling, and already building on the legacy of `getInitialProps`. 

Did I, somehow make it clearer at least? Or did I just blur it even more?

body:
Aha, NEXT_PUBLIC_ env vars are inlined at build time IIRC... You kind of have to remove that prefix. I'm always a bit doubtful about their server side behavior but, I think these are also inlined on SSR.

In general, you seem to understand the issue now, you just need to defer the reading of `process.env.YOUR_VAR` until runtime.

What the other person means, is to construct an object and reuse it, to minimize allocations and function calls whatever, but funny enough at build time Next.js may use many workers to build pages in parallel, and each would create their own JS context/environment. And that kind of, slightly, defeats the purpose. You can configure next.js to use one worker only, but like, first get a build running and a server working, worry about the fancy pancy details of patterns and architecture, right after that.

body:
It's not, the problem is how modules are loaded. Which is how JavaScript works. 

When the module is loaded the top level scope is executed. If you have statements/declarations that can't be worked during build time, or runtime for that matter, the module loading will crash.

body:
I need to use `fs`. My site is a web view into the NAS on my network that I can access from anywhere. I could've found an existing solution, but it gives me an opportunity to try out new [to me] frameworks, like next.js.

In cases where `getServerSideProps` is given a path that points to a directory, the page is returned, in HTML, for the user to view the contents of the folder. If the path points to a file, then the result should be the file itself

If I'm using `getServerSideProps` in a manner that wasn't intended, that's fine, my current workaround of having it send a redirect to an `/api` endpoint functions just fine. I'm not sure if this would make sense, but next.js could check to see if the response has ended after the call to `getServerSideProps` and, if so, essentially do nothing (trust that it has already been taken care of).

Normally this would be a perfect fit for middleware to make a decision, and if it is a file, issue a rewrite so that it is completely seamless to the browser, but my understanding is that I can't use modules like `fs` since, under certain deployments, the middleware is hosted elsewhere.

The only thing that would make my current work-around better is if `getServerSideProps` could do a rewrite and not a redirect, but based on what little knowledge I have, I suspect it's "too late" to do a rewrite at that point in time.
author: cbrianball

body:
Aha, now I understand, sorry if I was a bit dry on my answer.

Would it be okay to render the contents of a file inside an iframe? If you detect that it is a `file` path name you return in the props `url` to be used by the iframe to "download" the file contents to the client. 

Curious on how are you planning to handle binaries? If the files are only text based, you could read them and send the contents in the `getServerSideProps` response, and have conditional rendering on the client. 

Another thing would be, to check the extensions (?), or to include more info when you analyse a directory tree. I mean for example if you have `/documents`, and it has `/a`, and `/b.txt` to create a model:

```js
const current = {
  path: "/documents",
  contents: [
    { path: "/a", contents: [], type: 'dir' },
    { path: "/b.txt", contents: [], type: 'file' },
  ],
  type: 'dir',
};
```

Sort of putting the calculation upfront for depth 1 in the current directory. Edited to use `type` instead of `isFile`.

With this you could do the check in the middleware, by including the `type` as query/payload in the request, and do a rewrite for `file` types. A bit dirty solution, but perhaps you can polish it. Sadly direct navigation to a resource still needs the redirect. Interesting use case.

Thanks for asking this question. I noticed your [Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/nextjs/comments/tmg2ha/nextjs_preload_selfhosted_fonts_from_node_modules/) and [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71592690/next-js-preload-self-hosted-fonts-from-node-modules) posts also have zero responses.

I tried using the `file-loader` webpack plugin to import the font files in my JavaScript, hoping that the resolved file hash would be the same and I could use the imported source destination for my `<link rel="preload">` elements. However, the hash isn't the same as the hash generated by the Next.js webpack step that copies the font files to `.next/static/media`.

At this point I'm VERY close to just copying the fonts to the `/public` folder and copying the CSS into the project, so the font files don't get hashed. But this is a terrible solution.

It seems like Next.js should automatically preload any `@font-face` sources it copies to the output folder. This seems in-line with the performance goals of the project.

author: Dragate

body:
This.

I'd say that, while this is a valid approach to prevent divergent HTML, it is worth investigating the source of the divergence. Is it a Math.random call, a device width check on Server context? 

If it's really not possible to remove the source of divergence, then some fallback elements in combination with CSS, possibly with visibility hidden (not display none) to reduce layout shift, or plain skipping rendering of those sections and pay the layout shift price, ought to be considered.

At this point you might as well use `dynamic` with `ssr` set to false, and skip creating wrapper components, https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import#with-no-ssr.

body:
Hi,

This is a Node.js kind of thing that you should know, https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#responsesetheadername-value.

That being said, I wonder where does `response` come from in your case? and where does `res` come from? 

Headers is a Map, sometimes it can be plain JS object, but iirc, in api routes it is a map, and to get things from it you have to do `response.headers.get('location')`, which might be `null`, or contain the value. 

I am suspicious about the actual types of `res` and `response`, are you doing this in inside an `api/route`?

To be clear `.setHeader("key", "value")` should work 100%, if you manually set `location` to a value, it works right?

body:
I think this is a rather difficult (impossible?) thing to do: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/13387

From the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/custom-error-page#reusing-the-built-in-error-page)

> `pages/_error.js` is only used in production. In development you’ll get an error with the call stack to know where the error originated from.

Of course, 404's can be used to test the `_error.tsx` page by just going to a non existing route, but I image you want to test actual page failures. 

---

I think the idea behind this is that, if the error comes from rendering, a Boundary should catch it, and if it comes from `gSSP`, then you should formally handle it on the server.

I understand that, maybe people have logic and what not on the error pages, styling and such, that one wants to see and test, and this makes it rather difficult.

body: I was able to figure this out on my own. Basically the Image component was wrapping the image in a span that had position relative on it. This span was acting as the constraint rather than allowing my container to be the constraint for height/width values. I added some CSS to my container to basically override that span by selected the firstchild and changing the position property back to static(default) using the !important tag just incase as well. Presto it is working as intended.

body:
Hi,

Yes, that's how Vercel deploys work. I think we may be making assumptions here, let's boil things down to the basics.

- Ok, so all pages are rendered at build time
- You have connected your next.js repo to Vercel and have fixed the Vercel scripts at `https://vercel.com/<username>/<project-name>/settings`

> Build & Development Settings
>
>When using a framework for a new project, it will be automatically detected. As a result, several project settings are automatically configured to achieve the best result. You can override them below.

It should be set to Next.js

- Are all files needed for the build, available in the repo?
- When you deploy with Vercel, you can see the logs produced per deployment under the `Deployment Overview > Building` - There you should see a summary of the output:

```
Page                                                 Size     First Load JS
--
┌ ● / (ISR: 10 Seconds) (2091 ms)                    14.1 kB         133 kB
├   /_app                                            0 B             101 kB
```

Something like that, does it contain all of the pages you expect? Are there any logs?

author: pedrosodre

body:
We are battling with the same issue with nextjs `13.2.3` image server. We have Azure Front Door for CDN purposes in front of the image server. We have converted all the images to have header immutable header.

Even while the images are small there is delay (and we also have usage in locations other than our main regions from which we are hosting the images, including Australia and Japan), while servers are in US and Europe.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/363b0fb8-8cac-45dc-bbb2-9503672912e3)

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/51e0b0b4-0551-4cdd-a261-778d3641f0cd)

Azure Front Door shows average cache hit ratio of only 22 % for the past 30 days although our content does not change very often and the files are truly immutable. 

Front Door Byte hit ratio graphed here:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/51ebd85a-02e3-48ff-b543-e90a44ea3423)

I would say this should be a very valid use case to have that one wants to serve immutable files with proper cache-control directives (although next.js versions might affect quality of the results or what ever might be the reason not allow treating these as immutable). We have our own cache busting in place with images served from immutable paths and path changing on files being updated. And if there are really valid reasons that between nextjs versions the immutability is not good, then there should be possibility to add additional cache busting with query string altered based on nextjs cached image hash or something. And this support should be built in to nextjs it self.

body:
We have the same kind of setup, 

For our local env vars we have a .env in the root of our project, Then in the apps/ and packages/ we use env-cmd to start our apps dev build etc scripts like so

 "scripts": {
    "dev": "env-cmd  -f ../../.env --silent serverless offline start --verbose --reloadHandler",
    "start": "env-cmd -f ../../.env --silent serverless offline start",
    "deploy:app": "pnpm lint && env-cmd -f ../../.env --silent serverless deploy",
    "lint": "eslint '**/*.ts'",
    "lint:fix": "eslint '**/*.ts' --fix",
    "package": "env-cmd -f ../../.env --silent serverless package",
    "clean": "rimraf node_modules .serverless"
  },

the --silent flag makes sure that if no .env is present ( like on vercel etc) it skips the import and uses env vars in that system

and in our root we do the same with the turbo scripts just without the -f option because the .env lives there

body:
Hello!

If you use it like this, you'll most likely have a 
```
`destination` has segments not in `source`
```
error.

Because `redirects` expects a "path" matching. It's not aware of any "data object" that you could pass to it.

You'll probably need to manually redirect them.
If you have all of the redirections at build time, you could map through them and add them here.

author: 0x15BA88FF

body: No; svg images are vector graphics which are size independent. Unlike raster images like png/jpg/bmp/etc, they don't have an intrinsic size, and therefore 99% of next/image functionality doesn't apply to them. The lazy loading is the only real advantage I can see, but you can get this with `` without using `next/image`.
author: transitive-bullshit

body: oh sorry for the miss-information 😅, but overall I do recommend using next/image when creating next apps, they have other props for images like [placeholders](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#placeholder) which can be given a blur while loading, style, [objectFit](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#objectfit), [objectPosition](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#objectposition), [lazyBoundary](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#lazyboundary).. states like [onLoadingComplete](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#onloadingcomplete) and more handy stuff. it is also good to keep your code consistent but it is your choice to pick

body:
Hello!

You could use a query param for that.
If you don't want for it to appear on the url, you could also pair it with the [as](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link) property.

Something like:
```js
<Link
  href={{
    pathname: "/story/test",
    query: { loading: "on" },
  }}
  as="/story/test"
>
  Hello
</Link>
```

When clicking on the Link, you'll go to `/story/test` but will be able to retrieve the query param:

```js
const { query } = useRouter()

console.log(query.loading)
```

body: The problem with that is that you then need to add that tracking logic for every image you have loading.  I believe the context for a loading state should be built into the image component by default so that there is no external code needed to change the context of the image itself.  It would be more performant locally on the client by avoiding having an unnecessary download of a blurry image, it removes the need to have the server process the image twice (one blurry one not), and it would avoid having to have similarly matched sizes of the LoadingState to avoid layout shifts as that size is already built into the image size.

body:
Looks like your code/Firbease references `document` in a non-browser environment. Make sure it's not the case, as Node.js does not have `document`, hence the error.

If your code/library cannot run in Node.js, you can import the component using it dynamically with `ssr: false` so Next.js does not do a server pass on it: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import#with-no-ssr

body:
Late to the thread but figured this might help anyone still looking.
I've been using [i1n](https://i1n.ai) for my Next.js projects. It's CLI-first, you write your source strings and run `i1n push --translate` and it handles the AI translation + generates a `.d.ts` file with autocomplete for every key. If you typo a key, TypeScript catches it before production.

If you're already on next-intl or i18next, there's a Bridge Mode that wraps your existing setup with type safety. No migration, one line.

It also has an MCP server, so if you use Cursor or Claude Code you can tell your agent "internationalize this component" and it does the extraction, translation, and code rewrite. That part honestly saved me more time than I expected.

Open source (MIT): [github.com/Pakvothe/i1n-cli](https://github.com/Pakvothe/i1n-cli)
author: Pakvothe

body:
@thanhlmm I’ve tried a few of the i18n libraries with Next.js, and honestly the “best” one depends on what you value most. Here’s how I’d break it down based on my own experiments:

- **next‑intl**: This one feels the most natural if you’re using the App Router. It plugs right into server components, handles locale parsing, and keeps the bundle size small. For projects where you want tight integration and don’t need a huge feature set, it’s the smoothest option.
- **react‑i18next**: Very mature, tons of features (namespaces, lazy loading, advanced pluralization). It’s heavier than next‑intl, but if you’re building a large app with complex translation needs, it’s reliable and has a big community.
- **FormatJS / react‑intl**: Great if you need ICU message formatting (dates, numbers, plural rules). It’s a bit larger, but if your app has heavy formatting requirements, it’s worth it.
- **LinguiJS**: Nice developer tooling, message catalogs, TypeScript support. I used it in one project where we wanted strong CLI support for managing translations, and it worked well.
- **Rosetta**: Tiny footprint, very fast. I’d only recommend it for small apps or places where performance and simplicity matter more than features.

From my own projects:  
- For a modern Next.js app with App Router, I stick with **next‑intl** because it integrates cleanly and keeps things lean.  
- For enterprise apps with lots of translation complexity, **react‑i18next** is still my go‑to.  

So the criteria you mentioned — features, package size, performance — really do map to different choices. If you want “best integration,” go next‑intl. If you want “most features,” go react‑i18next. If you want “smallest size,” go Rosetta.

author: cjsjyh

body:
To late comers, I have realized that initial page chunk is the initial markup for fast page load or search engines and only initial view is included. However, Component's render result can change by user's interactions or various conditions which means that actual JS code is required to handle them. 

If your page is static like my example above, there won't be any point but it won't be the case for most web apps so that chunk is REQUIRED for your app to be fully functional.

body: my bad, I misinterpreted the documentation. Interesting enough, one could probably combine the file naming schema and the eslint rule project-wide, by adding specific eslint configuration based on a file-name pattern like shown here: https://stackoverflow.com/a/55184215
author: MauriceArikoglu

body:
Hello.

We created the ESLint rule to avoid confusions and get rid of most common reported errors.
`NextRequest` and `NextReponse` will only work for middleware, and not for pages or components (which was a misconception when the feature was released).

If you want to create functions in other files **intended for middlewares only** you could simply disable the eslint rule as you mentionned.

ESLint rules are there to help. If you know what you are doing, it's fine to disable a specific rule for a specific file.
author: thibautsabot

body:
I totally see where you're coming from, you want to hit the most common errors with the primary listing config. I also acknowledge that a totally valid solution to this problem is to refactor my function interfaces to not require `next/server` objects.

I don't understand where the harm is in allowing `import type` from outside middleware. From what I can tell, this creates a way to modularize middleware code without allowing `next/server` objects to be instantiated outside of the edge runtime.
author: davidhousedev

body:
It's just a way to notify people that these types should only be used for middlewares.

In your case, you could simply `// eslint-disable-next-line`
author: thibautsabot

body:
Hey!

The best way to know is always to try it out 😄.

But the way the build works, is that Next.js will look for every file inside your `pages` folder.
From there, it'll require every components needed and create a manifest with every JS chunks.

If your test file is not required for the page to work (and it shouldn't), the files should not be included in the destination folder.

author: mrmckeb

body:
Beyond consistency and avoiding divergent names for default exports, I'd also like to reduce the import bloat and pollution in Intellisense. Layouts and pages will rarely if ever be imported anyway.

The ideal solution imo would be if there was a named export in `page.js` or `layout.js` that was a reserved word for Next, e.g. `export function Page() { ... }` or `export function Layout() { ... }`

I think there is a problem because "+" is a special character used in regexp matching.

Could you instead use something like "%20"?

```
      {
        source:
          "/Area-Rugs/Result_N_4294962350%204294966291%204294955739_Ne_27.aspx",
        destination:
          "/Area-Rugs/Result_N_4294962350%204294966291%204294954922_Ne_214.aspx",
        permanent: true,
      },
```

works fine.

If you are interested at the code, it looks like it's this line: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/load-custom-routes.ts#L155 that is throwing an exception.

author: tavoyne

body:
There's one use case where a path looks like `[[...id]]`, and because we cannot bind TypeScript to the file name structure, we need to take the type that most correctly represents all use cases.

`post/[[...id]]` matches:

- post
- post/a
- post/a/b and so on.

So the `id` list could be `undefined`, and that's what the type represents.

body:
It's likely that you have a mismatch between the server output and the client.
Before React 18, you'd only get an error in your browser's console, but with the new major you will encounter that popup.

You should check the console for errors like:
```
Warning: Expected server HTML to contain a matching <div> in <div>.
```
and fix them.

I found the cause of this error. I'm using react-bootstrap and had a `
` element under a `` component, which showed an error in the console saying `Warning: validateDOMNesting(...):
cannot appear as a descendant of

.`. I've been developing with next.js with `reactStrictMode: true` since before the React upgrade and have simply ignored this error before. The Card.Text component can accept an `as="div"` prop to fix this. Another project of mine also has this error causing the hydration failed error: ``` Warning: Expected server HTML to contain a matching in

. a Anchor<@webpack-internal:///./node_modules/@restart/ui/esm/Anchor.js:33:7 NavLink<@webpack-internal:///./node_modules/react-bootstrap/esm/NavLink.js:22:76 Link@webpack-internal:///./node_modules/next/dist/client/link.js:115:23 ``` This was fixed by importing the component with no SSR dynamically as it is a component where a header is generated dynamically client-side and requires access to the window object in the browser.

author: psavan1655

body:
Take it easy with upgrading to React 18. 

In my case, I simply upgraded to React 18, fixed a dependency that did not work with React 18, and then my build pipeline worked.

I hope this is not a global thing. Rather I think it might be a project by project issue. 

Things to look out for:

- Are you breaking pure behaviour where expected? Use `strictMode: true` in `next.config.js`, and see if you get the same errors.
- Some of you dependencies might not be ready to work with React 18
- You may have "Divergent HTML" code, which before React 18 was silently working. 

Please do share as much info as possible, `next info` via `npx next info` should be at least included.

--- 

Edit: I am assuming here, that things work normally with React 17. Preventing divergent HTML is key to isomorphic/universal JavaScript, which is what we do with SSR (not only Next.js), the dry HTML rendered by the server has to match the client first frame.

body:
This is absolutely on the React side. You can prove this with most any responsive component utility. Just print a single piece of markup that will be one thing on the server and something else in the browser and you will see this hydration error. `
isDesktopOrLaptop: {isDesktopOrLaptop ? 'YES' : 'NO'}
` Since the server cannot determine the device or screen width without the browser the SSR page will render 'NO' but once it's in the browser it will render 'YES' and this causes the hydration error.

body:
My problems were all related to manipulating dates without taking time zones into account. The dates from raw data/APIs were being parsed/formatted into human-readable strings at build time on Vercel's server with its timezone (GMT probably?) and then the same process was happening on the client side with the user's timezone, resulting in different HTML.

So nothing "broke," it just seems React 17 didn't care about this. 😆

author: cellograph

body:
I feel compelled to point out (for anyone that ended up here from Google etc.) that these `if (typeof window === "undefined")` answers are not really "solutions" — they'll make the error go away but, at least in my case, the hydration errors that React 18 started complaining about were caused by _actual_ problems/mistakes/oversights in my code that were there the whole time, long before 18 came out. React just got "stricter" about pointing them out to us.

Checking if `window` is present kind of defeats the purpose of using Next, since the content will stop appearing in the actual HTML sent by the server, which is one of Next's biggest selling points! And as others have noted, it will **definitely** negatively affect SEO if you're not careful.

My advice is to look for _anything_ that could be different between the server (Vercel's, for example) and your or your users' browsers (different timezones with `new Date()`, languages/locale settings, etc.) or anything that could be different between buildtime and right now (outdated data from an API). For me, timezones were my biggest pain point. The `window` check should be the last resort IMHO, especially since separating out the problematic component and importing it with [`next/dynamic`](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import) + `{ ssr: false }` does the same thing but better. 

But to anyone more knowledgeable about this than me, feel free to correct any of this if I'm wrong! 😊

body: I think this error occurs because of the way server-side rendering occurs. Next js completely prepares the HTML page server-side before sending it to the client. If there is a library or piece of code that mutates the page after being sent to the clients then Next.js will throw this error in my case I was using trading view widgets which uses a script that renders the charts asynchronously. by using the script tag provided by next js to load the widgets  I was able to solve this problem.

author: drewbietron

body:
### **Fix for me** My issue was due to invalid markup. In my case it was throwing an error saying `
cannot appear as a descendant of

`. After fixing this the issue was solved. However, warning only shows in development for me. In production it was throwing the following three errors. -> 1) `There was an error while hydrating. Because the error happened outside of a Suspense boundary, the entire root will switch to client rendering.` 2) `Hydration failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server.` 3) `Text content does not match server-rendered HTML.` I guess my question has now become, why is it throwing this error just because of invalid markup? I'm all for fixing the markup within the application and making it compliant, but shouldn't the SSR and client response just spit out what you give it? If SSR spits out invalid markup, and the client spits out invalid markup, shouldn't they both match and this error isnt a thing? What part of the process has it manipulating the data returned so that they are mismatched?

author: JusticeBringer

body:
In my case, when I used router without waiting for server-side rendering, I was getting a similar error.

```jsx
  <LoggedIn needRedirect willSSR>
    {router.asPath === '/login?page=input_authcode' && (
      <LoginAuthcodeBody phone={''} />
    )}
  </LoggedIn>
```

**solution:**

Determine the status of the router with `isReady`

```jsx
  <LoggedIn needRedirect willSSR>
    {router.isReady && router.asPath === '/login?page=input_authcode' && (
      <LoginAuthcodeBody phone={''} />
    )}
  </LoggedIn>
```

author: Shin-Thant

body:
> Can you explain why? Pls...

because your declaration logic will be available until html element is rendered, mouted has default value false, when component returning some data it will check mouted, in this case for 1st time it will return null because mounted = false, and then when all html element is ready, useEffect will be trigger and set mounted to true , and in this situation your html and it logic will be available.

body:
In my case, I solved it as follows: I'm making a Comment component for my REDDIT-clone application And when I made the jsx to show all the comments under post, I had a small detail. The issue was that I was printing time.createdAt with a TimeAgo library, but apparently it was not synchronized with my local time, and in my database server, which is remote, it was with another time and in my local it was with 10 or 20 seconds late. `import Avatar from "./Avatar" import timeago from "lib/timeago" export default function Comment({ comment }) { return (

{comment.author.name} {" "} • {timeago.format(new Date(comment.createdAt))}

{comment.content}

) }` I had to format the Timeago and it stayed in a library like this: `import TimeAgo from "javascript-time-ago" import en from "javascript-time-ago/locale/en" TimeAgo.addLocale(en) const timeago = new TimeAgo("en-US") export default timeago` and after so many hours of debugging I was able to solve it!

I had a battle with these errors all week.

- I only got them production.
- I only got them when i was on a phone or was using the "emulator" in browsers.
- I fixed some locale / date related issues

But i got these errors on almost every pages still. Finally i just started to deploy new versions and managed to track it down to my images. 

And then i found this post 

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/28810

It wasn't the same issue but then i thought, what the heck, lets try to turn off "Mirage" in Cloudflare, and boom! Gone!

So if you get these errors and using Cloudflare take a look there.

body:
In my case a **firefox extension** was causing the issue! Specifically [firefox relay](https://relay.firefox.com/). When I **disabled the extension,** the hydration error went away.

Try **disabling any extension** that may be editing the webpage's HTML _(such as password managers, grammar checkers etc.)_.

#### Better solution
Disable SSR specifically for pages containing a form that firefox relay or other extensions may target.
see https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53139884/next-js-disable-server-side-rendering-on-some-pages

The error was happening on a login page, so the extension tampered with the HTML of the login form and therefore caused the hydration mismatch. The extension inserts a little icon next to an email `input`, which I think was causing the issue.

body:
Resolve React hydration error in a simple way 🎭

import "../styles/globals.css";
import type { AppProps } from "next/app";
import { useEffect, useState } from "react";

function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) {
  return (
    <👉Hydrated>
        << === Component {...pageProps} ===  >>
    <👉/Hydrated>
  );
}
export default MyApp;

const Hydrated = ({ children }: { children?: any }) => {
  const [hydration, setHydration] = useState(false);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window !== "undefined") {
      setHydration(true);
    }
  }, []);
  return hydration ? children : 
;
};
