author: hankbrigham

body:
We just encountered this issue so I did some digging. If using a third party styling library, in our case `styled-components`, and have a styled `<a>` inside `<Link />` you'll get the hydration error. However if you replace the styled `<a>` with a regular `<a>` you'll get the more useful error of:

`Error: Invalid <Link> with <a> child. Please remove <a> or use <Link legacyBehavior>.`

What's happening is that on the server the wrapping `<Link />` is being rendered as a sibling element to the styled `<a>`, but when rehydration happens the `<Link />` renders the `<a>` as a child element.

SSR:
![Screenshot 2022-11-17 at 12 03 54](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/55828757/202441563-83461308-071a-411e-a245-e8e375619f80.png)

After rehydration:
![Screenshot 2022-11-17 at 12 04 31](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/55828757/202441625-50e39b28-ecfd-4724-b7f3-5455fbaf6557.png)

Adding `legacyBehavior` to `<Link />` fixes the problem but it appears`legacyBehavior` isn't being respected when the child component uses a styling library.

UPDATE:

If you're not using `legacyBehavior` [this check in Link](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/link.tsx#L460) relies on the child element having the property `type: 'a'` however when investigating a styled component `a` it doesn't have this `type` property, instead it has `target: 'a'` property. As a result of this the correct error isn't being thrown and it falls through to the hydration issue.
author: JohnMarsden24

body:
It seems like it would be good to have a framework-level solution for this problem as there are always going to be differences between rendering environments on the server and client.

The only universal fixes involve doing an extra rendering pass on the client. If we're going to do that, why not have the framework automatically force a re-render in the event that a difference is detected? That would solve this once and for all instead of every user needing to find some bespoke way to do the same thing.

This would also solve the issue in cases where it's impossible for the site developer to solve them. e.g. When a browser extension has mutated the DOM in some way.

Is there an existing issue for this we could comment on or should we create one?

body: I am encountering this issue in attempting to implement theme toggle (dark mode) feature on my application. I am currently settling with [`next-themes`](https://github.com/pacocoursey/next-themes) after some [tweaks](https://github.com/pacocoursey/next-themes/issues/152#issuecomment-1319069123). However, since it is effectively applying attributes client-side after being rendered by the server, it still presents the hydration error (despite not having any FOUC when refreshing). Right now I'm settling with just using `suppressHydrationWarning` on my html tag element in layout.tsx (which only applies one level down) but it's not an optimal solution. Would be great if there was some framework or other solution to this as I can't think of any simple way of even adding a feature like toggling dark mode without the need for storing user preferences server-side or using cookies.

author: sksbkt

body:
From everything I read on X/Twitter and Github issues/discussions.
I really hope we'll have in future releases :
1. a way to be able to move actual errors logs such as : `Warning:` to "warning" level (console.warn)
2. a (better) way to debug hydration issues 
3. other alternatives than using `next/dynamic` with ssr false or `suppressHydrationWarning`

In many cases, I feel like it's a good thing to have `something` close to what I'll render from the server first, `then` update it at "client phase".
So maybe the way to go is using more `Suspense` stuff or idk, but it would be very cool to be able to tell `next` "it's okay, this is not the way you want this to go, you want the content not to change but I know what I'm doing" kind of stuff.
You would say "this is exactly what `suppressHydrationWarning` is for", but in reality, it's very difficult to find the right place to put it 
 to remove all warnings and it's annoying to add custom attributes to ignore errors everywhere.

To rephrase, it would be cool to have a way to tell "the content of 'this' is going to change at hydration" instead of "suppress any error hydration related coming from this element" without messing with imports or adding custom attributes.
I don't know what the solution could be, maybe a new helper component from `next` similar to `Suspense` in some way?
But I definitely think it's an important matter to address, I feel like people get really lost when they see these errors.

I really like RSC stuff, thanks for the hard work on Next otherwise.

https://twitter.com/search?q=nextjs%20hydration%20errors&src=typed_query

Solution: 

from:
`export default YourPage;`

to:

```

.
.
.
.
export default dynamic(() => Promise.resolve(YourPage), { ssr: false });

body:
Error: A tree hydrated but some attributes of the server rendered HTML didn't match the client properties. This won't be patched up. This can happen if a SSR-ed Client Component used:

- A server/client branch `if (typeof window !== 'undefined')`.
- Variable input such as `Date.now()` or `Math.random()` which changes each time it's called.
- Date formatting in a user's locale which doesn't match the server.
- External changing data without sending a snapshot of it along with the HTML.
- Invalid HTML tag nesting.

It can also happen if the client has a browser extension installed which messes with the HTML before React loaded.

https://react.dev/link/hydration-mismatch

-                       data-new-gr-c-s-check-loaded="14.1215.0"

body:
Hello!

It's hard to tell if it's a good thing or not, depending on the rest of the chunks.

On next 10 it seems that styled-components is not bundled inside "_app".
Are you using it on every page (I guess yes)? If so, an other chunk must contains the SC code somewhere else.

To make a good comparaison, I can suggest looking at the `.next/build-manifest.json` file after a build.

This file will contains a list of chunks needed for each page.
Something like:

```js 
"pages": {
    "/": [
      "static/chunks/webpack-05b8c18cdbe0b397.js",
      "static/chunks/framework-5f4595e5518b5600.js",
      "static/chunks/main-e2bb8fbd7686a5a4.js",
      "static/chunks/pages/index-cdd3bd45ef7a3766.js"
    ],
    "/_app": [
      "static/chunks/webpack-05b8c18cdbe0b397.js",
      "static/chunks/framework-5f4595e5518b5600.js",
      "static/chunks/main-e2bb8fbd7686a5a4.js",
      "static/chunks/pages/_app-54122c0fcb6b493f.js"
    ],
    "/hello": [
      "static/chunks/webpack-05b8c18cdbe0b397.js",
      "static/chunks/framework-5f4595e5518b5600.js",
      "static/chunks/main-e2bb8fbd7686a5a4.js",
      "static/chunks/pages/hello-cc430a3c6263c527.js"
    ]
  }
```

I'd compare the **total** of all chunk weight for a giving page with both Next 10 and Next 12.
For exemple, I'd add `webpack-05b8c18cdbe0b397.js`, `framework-5f4595e5518b5600.js`, `main-e2bb8fbd7686a5a4.js` and `pages/hello-cc430a3c6263c527.js` to get the total weight of the "hello" page.

Then I'd do the same for _app and see if it's an overall good change or not 🙂.

body:
We use Output File Tracing as well and we had errors when setting up NextRelic. NewRelic has two packages you need to install: `newrelic` and `@newrelic/next`. The former is required in my `_document.tsx` `getInitialProps` function because I need it to inject the web agent script tag in the HTML document thus it gets included in the `standalone` folder. I tried to use the `unstable_includeFiles` page config to include the latter inside my `page/index.tsx` as:

```ts
export const config: PageConfig = {
  unstable_includeFiles: ["node_modules/@newrelic/next/**/*"]
};
```

But I get an error `EISDIR` as `next` tries to use `copyFile` on a directory:
```
> Build error occurred
[Error: EISDIR: illegal operation on a directory, copyfile '/home/user/frontend/node_modules/@newrelic/next/lib' -> '/home/user/frontend/.next/standalone/node_modules/@newrelic/next/lib'] {
  errno: -21,
  code: 'EISDIR',
  syscall: 'copyfile',
  path: '/home/user/frontend/node_modules/@newrelic/next/lib',
  dest: '/home/user/frontend/.next/standalone/node_modules/@newrelic/next/lib'
}
```

My workaround for now is to actually load also `@newrelic/next` inside my `getInitialProps` in `_document.tsx` as:
```ts
require("@newrelic/next")
```
This way it gets included by `next`.

body:
Hello!

You can't select which version of Next.js will be used even when specifying a create-next-app version.
It's because the CLI will download and extract the code directly from the repo (of the canary branch).

The CLI version is **not** related to Next.js version.

See https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/create-next-app/create-app.ts#L139 and https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/create-next-app/helpers/examples.ts#L95

author: q11070

body:
To add an @q11070 's answer, the file generated depends on version of `create-next-app` (i.e. `npx create-next-app@12`). There are some breaking changes between major versions, so it is necessary to generate file of specific version.

For example, if you want to create next12 application, you should run 

`npx create-next-app@12 <app-name> && cd <app-name> && npm i next@12`

body:
Hi,

We did find a solution for this back in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/36754, it even ended up on an issue on React's repo.

Long story short, any form of invalid DOM nesting causes React to throw on the server, and that ends up as mismatched HTML between server and client.

It was also a problem on React 17, and before, but it never exploded like this because the of the rendering root. Nowadays, this produces a double error (first one is a warning/error about DOM nesting), in which the latter is fatal.

body: Hi, First of all, I'd say it's best to wrap an anchor tag: ```jsx My Link ``` Second, if you analyse the DOM, even with JS disabled, you'll see than an actual `a` tag with `href` is rendered. What does this imply? - Natural browser behaviour, that is, clicking on a link, takes you to the page. - Next.js has the opportunity to do an "SPA" like transition What does this imply, if JS is disabled? - Natural browser behaviour, that is, clicking on a link, takes you to the page. - Next.js doesn't have the opportunity to an "SPA" like transition This means, that the entire page is destroyed, and navigating to the clicked page is an entirely new request. To convince yourself of this, open the `devTools`, and in `console`, select `preserve logs`, and see that with JS disabled, clicking on the link logs: > Navigated to http://localhost:3001/my-page But, with JS enabled, nothing is printed, but the page "transitions" anyway. That's the SPA like behaviour of Next.js, that it can't do, if JS is disabled/blocked. To sum up: - Clicking `a` tags will always work (as long as there's an `href`, because that's just how browsers work) - Interactivity depends on JS (SPA like page transitions, etc.) ## What the docs say: It is important to carefully read every sentence in the docs: > - Enable JavaScript in your browser and [check out this page](https://create-react-template.vercel.app/). This is a plain React.js app > built with [Create React App](https://create-react-app.dev/). > - Now, disable JavaScript and access [the same page](https://create-react-template.vercel.app/) again. > - You won’t see the app anymore — instead, it’ll say “You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.” This is because the app is not > pre-rendered into static HTML. This part is actually, making the same point I am making. A `Create React App` site, with JS disabled, won't navigate! I think you must have missed that part. The docs are saying that, the benefit of Next.js is that even with JS disabled, things will work (for the most part)! 🎉 🎉

body:
Still not enough info, but much better. My guess:

- `child_process: false`, I think the problem might be here. You need to reorder your imports, so that `child_process` is not needed by the client, at all, because it just doesn't exist on the client, and that you can build without this hack.

A couple of additional things there:

- Using ~ or ^ to mark dependencies could already be problematic. Not everyone respects semver. I think this is not the issue.
- You have `react-scripts` also installed
- Although, for web application, everything is really a devDependency, it is good to divide these into, those that will end up in the web application `dependencies`, and the rest `devDependencies`. 


What node version are you using? And is it possible for you to organise your imports/exports so that `child_process` is no longer needed? Or is that needed because of a library you use?

body:
Do you have anymore information about what you are trying to do? I just spinned up a project with `12.1.1` and this in `.env`:

```
SOME_ENV_VAR=021929202
```

And either `dev` or `build` work fine.

How does the `.env` file look like? (remove secrets!) - and how are you using the env variables?
author: icyJoseph

body:
Hi @icyJoseph,
I tried `12.1.1` with this .env and get the error:

```env
SIGN_IN_VALIDATION="^.+@domain\.com$"
NEXT_PUBLIC_CDN_DOMAIN="cdn.domain.com"
```

It throws an error even though I haven't used any vars at all.

I realized that `12.1.0` can load the env vars in `next.config.js`, but it doesn't since `12.1.1`
author: hckhanh

body:
## Solution

I found the solution here: https://github.com/motdotla/dotenv-expand/issues/65

You need to escape the `$` in `SIGN_IN_VALIDATION`:

```
SIGN_IN_VALIDATION="^.+@domain\\.com\$"
```

Just make sure, it still works as expected.

---

## Investigation

I see, that information was key, should've included that in the original report.

I found this commit https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/34765 that was included in `12.1.1`, where `dotenv-expand` was upgraded from `5.x.x` to `8.x.x`.

So the minimal reproduction is:

1. `yarn create next-app test-app`
2. `cd test-app`
3. `yarn add next@12.1.1 --exact`
4. Create `.env` and fill it with `SOME_ENV_VAR="^.+@domain\\.com$"`
5. `yarn build` outputs the error.

~~Now we must think, what part of the dry REGEX must be culprit. With this information you should be able to re-open a bug report, I think.~~

icyJoseph

body:
Real Experience Score is an interesting metric indeed, I've seen mine be stable at 100 for days, and then suddenly a 7. 

- Lighthouse is a tool that goes against a check list, and that takes various time/load measurements on the site. By this very fact, it is dependent of the configuration with which it is run. On one of my laptops, I have to dwarf the CPU to x8, while in the other, to x2.
- With Lighthouse you can sort of, try to setup your site for success, but it doesn't mean it will be successful in terms of performance.
- If your site is mostly pre-rendered, the next.js server is pretty much just passing down HTML + bundles, and that shouldn't be so hard on it, even if a bot is trying to scrap so much. Usually bots don't have a runtime either, so I guess very few calls are actually made to API routes. If you do have ISR or gSSP, then I'd expect some heavy-load from a scrapper bot.
- I would think that the geographic location of users, specially in mobile, does affect the site metrics a lot. 

However I'd also like to hear about your FCP, LCP, CLS, and FID (if any).
author: icyJoseph

body:
Thanks for the input @icyJoseph 

The majority of the site is pre-rendered. We do, however, have pages with dynamic content where I'm using ISR given the number of pages that would be required to create at build time. 

FCP typically measures below 2s, with a few spikes going up to to wild numbers. I assume this is due to visitors searching on machines with poor performance/connection speeds or mobile in spotty service areas. 

LCP is typically within a healthy range, but still suffers from random spikes going to silly numbers that just don't seem to make a whole lot of sense (These occur more infrequently). 

CLS is close to, if not always 0 as of late. 

FID is typically in the single digits (last check shows an average of 7 ms)

body:
Hi, I have figured out the issue.
`LoginForm.tsx` was inside my `features/LoginForm.tsx` folder.

After adding `tailwind.config.js`

```JS
content: [
    './pages/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}',
    './components/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}',
    './features/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}',
  ],
  ```
  It works as expected now.

author: Nutlope

body:
Hi,

I think you are a bit confused. ISR basically takes note of the page definition, and re-runs it, server side, as needed. 

That's the same for simple revalidate, the `getStaticPaths` + `getStaticProps` combo, or manual `unstable_revalidate`. 

Why would there be any difference, between what you push to GitHub or the production server? Are you committing the outputs of running `next build`? ISR doesn't change the source code of the application.

The entire point of ISR is that the most recent build of any page, contains the latest, most up-to-date data, but that's up to the 3rd party providing such data. So that, if we run your GitHub code, after you've deployed, we should see, an even more up-to-date version of you data.

As to where it is saved, I do believe it is somewhere in `.next`, there's also a flag to control the size of the `ISR` cache, `isrMemoryCacheSize?: number`.

I ecountered the same issue. It happens when there is a **suspense** component above a page and the site in in **build** mode.
When I **remove all suspenses** from my layout, the metadata moves to the head.
To do this, I have to disable the **dynamicIO** experimental feature, what demands me to use suspense. 
As a side effect, I cannot use the cache tags.

In short:
Remove all suspenses above the page.jsx files, remove dynamicIO from next.config.mjs and remove all cache tags.

This appears to be a bug and I think it's the task of nextjs to fix it.

body:
Hello!

Yes they are regexp, you can find the related code here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/load-custom-routes.ts#L360

It's using [`matchAll`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/String/matchAll).

Value is `string | undefined`.
If it's a string, it'll be used as a regex to capture a specific part of the value.
If it's not defined, it'll match [anything](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/load-custom-routes.ts#L356).

body: Ok so I figured out my error, I was trying to revalidate a "new" page, but that's actually not necessary. Instead I needed to set my `getStaticPaths()` fallback to 'blocking' instead of `false`. Hope this will help someone else learning about next.js!

Hello @LoaiDev 

Looking at the output of `next export`'s `build/` folder:


1. The produced `index.html` pages contain a unique footer that dictates the page to render (see below).
2. The file `_buildManifest.js` (under `build/_next/static/…/`) contains a list of all the routes, and the JS files that need to be loaded to handle/render that page.

```html
    <!-- from index.html / [resourceId].html file -->

    <script id="__NEXT_DATA__" type="application/json">{
      "props": {
        "pageProps": {
          "statusCode": 404
        }
      },
      "page": "/path/to/some/resource/[resourceId]",  <<<<<<<< THIS HERE
      "query": { },
      "buildId": "KWMalEB3hB-Ya5uRudhT0",
      "nextExport": true,
      "isFallback": false,
      "gip": true,
      "scriptLoader": []
    }</script>
```

```javascript
    // from _buildManifest.js
    return {
        "/":                                                ["static/chunks/pages/index-b5903b905e5cf194.js"],
        "/_error":                                          ["static/chunks/pages/_error-9ba256efc4caf4b0.js"],
        "/path/to/some/resource/[resourceId]":              [
            I,
            T,
            "static/chunks/pages/path/to/some/resource/[resourceId]-9782bd746bb74d83.js",
        ],
```

An alternative, for NextJS, would have been to produce a single `index.html` (duplicated everywhere), that instead uses `window.location` (not `__NEXT_DATA__.page`) to figure out which `_buildManifest.js` item to use.

so: 
* window.location  = `https://your-site.com/path/to/some/resource/123`
* -> resource path = `/path/to/some/resource/123`
* -> match in _buildManifest = `/path/to/some/resource/[resourceId]`

and if no match is found: render _buildManifest item `/_error` (ie: the 404 page)

body:
For the env use `process.env.NODE_ENV`, and match it against, development, production or test.

For the domain name, it is slightly trickier. Servers, don't usually know the domain name of the site they are serving. In fact, white label solution servers might be serving several pages! 

So either, you hard code it at build time, for example Vercel deploys may use `VERCEL_URL`, as per [docs](https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/projects/environment-variables#system-environment-variables). Other services might offer a similar solution.

Or you can render all pages on demand and check the incoming request to derive the domain name.

Or, you could develop a 3rd party service (or API route perhaps), which through a query, can respond with different images. (Less preferable).

body:
Isn't there like a `Don't show again for session` option?

I disagree very much with your observations though. Hot reloading is not equal to full refresh. A full refresh is quite the contrary, it means that because a little thing changed, we ought to destroy the entire page, as opposed to hot swap the changed area. And for about 2 decades, iirc the first time I saw hot module reloading was in 2015, with redux reducers. 

Try with a more recent version of `Next.js` I have a feeling `12.0.10`, might have had a bug https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/31827 regarding the overlay.

You're correct, I confused my reloading models. Let's call it 'browser updating' more generally.

When Next figures out a full refresh is needed to update the browser, why ask? If it fears to reload "too much" (so timidly warns) I am no longer getting the benefit of any auto updating. If it reloads "too much", it keeps me productive. Please, reload too much when you detect it's necessary!

author: icyJoseph

body:
No, you shouldn't need to set fallback='blocking' if you are on NextJS 12. See the link I included.

The reason it is not working is because NextJS uses a hardcoded list of bots to test against. It will not know bots like Elasticsearch, Nutch, etc.
author: st11x

body:
Ah right, I misunderstood your use case, because there's a mistake on the link you've included, at least a diction mistake. I like the statement from the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-true) much better:

> The paths that have not been generated at build time will not result in a 404 page. Instead, Next.js will serve a [“fallback”](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-pages) version of the page on the first request to such a path. Web crawlers, such as Google, won't be served a fallback and instead the path will behave as in [fallback: 'blocking'](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-blocking) 

That being said, for your use case, like you say, unfortunately, the `isBot` function in the codebase does have a hard coded list, which makes your solution look like the best fit as of now.
author: icyJoseph

body:
Hello.

These two properties will have to different output!
When you open a web page, the browser requests the HTML document from a server, parses its contents, and submits separate requests for any referenced resources.

If you are using `loading="eager"`, the [eager](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/img#attr-loading) property will be passed to the `img` tag, downloading it as soon as it is detected in the HTML code.
In opposition, the default loading strategy is `lazy`, delaying the loading of the image until it is visible in the viewport. 

For the `priority` attribute, Next.js will add a `<link preload>` tag [at the top](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/image.tsx#L823) of your page.
Preload lets you tell the browser about critical resources that you want to load as soon as possible, before they are discovered in HTML.

While both of these methods fetch the image before they are visible, `priority` will be more "aggressive" and this is why the Next.js [documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#priority) suggests using it for LCP images.

You might be interested by these articles: 
https://web.dev/preload-responsive-images/
https://web.dev/preload-critical-assets/
https://web.dev/browser-level-image-lazy-loading/
author: thibautsabot

author: dhorkin

body:
How did you try to use `jQuery`?

Often I have 3rd party providers that themselves load `jQuery` and things work just fine, of course, we don't use `jQuery` on the server ourselves. The result of server side rendering is the so called dry HTML, which is a string. To try and manipulate the DOM, on the server with `jQuery` shouldn't work I think. It constitutes unguarded DOM access, akin to reading from window directly.

Inside `useEffect`, event handlers and most promises, you should be fine though.

That being said, Next.js builds on React, which is what you should be using to construct your DOM. Mixing paradigms, and ownership of the DOM can only result in mysterious bugs, and hard to maintain code.
author: icyJoseph

body:
Doing this is not trivial, I think.

If we break down the problem, first of all, you have a source string that ought to be modelled as objects. Second, you need to walk these objects to render them as React Elements. Third, whenever you encounter an `a` tag, you'd return a `next/link` wrapping the `a` tag. 

You could experiment with something like https://www.npmjs.com/package/html-react-parser - which at the cost of many kb's can help you out (10 kb according to https://bundlephobia.com/package/html-react-parser@1.4.10)

Matching html with regex is very much not recommended. 

Taking html strings is always a matter to be treated carefully. XSS is nasty. I am not sure about the source of your string, but if you could model it as objects (nearly like React object, with children an all and prop-like attributes), you'd be doing yourself a favour.

body:
Hi,

The problem is that at runtime Next.js wraps its Router Context Provider around your app. For debugging in production using React Dev Tools, it is good to be able to figure out where this Provider is, usually the 1st Provider from the top. 


To your question, you need to mock the router, for quick stuff I use https://www.npmjs.com/package/next-router-mock. Because, as you say this makes sure the router works, and then I just test my UI. This is akin to trying to test a component that uses `dispatch` from Redux without having a Redux Provider on the tree. It doesn't matter if its `jest`, the browser, or any other runtime, there must be a Provider somewhere, unless you mock the library.

This discussion contains a few more options: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/23034.

body: If you have a rewrite in middleware adding the current host you will need to do the same when calling `res.revalidate()` e.g. `res.revalidate('/_sites/curretHost/${pathname}')`. An example of this can be seen in the platforms starter project: https://github.com/vercel/platforms/blob/14a8098ea765560858e81f9629e19780e3ae013c/lib/revalidate.ts#L8

body:
Why would a page need to have that prop type built in? Are you trying to do something like this? ```tsx // pages/another-page.tsx import MyPage from './pages/my-page' const AnotherPage = ()=>
Injecting content
 export default AnotherPage; ``` While that's technically possible, it seems prone to grow in complexity. A `NextPage` conceptually is a top level abstraction that represents an entire route/page, and it's not necessarily a React Component. Why not remove the `NextPage` type, and move that to be a `Layout` Component, living for example in `components/`, and then use it as such, a Layout component, across pages. The docs document a case that resembles my assumption, https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/layouts#with-typescript.
author: icyJoseph
gd-cho

body:
In this case, `NextPage` is not the type you want. Either do: ```tsx import type {ReactNode} from 'react'; type LayoutProps = {children?: ReactNode} const Layout = (props: LayoutProps) =>
{props.children}
``` Or just use `FC` ```tsx import type {FC} from 'react'; const Layout: FC = (props) =>
{props.children}
``` Here's a bit more to read: https://www.carlrippon.com/react-children-with-typescript/ - but the learning is that `NextPage` is not a type to compose. It is something that Next uses as handover type between React components and their routing system. In practice a NextPage cannot really have children coming through props. NextPage's can only have serialisable data coming in as props.

author: thibautsabot

body:
Okay, so was able to do some troubleshooting. Based on some things I read on the web, it looked like certain scenarios disabled Content-Length when encoding (gzip, br, etc) was used. My axios call in the client side code is populating those values by default (seems sensible), and whenever the `Accept-Encoding` header is sent, the response comes back _without_ `Content-Length`.

When I send a hand-rolled request to my API without `Accept-Encoding`, the response includes `Content-Length`.

author: smo043

body:
Hello!

I'm not sure to fully understand your issue.
`getStaticPaths` will be ran only once. It'll return every pages that needs to be statically generated.

However, `getStaticProps` has to be called for **every single pages mentioned in `getStaticPaths`**. Next.js needs to pre-render all of them.
You can't bypass this as this is the expected behaviour.
If you want your build to be fast, you'd need to generate less pages (5000 is a lot).
author: thibautsabot

body:
You can use ISR, and generate only the most important pages in the build stage, using `fallback: true` or `fallback: "blocking"`.

`next/link` will eagerly generate the pages using this technique.

If someone comes to a page, when using `fallback: true`, the non-bot users will see a fallback for a moment. Otherwise, "blocking", waits for the rendering to happen.

See what I did here to generate a page fro each Pokemon, but only generate 3 at build time: https://github.com/icyJoseph/next-js-react-gbg/blob/main/demo-app/src/pages/pokemon/%5Bid%5D.tsx.

body: Nice chatgpt response, but `process.cwd()` will be the same across different functions because `npm` sets it to to the root of the project.
author: GabenGar

body:
@GabenGar 

- Firstly, Node.js sets `process.cwd()`.
- My English is not very strong, so I rewrote my answer with the help of GPT.
- **Vercel with Next.js** examines static `process.cwd()` calls in your files to determine what to include in the serverless build. While the result of `process.cwd()` remains consistent across different functions, it’s crucial to use it in the same function where it’s needed. If you pass it between functions, you may encounter errors when trying to read files because serverless functions do not allow reading files from the filesystem.

author: GabenGar

body:
Hi,

What do you mean with `"recompiles"`?

API routes are serverless functions, which are spin-up and down, as needed. This is done in order to consume as little resources as possible and prevent memory leaks. You should not assume that API routes, are the same instance through time. 

See this video for a more versatile explanation: https://youtu.be/jv4Soz08gBw?t=1315

I am not sure if you can tell the next server to try and keep these alive for as long as possible, but even if you could, if you deploy to Vercel for example, they'll timeout and be shutdown. I think the same applies for AWS amplify and others.

I think you may need to look into how to externally resolve the api routes. I haven't done this myself, but at the very least the API route needs to export configuration, like so, [from the docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/api-middlewares#custom-config):

```ts
export const config = {
  api: {
    externalResolver: true,
  },
}
``` 

Here's an [example](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/main/examples/custom-server-express) using `express`.

I'd suggest trying to design your application so that these metrics are not stateful, or could be stored elsewhere, and do not depend on ever running serverless functions.

author: chipit24

body:
Hello!

Runtime configuration is useful when you want to inject env variables **after** you built your application.
Sometime you want to only build your app once and deploy it to multiple environments (staging, production, etc..).

In this case, runtime variables will be used. You'd need to opt out of Automatic Static Optimization because you don't want to use the variables that were present at build time.

If you chose to build your application for each environment (which is the expected way) you don't need runtime config at all and can directly use https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/environment-variables
author: thibautsabot

body:
> inject env variables after you built your application ... You'd need to opt out of Automatic Static Optimization because you don't want to use the variables that were present at build time.

Isn't this a contradiction? If you use runtime config you need to opt out of SSG, but if you opt out of SSG and use SSR, then you might as well just use env vars. No?

Locally, I set `NODE_ENV=production`, ran `npm run build` and then `npm start` (to get a prod build running), and I'm seeing my public runtime config with no issues! This seems to contradict what the docs mention about needing to opt out of SSG to use runtime config. I suppose that part of the docs has been left to get stale 🤷
author: chipit24

body:
What the docs mean is that you can't leverage SSG and inject environment variables after the app has been built.
If you use SSR on everything, then yes you can directly use env vars 🙂.

author: cristian-eriomenco

body:
Hello!

I'm not sure where this "10x" is coming from ^^.
Next.js does make your life way easier, no doubt about it.

If you try to handle SSR and SSG by hand (which is only a small part of what Next.js can do), you'll probably end up with a super complex and "flaky" codebase.
You also have TypeScript, CSS-in-JS, CSS Modules, Webpack config, etc... out of the box.

This is a great abstraction to start fast and not waste any time with tool configuration.

For performance, you got automatic Image optimisation, code splitting, auto polyfill etc..

And most importantly, you have a great community and a core team dedicated to innovation and improvements!
author: thibautsabot

body: No, but it makes writing hybrid applications at least 10x less annoying. If you want to have server and client render logic within the same codebase you'll either have to make major tradeoffs or write a ton of server boilerplate from scratch.
author: GabenGar

body:
Hello!

I'm assuming you mean `getStaticProps` and not `getInitialProps`.

Also, your `getStaticPaths` methods returns an empty array for paths, meaning that **0** pages will be pre-rendered.
It'll behave like server side rendering, see: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-blocking

You mentioned that the `postData` is present inside the JSON data, which a good start.
Could you share the rest of the component (how you are using) these data?

Thank you.

author: Peluboy

body:
I'm not the authority on this nor have the right words, but loosely ...

create-react-apps have an index.html file in the public folder because they require a starting point on the client side to load the app into the browser.

next-js apps don't require the index.html file because the app is created 'server side' in /pages/index.js -  the browser pulls down a completed page (like a normal static html site)

So if you have installed next.js in an existing create-react-app you'll need to remove the index.html from the public folder because the browser will pull that file first and ignore the /pages/index.js file. Giving you a blank page.

body:
For a while, I encountered a consistent issue where some users could view my MERN stack app deployed on Vercel, while others complained of a blank page. I was able to resolve this issue by following these steps:
1. I added the following code to the app's BrowserRouter component: `<BrowserRouter basename="/">`. This helped to ensure that the app's routes were correctly resolved.
2. I removed the "homepage" attribute from the frontend package.json file. This attribute can sometimes cause issues with app deployment on Vercel, and removing it can help to resolve such issues.

Hello!

The prefixes are called "hashes" and are generated by Webpack to avoid any kind of a collision between filenames.

When using an image with [next/image](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image) for example, you don't have to provide this hash.

See https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/build/webpack-config.ts#L1588 and https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/build/webpack/loaders/next-image-loader.js#L16

author: GabenGar

body:
Say for example that you have to render `/pokemon/[id]`, seeing that there's 800+ Pokemon's (last time I checked), that'd be a really long build time. 

We can do the following (ISR):

1. Return 3 (absolutely arbitrary number) paths from `getStaticPaths`, for Pokemon's 1, 4 and 7.
2. Include the `fallback: true` or `fallback: "blocking"`, props in the `getStaticPaths` response
3. Make sure `getStaticProps` handles `notFound` or `redirect` cases for malformed paths, or non existing data (apply revalidate to `notFound` as well)
4. Deploy your app
5. A request arrives for Pokemon 300, which you have not generated in step 1
6. ISR kicks in because of `fallback` being set in step 2
7. A page for 300 is generated and from now on, everyone arriving to it will immediately see it
8. If you render a page with a bunch of `pokemon/[id]` hrefs using `next/link`, these are also generated 

You can take a look at this demo I built for a MeetUp on my city https://github.com/icyJoseph/next-js-react-gbg, which does exactly the above, at this route https://github.com/icyJoseph/next-js-react-gbg/blob/main/demo-app/src/pages/pokemon/%5Bid%5D.tsx.

In your particular case, I'd return the top paths, by some metric (for example: popularity, or amount of revenue per page), and let the fallback true option handle the rest.

When you use `fallback: true` Client Side SPA like transitions, will need to check `useRouter().isFallback`, and if you use `fallback: "blocking"` then the server waits until the page is ISR generated. Crawlers and bots, will never see fallback UI.

Also, `revalidate` helps you to keep these pages updated as people navigate to them.

---

`getServerSideProps` could also do this if you combine it with a cache layer, or a CDN kind of solution. Please refer to https://nextjs.org/docs/going-to-production#caching, and do read the entire thing. 

In short you have to set `cache-control` on the response from `getServerSideProps`, and then your caching layer should make sure to replicate this to all users (otherwise it'd scoped only to a user).

> Note: Your deployment provider must support caching for dynamic responses. If you are self-hosting, you will need to add this logic yourself using a key/value store like Redis. If you are using Vercel, [Edge Caching works without configuration](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching).

Hi,

I think this might be a networking issue rather than a Next.js problem, seeing that you have `::1:3001`, which is, AFAIK, an IPv6.

- Are you perhaps using Firefox? I do recall having problems with an auth service that required the IP of the client, but couldn't account for "IPv4 mapped IPv6" IPs,  which was only sent by FF. Chrome, Safari, etc., did send the IPv4 IP.
  - I just saw you use Chrome, but it'd still check that your local network
- Maybe try replacing `localhost` for `127.0.0.1` in your proxy.

body:
Hi,

Why are you doing:

```jsx
<Link href={{ href: route, query }} locale={otherLocale}>
```

`href` is not part of the `href` prop, `pathname` is:

```jsx
  <Link
    href={{
      pathname: route,
      query,
    }}
    locale={otherLocale}
  >
```

I'd also recommend using `pathname`, or `asPath` from `useRouter`:


```jsx
 <Link href={asPath} locale={otherLocale}>
```

or

```jsx
  <Link
    href={{
      pathname,
      query,
    }}
    locale={otherLocale}
  >
```

Either way, using `href={{pathname: '/something', query}}` will fix the error you are seeing. See more in the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link#with-url-object).

body:
Yes, but probably more than that, considering you have other plugins which don't get invoked inside `webpack()`.
Although I've looked at them and both `@zeit/next-css`  `@zeit/next-source-maps` say they are deprecated, so you probably can drop them from dependencies. 
`next-compose-plugins` looks like a needless wrapper for your usecase (unless it's an example, you don't have enough plugins to warrant it).
`@next/bundle-analyzer` says to import it conditionally which probably matters a lot for CJS:
>Note: if installing as a devDependency make sure to wrap the require in a `process.env` check as next.config.js is loaded during next start as well.

So if you do a deps maintenance work you can drop 4 out of 5 imports in this config and probably move lodash import behind a dev condition too.

body:
Here's how you could share data between a `_middleware` and a `api` route handler or a `gssp` function: 
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/37180#discussioncomment-2833562

You can:
- Use a custom header and set it's value in the `_middleware`
- Access it as a part of outgoing headers via `res` in you `api` route handler or `gssp` function
- Remove the custom header before sending the response

as @thibautsabot & @icyJoseph suggested

Well, if it's not present in the context then yeah, but if you enforce a factory pattern, and execute the factory with the filename as argument, then you'd gain a function which has closure over the filename, which certainly reduces the room for mistakes.

author: bordeo

body:
Next.js API routes run in Node.js, which doesn't support FormData, so you need a way to create those.

Setting the content type won't cut it because multipart-form streams are a special kind of format. See in the browser that the outgoing request has content type `multipart/form-data` followed by a seemingly rubbish string, called boundary. 

This sort of thing is best to be handled by a package, for example https://www.npmjs.com/package/form-data, because of all edge cases that need to be handled.

I also think you don't need to specify the content type, `axios`, should be able to figure out the data type, and create the boundary string.

body:
> To use with a server action I had to create a new FormData object from the library form-data and recreate the file object from the buffer, might be late for you @ocodista but hope helps someone else:
> 
> ```
> "user server"
> import {z} from "zod"
> 
> import FormData from "form-data";
> import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
> 
> 
> const API_URI = "http://localhost:8000/api"
> 
> const fileSchema = z.instanceof(File, {message: "Required"})
> const imageSchema = fileSchema.refine(file => file.size === 0 || file.type.startsWith("image/"))
> 
> const addProduct = z.object({
>   name: z.string().min(1),
>   description: z.string().min(1),
>   priceInCents: z.coerce.number().int().min(1),
>   file: fileSchema.refine(file => file.size > 0, "File required"),
>   image: imageSchema.refine(file => file.size > 0, "Image requried")
> })
> 
> export async function create_product(
>   previousState: unknown,
>   formData: FormData
> ) {
> 
>   const validation = addProduct.safeParse(Object.fromEntries(formData))
> 
>   if(validation.success == false) return validation.error.formErrors.fieldErrors;
> 
>   const {data} = validation;
>   
>   // create a new FormData object
>   const formObj = new FormData()
>   formObj.append("name",data.name)
>   formObj.append("description",data.description)
>   formObj.append("priceInCents",data.priceInCents)
>   if(data.image) {
>     const imageBuffer = Buffer.from(await data.image.arrayBuffer())
>     formObj.append("image", imageBuffer, {
>       filename: data.image.name,
>       contentType: data.image.type
>     })
>   }
> 
>   if(data.file){
>     const fileBuffer = Buffer.from(await data.file.arrayBuffer())
>     formObj.append("file",fileBuffer, {
>       filename: data.file.name,
>       contentType: data.file.type
>     })
>   }
> 
>   const headers = formObj.getHeaders()
> 
>   const resp = await fetch(`${API_URI}/products`, {
>     method: "POST",
>     headers: {
>       ...headers
>     },
>     body: formObj.getBuffer()
>   })
> 
>   
>   console.log(resp.ok)
>   console.log(await resp.json())
>   
>   redirect("/admin/products")
> }
> ```

You saved me hours of struggle getting this work thank you so much for you answer 🙏🏼

body:
Hello!

You don't need Vercel to use multi-zones, but it might require some manual configurations.
How are you deploying your application today?

In theory, you just need to "duplicate" your deploy process for as many zones as you have.
The main difference is that you'll need to leverage [rewrites](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/rewrites) on your main app and [basePath](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/basepath) for the others
author: thibautsabot

body:
Right now I have the following:

```
- Application
  - Page1
  - Page2
  - Page3
```

I want to decouple these pages into standalone repos, like this:
```
- Application1
  - Page1
```

```
- Application2
  - Page2
```

```
- Application3
  - Page3
```

I want the ability to SPA among these pages. I am using AWS and serving from CloudFront. Is this helpful?
author: mkotsollaris

body:
You can't keep the "SPA behavior" among zones.

Even when rewriting on the same domain, each zones is a separate Next.js app with its own server and files.
The URLs will look the same, but you can't have client-side navigation when jumping from one zone to another.
author: thibautsabot

body: I see! Is there any sorts of wizardry you can think of to deploy pages independently (different repos) while still maintaining a SPA transition?
author: mkotsollaris

body:
Not really, no.
The closest thing I can think of is Module Federation with Webpack 5: https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/ but this is not what is being used by Next.js and I don't know the full extents of its capabilities.
author: thibautsabot

body:
@mkotsollaris Module Federation is probably the most viable solution to your problem, but currently there is no free way to do this.

I got to read a lot about MF in the nextjs and the implementation seems to be something well complex and is still in beta, especially in SSR environments.


Also:
Maybe this can help you: https://github.com/telenko/node-mf
discussions on Versel about MF: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33327

https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/tree/master/nextjs-react (Free)
 | But @module-federation/nextjs-mf 2.3.1 won't support the newest version of NextJS. For Next 11/12 only 3.x.x but is paid.


(Module Federation Demo) https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/tree/master/nextjs-ssr

https://app.privjs.com/buy/packageDetail?pkg=@module-federation/nextjs-ssr (Paid $100/M)

I believe that for now there is no way to make it work for free and ready for production. And does not appear to have plans for this feature to be implemented.

author: hhhminme

body:
I don't think this has to do with NextJs. 

When running the `getServerSideProps` the communication would be container to container. So for these requests, routes to `localhost` would not work. Instead it would need to be something like `backend:8080` instead (when communicating from the `frontend` service to the `backend` service). The `localhost` domain would work as expected on the client side requests.

When you run this without docker everything works because all services are on localhost. When run via docker-compose they no longer are.

body:
I guess the page using `react-table` is being pre-rendered? 

Locally change your node version to 14, I can recommend [fnm](https://github.com/Schniz/fnm), and then:

- Use `dynamic` on the component using react-table with `{ssr: false}`, see [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import#with-no-ssr).
  - This would delay the execution of this code to the client, where performance is available.
- Use a client side set flag:
```ts
const [show, setShow] = useState(false);

useEffect(()=>{setShow(true)},[]);

return show && <ComponentThatUsesTable />
```

With these, or any other creative way you can think of, you could make it work at least. It does seem to be like `react-table` needs to provide `ssr` support. Unguarded access into browser/DOM API's causes this kind of issues.

---

You could make a PR to `react-table` and change to something like:

```js
if (opts.key && opts.debug) depTime = isServer() ?  0 : performance.now()
```

or

```js
if (!isServer() && opts.key && opts.debug) depTime = performance.now()
```

It depends on what's `depTime` used for of course. `const isServer = typeof window === 'undefined'` should do the trick.

According to the docs here: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving:

```js
import Image from 'next/image'

function Avatar() {
  return <Image src="/me.png" alt="me" width="64" height="64" />
}

export default Avatar
```

Notice that it is `/me.png` and not `/public/me.png`.

Although, I wonder if here you should do something like `${process.cwd()}/public/example.png`. Not sure that's gonna cut it, but I'd start from there.

body:
Alright, I think I have understood the problem.

`Email.js` is actually checking for a file, and from within serverless functions that's not necessarily possible. It is, but not always. It depends on the serverless runtime.

From the `vercel` [docs I see that you need to specify which static assets should be made available to the runtime](https://vercel.com/support/articles/how-can-i-use-files-in-serverless-functions).

Furthermore, this is a [discussion here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/32236) which I think you should go through.

It turns out this might not be so trivial. @thibautsabot perhaps you have more info on this than I do?

---

One thing I'd do, is host the images elsewhere, and fetch them into a stream, because `Email.js` supports streams as attachments.

body: Hi, `router.isFallback` is `true` when showing the fallback page (loading page) while the page is generated in the background, this is set to `false` once the data is loaded and props have been updated.
author: ijjk

body:
Let's simplify a bit:

- When a visible `next/link` points to a page using `fallback: true` with `getStaticPaths`, that is, ISR, the page is pre-rendered by the runtime Next.js server, so that when the user clicks on it the page already exists.
- However, if you navigate to a non previously generated page, and fallback is true, not "blocking", then Next.js responds and gives you the ability to show a spinner, check [here](https://github.com/icyJoseph/next-js-react-gbg/blob/main/demo-app/src/pages/pokemon/%5Bid%5D.tsx).

It is possible of course for the first case, to not be done yet, and upon navigation a spinner is still shown, but less likely.

Bots, will not see the loading state, for SEO reasons, and because bots don't care about loading states I guess.

body:
Hi,

Yeah, I tried a few things: 

- Your repo I couldn't get started so I couldn't do much more there, but I wanted to try this answer https://stackoverflow.com/questions/67570519/redirect-destination-nextjs-and-react-native-expo-web
- I followed this guide https://docs.expo.dev/guides/using-nextjs/, and there I saw that it used `next 9.1.3` which didn't support redirects, or rewrites, so I upgraded to 11.1.2. However, with this projects, the rewrites don't even work at all.

I think, you can try a couple of things:

- Remove plugins, one by one, to try and see if one of them has a bug.
- Try to get the rewrites working using the https://docs.expo.dev/guides/using-nextjs/ this guide, and take your learnings to the expo app.
- Ask on the expo adapter Github page, https://github.com/expo/expo-cli/tree/main/packages/next-adapter, I have a feeling, they haven't seen this before, and it is a bit scary that the adapter is labeled as experimental.

body:
I had the same issue and I was able to resolve it. The server I was proxying to enforced a trailing slash so if /A was going to /B it would redirect to /B/

I fixed it by doing updating it from this:

```
{
          source: '/main/:path*',
          destination: 'https://external-service.com/main/:path*'
}
```

to this:

```
{
          source: '/main/:path*',
          destination: 'https://external-service.com/main/:path*/'
}
```

body: Thank you! I was encountering the same problem and after adding a trailing `/` at the end of both the source and the destination, the rewrite worked again. Indeed, this also catches the case when the source does not have a trailing slash because Next.js auto redirects (thanks to `trailingSlash: true`), and then the path matches.
author: v36u

body: I noticed that if you have a backend that uses nextjs sometimes if you use middleware like clerk, they will respond with 307/308 and it will mess things up, so the best thing is to limit the paths clerk will respond with 307/308
author: pencilcheck

body: Thank you very much for the investigation. Just wanna add that `NextResponse.rewrite` used in Middleware has the same behavior as `rewrites` in Nextjs config. In my case, I'm using Middleware to rewrite requests to a separate FastAPI backend (essentially proxying them, ref [Nextjs BFF guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/backend-for-frontend#middleware)). Some requests are perfectly rewritten carrying the cookies, while some fail with the following series of requests: - The first `/merchant/generate/` request with 308 status was not received by the backend and was redirected to `/merchant/generate`, so it was indeed an Nextjs behavior as you said. - The second `/merchant/generate` request was rewritten by Nextjs Middleware and received by the backend, redirected with 307 status due to the backend's behavior because it requires the trailing slash. This way the redirected `Location` in the response header was `/merchant/generate/`. - The third `/merchant/generated/` request was to the backend domain, thus not carrying the cookies, and got the 401 error.
author: GenhaoLi

body:
Hello!

I think what you are looking for is `layout="fill"` https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#layout.

> When fill, the image will stretch both width and height to the dimensions of the parent element, provided the parent element is relative.
This is usually paired with the [objectFit](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#objectfit) property.

author: DonikaV

body:
FYI I also came across this error, straight after I added Sentry to my Next.js v12 project.
I didn't had a custom 404 page yet, but Sentry created a custom `_error.js`, which triggered the warning about a missing 404 page.
I quickly added one and got that error.
```
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'getInitialProps') at Object.<anonymous>
```

After digging around a bit, I realized that I didn't had a `default export` in my `404.js` 🤦🏼 .
After changing the `export function Custom404()` to a `default export`, the error went away.

**tl;dr:** Verify, that your `404.js` (and of course all other pages) export the component as a `default export` 💡

App is undefined and as such is unsafe to call `getInitialProps` on, maybe the check should assert that `App` is defined before calling `getInitialProps` on it?

Looking at it - this may be related to the async to generator transformations somehow...

body:
I was facing the same issue, tested the different fix approaches mentioned in this thread but none of them worked... So I started debugging branch commits one by one and found out the root cause of this error triggering in Cypress CI process was related to a code change I made to a index.ts file in the project. 

Basically, this index.ts file does the following inside the folder it is inserted:

export * from './component-1'
export * from './component-2'
export * from './component-3'

My error was updating the order of this exports, after undoing the order changes I made, the error was gone. 
Hope it helps someone going through de same.

body:
Leaving this comment for all those still experiencing the issue.

Same as stated in [this comment](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/36221#issuecomment-1319229791), I started to experience the issue right after adding Sentry to my Next v12 project. 
The issue started to appear with `@sentry/nextjs: 7.17.1`.

[Changelog report](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry-javascript/blob/develop/CHANGELOG.md#7171) for `v7.17.1` lead me to [this PR](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry-javascript/pull/6013/) and specifically this line:
```ts
test: new RegExp(`^${escapeStringForRegex(pagesDirectory)}.*\\.(${pageExtensionRegex})$`),
```

This change was significant in my case since my project contained both `/pages` and `/pages-components` folders. Previously, given regex was matching only `/pages` folder but the new change included `/pages-components` as well.

Not completely familiar with how sentry boostraps in Next but renaming my `/pages-components` folder into something else (that doesn't start with `pages`) resolved the issue in my case.

ps. after this renaming, my project is ok even with latest `@sentry/nextjs` version `7.57.0`.

body:
From your code, `getUser` is only called when there is a `token` in the localstorage.

If you don't have a token (the default behavior), `user` will be null.

I noticed that you added a `useEffect` to redirect the user to the login page in this case, but useEffect are only ran client-side after the component has been rendered!

If you want to keep this logic, you could use optional chaining like `{user?.name}` or add an early return in case the user doesn't exist to avoid rendering the Dashboard.

body:
Hello!

JS chunks are automatically created by Webpack with an optimized strategy to provide the best performance overall.
Multiples chunks can be used for the same page, but they'll be downloaded without any required actions from your end.

If you want to override it, you could use a custom webpack config: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/custom-webpack-config but I'd advised against it.

The default configuration from Next.js has been fine tuned and tested on a large number of small and huge websites.

body:
Catch all routes are very aggressive, and a top level catch all route will indeed, catch absolutely everything. 

In your case a request for a service worker is arriving at Next.js. 

Say for example that there's no `public/favicon.ico` file available in your `public` folder.

The catch all route, will then kick in and `uid` will be `favicon.ico`.

Again, in your case, the browser seems to have saved a service worker and it is making a request to get it.

You ought to be extremely careful with catch all routes at the root of the application, as they'll swallow most things.

You can use the `resolvedUrl` in the `getServerSideProps` handler `context`, to decide whether or not you are faced with a valid request. Alternatively, `_middleware` at the root, could also handle this type of requests.

author: justinh00k

body:
I agree that this is super frustrating and that there **should** be a flag that indicates the request will be handled by public.  However...

That is actually entirely impossible given the current request handling architecture in Next. If we use the [Middleware documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/routing/middleware#matching-paths) as an example, middleware is executed early to allow redirect / rewrites (step 3). Next does not check the file system until step 5 where it attempts to handle static assets.

So if anything, supporting a "public" flag would need to be in the opposite direction, something like: "prefix this route, unless it can be handled by `/public`" ... But that doesn't really make sense and would likely introduce a lot of complexity.

#### Alternate Solution
Instead what I did was embrace the locale. Here is the structure of the solution:
* No files of any kind in the root of `/public`
* Create a single folder for your default locale (e.g. `en_US`) and place all of the standard static files there (favicon.ico, robots.txt, ...)
* Write a pre-build script which creates a copy of the default locale assets for every locale that you support (you can write it such that it only does this in CI equivalent environments)

Now, you can use a simple matcher like: `'/((?!api|_next|_vercel).*)'` 
And not have to worry about introducing unexpected bugs because one of your paths happens to contain a `.`.

Further, if you did have static assets that you store under `/public` you would continue to operate those in the same way. Meaning, they would exist outside of the locale folder under `/public` and you would update your matcher to be something like: `'/((?!api|_next|_vercel|static).*)'`.

Last thought, while expanding the matcher to include more files does approach a "cleaner" solution, there is a performance tradeoff as the complexity of the RegExp grows. While maybe less "clean", I think this approach has the correct balance of organization and performance. Plus assets like `site.webmanifest` should arguably be contained in a locale specific folder.

Hope this helps.

author: beforedisappear

body:
Just chiming in as I'm running into this as well in 2024. For the time being, adding subdirectories to `/public` seems to be the cleanest way to handle large batches of files. ie: `public/images/...`.

```javascript
export const config = {
  matcher: [
    /*
     * Match all request paths except for the ones starting with:
     * - api (API routes)
     * - _next/static (static files)
     * - _next/image (image optimization files)
     * - images directory in /public (public static images)
     */
    '/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|images).*)',
  ],
};
```

- root level files can still be targeted (or not) without having to target every individual asset.
- More elegant than using `.*\\..*` which is way too catch-all (and has its own issues as described above).
- Bonus: encourages organization? 🤭

Warning: Expected server HTML to contain a matching
in

. See more info here: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/react-hydration-error Component Stack Call Stack performConcurrentWorkOnRoot ``` Has anyone else had this issue?
author: jonxuxu

body:
It can be caused one of these two:
- Your app is mismatching rendered html during hydration, in that case the console would be full of messages saying hydration mismatched and where it mismatched
- You have strict mode enabled and are using React 18 which changed strict mode semantics to catch concurrent rendering issues. You can read more about that here: https://reactjs.org/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18.html#gradually-adopting-concurrent-features. That would indicate the component you're using is not compatible with strict mode and you'll want to file an issue on the respective repository.

author: Zonalds

body:
Hi,

For completeness sake, I went to the carbon Github page, and found the simple bar component.

We can now make absolute sense of the issue.

When they mount the chart, they assign an `SBC`(internal stuff) object to `this.charts`, in `componentDidMount`, and output a `div` to the `DOM`.

https://github.com/carbon-design-system/carbon-charts/blob/master/packages/react/src/bar-chart-simple.tsx#L13

The React 18 Strict Mode, **in development mode**, simulates an immediate `unmount` and  re-`mount`, immediately.

>  With Strict Mode starting in React 18, whenever a component mounts in development, React will simulate immediately unmounting and remounting the component

But they never clean up the `SBC` object, which I believe keeps the chart up in the DOM. They sort of expected that to happen when the entire component un-mounts, but the Strict Mode behaviour forces the library to again output a `div` to the `DOM`. This is why we see duplicates.

The library has to implement `componentWillUnMount` where they should take care of cleaning up the `DOM`.

body:
@kyun Which version of React are you using? Perhaps it's all about the features of react@18 [https://reactjs.org/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18.html#new-strict-mode-behaviors](https://reactjs.org/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18.html#new-strict-mode-behaviors)


You can turn off strict mode as mentioned [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/react-strict-mode). (But doing this as a permanent solution is not recommended)
author: vadymshymko

body:
This is a combination of two things.

- New Strict Mode behaviour, which instead of rendering twice as in React 17, now does two mount-unmount cycles, in development mode
- Next.js' query object behaviour in statically generate pages. Read this carefully please. `getStaticProps` happens at build time, when no request exists. Nothing has arrived to the server, because there's no server. How should Next.js pre-render in such a case? Well, it pre-renders the page with `query` (from `useRouter()`) set to `{}`, and then, once the page is visited by a client, Next.js triggers an immediate re-render with the query that the browser URL shows.

The docs say it better than I can, https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/dynamic-routes#caveats:

> Pages that are statically optimized by [Automatic Static Optimization](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization) will be hydrated without their route parameters provided, i.e query will be an empty object ({}).

And for React 18 Strict Mode, https://reactjs.org/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18.html#new-strict-mode-behaviors:

> With Strict Mode starting in React 18, whenever a component mounts in development, React will simulate immediately unmounting and remounting the component

Also, `console.log` is a bit of a wild way to debug, in this case anyway, I'd recommend using `console.count`. It is cognitively easier to follow what's going on.

To sum up:

1. Things render and mount as expected
2. React's 18 Strict Mode, unmounts and repeats step 1
3. During 1 and 2, the query is empty as per static generation, so now Next.js triggers a render with the updated query object.

> Unfortunately we don't intend to be completely consistent with the behavior of the babel plugin, because there are some behaviors and logic that are difficult to implement in Rust or that we think are very performance-compromising

That makes sense, thanks.

Is there any chance of a transform that essentially does this (when `autoLabel` is enabled)?

Input:

```ts
const paragraph = css`
  color: orangered;
`;
```

Output:

```ts
const paragraph = css`
  label: paragraph;
  color: orangered;
`;
```

The details of Babel/SWC transforms are unfortunately out of my depth, but (aside from the filename bug) this would essentially cover my use case.

AFAIK Babel recursively includes the name of every variable interpolated in the `css` string, which is IMO overkill and can make the label very difficult to read. I can’t speak for all users but I think most would be okay with just a label derived from the variable name. Without that it’s much more difficult to look at rendered HTML and figure out where its style came from.

body:
I think, given that React 18 is a major release, and that the Suspense behaviour has changed, it is not particularly good at this time to loose sleep over it. The CSR behaviour and lazy loading components are on place, but the rest should come later on, and it will require the ecosystem of libraries to catch up.

Here's some links that made me arrive to the above:

- https://github.com/vercel/swr/issues/1898#issuecomment-1094426113
- https://github.com/reactjs/rfcs/blob/main/text/0213-suspense-in-react-18.md#new-feature-server-side-rendering-support-with-streaming
- https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/37

Basically, data fetching libraries in SSR, at the moment, data fetching libraries, are actually resolving the request. The streaming down partial HTML, is not ready just yet.

body:
API routes are not pages, so the contents of `_app`, and `_document` are not shared, let alone React itself. 

This is good because if I wanted to have other types of content server from `api/routes` for example, robots.txt, then I'd serve true text, rather than text wrapped in the `_document` and `_app` rendered html. 

There's two things you could try here:

- use a `page` with `getServerSideProps`, decode the address query and pass it as props (HTML as a string) to the page, which in turn dangerously set it as inner html for a node (usually a div)
- serve or attach link/script tags on the served HTML pointing to highlight.js [hosted versions](https://highlightjs.org/download/), and don't forget to include a script to do `hljs.highlightAll()`

Hi. 
> Is there a way to get good performance score with SSG + CSS-in-JS ?

In short - yes!) But, it seems to me, this is too general a question, since there can be many reasons for the large value of CLS)

> If so, how to render the styles server side ?

Please, see [styled components docs about integration with nextjs](https://styled-components.com/docs/advanced#nextjs). Hope this help!)
author: vadymshymko

body:
Hi,

Styled Components gains an additional benefit when used in SSR, which is a hybrid mode, where the styles are calculated on the server, and therefore exist in the initial HTML send to the client, but can also be dynamic once in the client.

You must implement something called `ServerStyleSheet` in the `_document` file, see [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-styled-components-babel/pages/_document.js).

I assume the CLS comes from a site that doesn't do the above, but generates the styles on the browser, which means there's a flash of un-styled content, and then suddenly everything gets shuffled around with the client side calculated styles.

author: thibautsabot

body:
Well, `next lint` does use `eslint`, but the thing is that, IIRC, `eslint` looks for a configuration file at the root of your project, or you can tell it where to look for it as a CLI option, whereas `next lint`'s ESLINT configuration it's just not visible to you. 

You can of course extend/override `next lint`, with your own stuff, but I'd say, unless your team fully agrees on various conventions, it's best to just take `next lint`'s rules.
author: icyJoseph

body:
So if I want to use a custom configuration of `eslint`, is it correct to use it directly instead of use `next lint`?

The next lint for example is not catching some unicorn plugin errors
author: RigottiG

body:
Well, technically yes, but you'd miss out on the `next lint` rules as well. Instead you could extend/override the `next lint` configuration.

See here, https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/eslint#additional-configurations.

@icyJoseph I still don't understand how all this eslint config works for Nextjs. My setup runs Nextjs in a docker container. And they produce different results when eslint and next lint use the same config file. And I wonder why that would happen. 

So in the case of "next lint" the full command that dockerised env runs is "next lint -c /app/.eslintrc.json" and in the case of eslint its "eslint -c /app/.eslintrc.json". as far as I can tell they both use the same file, since when I add "s" to the end of the file, they both complain the file is missing.

But results are different:
```
$ next lint -c /app/.eslintrc.json /app

./pages/index.jsx
7:28  Error: Unable to resolve path to module '../components/molecules/WalletOverview'.  import/no-unresolved
7:28  Error: Missing file extension for "../components/molecules/WalletOverview"  import/extensions
18:9  Warning: Unexpected console statement.  no-console

and eslint:

```

author: OdifYltsaeb

body:
if the eslint config file contents is only:

```
{
    "extends": [
        "eslint-config-airbnb",
        "eslint-config-next",
        "next/core-web-vitals",
        "prettier",
        "next"
    ]
}

```

The results are wildly different. Running next lint does not pick up some files, and running eslint does not pick up some errors (and different files). The example here https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-eslint/.eslintrc is of no help either. 

Googling around, it seems like changing package.json contents to `"lint": "next lint --dir .",` is of some help. But not entirely, as there is some config, somewhere, that is not quite the same as running it with eslint.

body: `next lint` does more than just run eslint; it also ensures your project is configured as expected. See my full writeup here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/59347#discussion-5933112
author: shirakaba

body:
One issue I've run into unfortunately is that `next lint` doesn't take "rest" args as a list of files to check, but requires each to be prefixed with `--file `, so a tool like _lefthook_ requires me to process something like `{staged_files}`, to add the `--file ` arg name.

While this might seem like not a big deal, the reason for choosing _lefthook_ in the first place is partially because it's more performant. My options are to have _lefthook_ call a js script that manipulates the list of files, or to use some bash-fu to insert the arg names. It would be nice if `next lint` just worked like _prettier_ and _eslinr_ cli do, and I didn't have to do this...

author: icyJoseph

body:
yaa , like `window.reload` ,

i have done command "`next build`" then "`node server`" , and this reloading problem doesn't appear anymore.. so what is the difference between " `next build` -> `node server` " and "`next dev -p 3000`" ?
author: ahmad-azzam

body: Are you using a custom server? `next build` is typically followed by `next start`, and `next dev` behaves as much as possible like them, but if there's a custom server then it becomes harder to tell what the problem might be. Not sure why you do `node server`. The only thing I could see happening is that there's not reloading of the page, but some HMR (hot module reloading), not well handled is causing the SPA to remount. If you open your devTools, and in the console options set `preserve logs`, you should see a message saying `Navigated to `: If that's the case, then theres' an actual `window.location.reload` call, AKA true page reload, otherwise, it is a HMR problem.
author: icyJoseph

body:
> My next version is 9.5.5

Next.js 12 and later does have this edge case where it reloads the page at random, so I'd recommend upgrading to the latest version of Next.js.

body:
Looks like `next@10.2.3` does not work with composite projects.
Removing `composite` and `"references": [{ "path": "../../libs/xxx" }]` from `apps/my-nextjs-app/tsconfig.json` solved the issue.
This is not a good solution because now TS is unaware of dependencies for its `tsc --build` command.

body:
Hey folks, wanted to swing back here with an update. After digging through many different issues and discussions, I've [**made a new page in the documentation**](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy) ([PR](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/54601)) specifically for Content Security Policy and nonces. This docs page: 

- Explains how to generate a `nonce` with Middleware
- Shows how to consume the `nonce` in a route with `headers()`
- Shows a complete CSP without needing to use any `unsafe`
- Shows how to ignore the `nonce` Middleware from running on prefetches / static assets

Further, we've patched some bugs and made improvements to `nonce` handling in Next.js that will be available in the latest `canary` version (for those of you time traveling from the future, upgrade to Next.js 13.5). We also updated the `with-strict-csp` example in the `examples/` folder, which is backlinked from the [new documentation page](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy).

Really hope this helps out, thank you all 🙏 I'll be closing this discussion out. To continue the discussion, [please go here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/54907).

body:
Hi, 

I think you are missing the `Kepler.GL` middleware, `import {enhanceReduxMiddleware} from 'kepler.gl/middleware';`, but funnily enough, when I include it I get other errors. I found it in the [docs](https://github.com/keplergl/kepler.gl#1-mount-reducer) by the way.

In your case do:

```js
const middleware = [thunk, enhanceReduxMiddleware];
```

And you'll get a different error message.

## Edit

```js
const middleware = enhanceReduxMiddleware([logger]);
const enhancers = [applyMiddleware(...middleware)];

// using createStore
const store = createStore(reducers, initialState, compose(...enhancers));
```

But it still outputs the error... looking into it once again

## Edit 2 :tada:

I got it to work! :tada: :tada:

It is kinda of strange, but `kepler.gl` has its own react-redux version installed, and it looks like you have this options:

- Assume the implicit `react-redux` installed by them (so you don't install it yourself, which I think is not good)
- Use something like resolutions, if you use `yarn`:
```json
  "dependencies": {
    "kepler.gl": "^2.5.5",
    "next": "12.1.5",
    "react": "17.0.2",
    "react-dom": "17.0.2",
    "react-redux": "8.0.1",
    "redux": "^4.2.0",
    "styled-components": "^5.3.5"
  },
  "resolutions": {
    "react-redux": "8.0.1"
  },
```
- `npm install react-redux@7.2.8 --save-exact` should do the trick too
  - To get the version do `npm ls react-redux` and you'll see the duplicated version

At the end of the day it is a problem with `react-redux` versions as documented here: https://github.com/reduxjs/react-redux/issues/1816
